summary: Morels (Morchella) are prized spring mushrooms with a distinctive honeycombed, pitted-and-ridged cap fused to the stem. On this map they anchor the spring mushroom flush; every morel recipe here (saute, drying) cooks them first, which is non-negotiable.
identification: A true morel has a cap covered in pits and ridges (a honeycomb) and, when sliced lengthwise, is completely HOLLOW from cap tip to stem base, with the cap attached directly to the stem. The dangerous confusion is with false morels: Gyromitra species have a wrinkled, lobed, brain- or saddle-shaped cap that is NOT cleanly hollow but is chambered or stuffed with cottony/wispy flesh, and Gyromitra esculenta contains gyromitrin (metabolized to monomethylhydrazine), which can cause severe poisoning, organ damage, or death - so no Gyromitra should ever be eaten. Verpa species also mislead beginners: their thimble-like cap hangs free, attached only at the very top, and the stem is stuffed with cottony fibers rather than being cleanly hollow; Verpa can cause GI upset and coordination problems in many people and is best avoided. Note the hollow test alone is not sufficient - 'half-free' morels are also hollow, so use the full profile (pitted honeycomb cap, cap-to-stem attachment, clean hollow interior) and confirm every specimen with an expert, matching the map's 'lookalikes' safety tag.
habitat: Widespread across the contiguous US, most reliably in the East, Midwest, and mountain West. Look in spring on forest floors, often near dying or dead elms, ash, apple, and cottonwoods, in old orchards, and on recently burned ground. Fruiting is temperature-driven and roughly spans March through May after warm rains, earlier in the South and later at higher elevations and latitudes.
craftUse: Culinary use only, and always cook thoroughly: even true morels are toxic raw or undercooked and can cause GI illness, so they are never eaten raw. Saute in butter (as in the map's Morel Cream Saute) or dry for storage and rehydrate later (Dried Morels) - drying concentrates flavor but the cook-before-eating rule still applies. There are anecdotal reports of stomach upset when some people pair morels with alcohol, so try a small amount the first time. Their hollow structure traps grit and insects, so slice and rinse before cooking.
safetyNote: Never eat a morel raw. Cook thoroughly, and rule out false morels: Gyromitra (chambered, brain-like, can be fatal) and Verpa (cottony-stuffed stem). Confirm identity with an expert.

sources.title: Gyromitra Mushroom Toxicity - StatPearls, NCBI Bookshelf
Morchella Look-alikes - Midwest American Mycological Information

summary: Wild blueberries (Vaccinium) are small acid-soil shrubs bearing sweet blue-black berries with a five-pointed crown at the tip. They are a mainstay summer berry on this map, feeding jam, tray-freezing, and other preserve recipes.
identification: Look for a shrub (low, spreading lowbush or taller highbush) with small berries carrying a distinctive five-lobed calyx 'crown' at the blossom end. Split a berry: blueberries are pale green or white inside with many tiny soft seeds. This separates them from eastern huckleberries (Gaylussacia), which are darker inside with about 10 large gritty seeds - a distinction that is culinary, not safety-critical, since both are edible. More importantly, only eat berries you can positively tie to a blueberry shrub with a crowned tip: several unrelated plants with dark round berries are toxic - buckthorn (mildly poisonous, causes GI upset), nightshades (tropane alkaloids, can be deadly), pokeweed, and Virginia creeper (oxalate-bearing) among them - and these lack the true five-lobed crown. When in doubt, leave it out.
habitat: Native and widespread across the contiguous US, concentrated in the Northeast, Upper Midwest, Appalachians, and mountain West. They favor acidic, well-drained soils (pH roughly 4.5-5.5) in open woods, clearings, old fields, sunny forest edges, and bog margins. Fruit ripens mid to late summer, about June through August across much of this range.
craftUse: One of the easiest wild fruits for preserving. Use ripe berries fresh or freeze them tray/IQF-style to keep them loose (the map's Tray-frozen wild berries recipe), or cook into jam - the map pairs them with huckleberries in a water-bath-canned jam. Skins and natural pectin make them cooperative for jam and fruit spreads; sort out stems and any green or shriveled fruit first.
safetyNote: Positively identify the crowned blueberry shrub - do not gather look-alike dark berries (buckthorn, nightshade, pokeweed, Virginia creeper) from unfamiliar plants.

sources.title: Vaccinium angustifolium (Lowbush Blueberry) - NC State Extension
The Difference Between Blueberries and Huckleberries - Intl. Wild Huckleberry Assoc.

summary: Wild blackberries (Rubus allegheniensis and relatives) are thorny cane fruits whose black, clustered aggregate berries are among the most abundant and beginner-safe wild foods. They drive several berry recipes on this map, from shrubs and sodas to fruit leathers.
identification: A bramble with arching, thorny (prickly) canes and compound, toothed leaves; the fruit is an aggregate of many small drupelets. The key trait separating it from raspberries: when picked, a blackberry keeps its white central core (receptacle) inside the fruit, so the picked berry is solid, not hollow. All true Rubus are edible with no toxic look-alikes, so confusion among brambles is culinary, not dangerous. The one thing to note: poison ivy and poison oak (Toxicodendron) can grow tangled among brambles, but they never have thorns and their leaflets are not toothed/serrated - so avoid contact with any smooth-edged, thornless three-leaflet plants nearby. The map's 'thorns' tag flags the real hazard of the brambles themselves - the prickles, not the fruit.
habitat: Native and common across nearly the entire contiguous US. Thrives in full sun on disturbed ground: field edges, roadsides, clearings, fencerows, old pastures, and recovering cutover land. Berries ripen through summer, roughly June through August in much of this range, reddening before turning fully dull black and pulling free easily.
craftUse: Extremely versatile and forgiving for a beginner. Use ripe berries for shrubs (Wild Berry Drinking Shrub), wild-fermented sodas, and fruit leathers on this map, and for jam, jelly, and syrup generally. Blackberries are seedy; straining the pulp (as in the Jelly-Bag Berry-Pulp Fruit Leather) yields smoother spreads. Pick only fully black, sweet berries - red ones are sour and underripe.
safetyNote: No toxic look-alikes among the fruit, but the canes are thorny; wear long sleeves and gloves, and avoid thornless, smooth-edged three-leaflet plants (poison ivy/oak) growing among them.

sources.title: Brambleberries - Rubus spp., Edible Wild Plant Info
What is the Difference Between a Raspberry and a Blackberry? - Penn State Extension

summary: Wild raspberries include black raspberry (Rubus occidentalis) and red raspberry (Rubus idaeus), thorny brambles bearing soft, hollow aggregate berries. They are a safe, familiar summer berry on this map, grouped with blackberries for preserves.
identification: The defining trait: a picked raspberry is HOLLOW, like a thimble, because the white central core (receptacle) stays on the cane. This cleanly separates raspberries from blackberries, whose core comes away with the fruit. Black raspberries have strongly whitish-waxy (glaucous) canes and hairy drupelets; red raspberries are red when ripe. All true Rubus are edible with no toxic look-alikes, so confusion among wild brambles is a matter of flavor, not safety. As with all brambles, steer clear of any thornless, smooth-edged three-leaflet plants (poison ivy/oak) growing in the patch. The map's 'thorns' tag flags the prickly canes.
habitat: Native and widespread across the contiguous US, especially the Northeast, Midwest, Appalachians, and mountain West. Found on sunny disturbed ground - forest edges, old fields, clearings, roadsides, and recovering woodland. Fruit ripens in summer, about June through August across much of this range, with black raspberries typically a bit earlier than reds.
craftUse: A prized dessert and preserve berry. Ripe berries go into jams, jellies, shrubs, sodas, and fruit leathers alongside other brambles on this map. They are delicate and crush easily, so handle gently and use or freeze soon after picking. A ripe raspberry pulls off cleanly, leaving the white core behind; if it clings, it is not ready.
safetyNote: No toxic look-alikes; watch for thorns on the canes and avoid thornless poison ivy/oak growing among them.

sources.title: Brambleberries - Rubus spp., Edible Wild Plant Info
What is the Difference Between a Raspberry and a Blackberry? - Penn State Extension

summary: Wineberry (Rubus phoenicolasius) is an invasive Asian bramble now widespread in the eastern US, bearing bright, shiny red raspberry-like fruit. On this map it is flagged for invasive removal - harvesting it is encouraged as low-impact management.
identification: Unmistakable among brambles: the canes, leaf stalks, and the calyx enclosing each unripe berry are densely covered in reddish, sticky glandular hairs, giving stems a fuzzy red look. Leaves are compound with 3 leaflets, bright white-woolly underneath. Ripe fruit is a glossy, translucent bright red aggregate berry that sits in a hairy cup until ripe. Like all Rubus it is edible with no toxic look-alikes; the red-haired canes distinguish it from native raspberries and blackberries.
habitat: Not native. Introduced from East Asia and now naturalized across much of the eastern US - roughly from New England and New York south to Georgia and west to the Great Lakes and the Ozarks - and reported in 20-plus states. It colonizes forest edges, streambanks, trail sides, clearings, and disturbed woods, often forming dense thickets that crowd out native plants. Fruit ripens in summer, about June through August in this range.
craftUse: Sweet-tart and juicy, good fresh and excellent in jams, jellies, shrubs, sodas, and fruit leathers wherever raspberries are used. Because it is invasive, foraging it doubles as removal - the map tags it 'invasive removal.' To reduce spread, avoid dropping ripe fruit or moving cane fragments, and consider cutting or pulling canes where you pick.
safetyNote: Invasive - harvesting helps; avoid spreading seeds or cane fragments. Watch for thorns.

sources.title: Wineberry (Rubus phoenicolasius) - University of Maryland Extension
Wineberry (Rubus phoenicolasius) - Wisconsin DNR

summary: Wild grapes (Vitis) are woody climbing vines with forked tendrils and clusters of dark, seedy grapes. They are a key late-summer fruit on this map for verjus and jelly - but they share habitat with serious toxic look-alikes, so this profile leads with safety.
identification: True grapevines climb with FORKED TENDRILS opposite the leaves, have heart-shaped/lobed toothed leaves, shreddy peeling bark on older wood, and clusters of berries each containing 2-4 pear-shaped seeds. Two dangerous look-alikes share the same thickets. Canada moonseed (Menispermum canadense) TWINES (no tendrils), has smooth-margined, non-toothed leaves whose stalk attaches slightly inside the leaf underside, and each berry holds a SINGLE flat, crescent-shaped seed - it is poisonous and potentially fatal. Virginia creeper (Parthenocissus quinquefolia) has compound leaves of five leaflets and dark blue-black berries on red-pink stalks; its berries contain oxalate crystals and are toxic. Always crush a berry and check the seed: multiple pear-shaped seeds and forked tendrils = grape; one crescent seed, five-leaflet leaves, or twining habit = discard. (Porcelainberry, another look-alike, is also inedible.) This matches the map's 'lookalikes' tag.
habitat: Vitis species are native and widespread across the contiguous US. Vines climb into trees, fences, and thickets at woodland edges, along streams, roadsides, and clearings. Fruit ripens in late summer into fall, roughly August through October in much of this range.
craftUse: Wild grapes are tart, seedy, and thick-skinned - better cooked than eaten fresh. On this map, unripe green grapes press into verjus (a tart, non-alcoholic acidulant, as in Wild Grape Verjus), and ripe fruit cooks into jelly, often blended with crabapple for pectin (Wild Grape & Crabapple Jelly). Expect to strain out seeds and skins. Note: grape LEAVES are also edible and traditionally stuffed.
safetyNote: Verify every berry has multiple pear-shaped seeds and the vine has forked tendrils; a single crescent seed (moonseed) or five-leaflet leaves (Virginia creeper) means toxic - do not eat.

sources.title: Wild Grape - Vitis, Illinois Wildflowers
Pokeberries and Grapes Look-Alike; Common Moonseed - Poison Control

summary: American elderberry (Sambucus canadensis) is a large shrub with flat-topped white flower clusters that become drooping clusters of small dark-purple berries. It is a signature preserve plant on this map - but ONLY cooked ripe berries and flowers are used; the rest of the plant is toxic.
identification: A tall shrub with opposite, pinnately compound leaves (5-11 serrated leaflets), soft white pith inside young stems, broad flat-topped clusters of tiny 5-petaled white flowers, and drooping clusters of small round dark-purple berries. Rule out three look-alikes. Water hemlock and poison hemlock are DEADLY, but they are herbaceous with hollow, non-woody stems, umbrella-shaped (umbel) flower heads, and grow in wet ground - elderberry is woody with bark and lenticels. Pokeweed is toxic, but its berries hang in a single elongated raceme on a smooth red-purple herbaceous stalk, versus elderberry's flat woody cluster. Devil's walking stick (Aralia spinosa) closely mimics elderberry's dark berry clusters on reddish stalks, but its main stem and stalks are covered in sharp spines and its huge compound leaves are very different - never harvest berries from a thorny/spiny stalk. This matches the map's 'toxic parts' and 'lookalikes' tags.
habitat: Native and widespread across the eastern and central US and beyond. It favors moist ground: streambanks, ditches, wet meadows, roadside swales, and thicket edges in full sun. Flowers appear early-to-mid summer; berries ripen mid to late summer, roughly July through September in this range.
craftUse: Use only fully ripe berries, and always COOK them: raw and unripe berries, plus leaves, stems, roots, and seeds, contain cyanogenic glycosides that can cause nausea and vomiting, so recipes simmer the berries before use. The map cooks them into cordial and syrup; the white flowers (elderflowers) make a lighter cordial. Strip berries from the toxic green stems before cooking, and never include leaves or stems.
safetyNote: Never eat raw berries, leaves, stems, or roots; cook ripe berries fully. Rule out deadly water hemlock/poison hemlock (herbaceous, hollow-stemmed, umbel flowers), pokeweed, and spiny devil's walking stick by the woody, spineless stem and flat flower cluster.

sources.title: Play it safe when preserving elderberries - OSU Extension (EM 9446)
Cyanogenic Glycoside Analysis in American Elderberry - PMC

summary: Ribes are small shrubs producing tart gooseberries and currants; the group here spans smooth-fruited currants and spiny gooseberries. They are a tart, high-pectin preserve fruit on this map, cooked into jelly.
identification: Low to medium shrubs with lobed, maple-like alternate leaves and small, translucent, tart berries hanging singly or in short strands. Currants are usually thornless with smooth berries in clusters; gooseberries typically have spines at the nodes and larger single berries, often with a papery 'tail' (dried flower remnant) at the tip. Berries can be red, black, purple, green, or amber depending on species. All Ribes fruit is edible; the map's 'lookalikes' tag reflects distinguishing among Ribes species and separating them from other small-fruited shrubs - confirm the maple-shaped leaves and node spines/currant clusters before picking.
habitat: Native Ribes occur across much of the contiguous US, especially the mountain West, Northeast, and Upper Midwest, in cool moist woods, streambanks, rocky slopes, and forest edges. Fruit ripens in summer, roughly June through August. Note a land-management caveat: Ribes are alternate hosts for white pine blister rust, so some states and localities have historically restricted planting or growing them near white pines - check local rules.
craftUse: Tart and rich in natural pectin, which makes Ribes excellent for jelly and jam even without added pectin - the map cooks gooseberries and wild currants into jelly. Fruit is usually too sour to enjoy raw and benefits from cooking and sweetening. Gooseberries may need 'topping and tailing' (trimming stem and blossom ends); currants strip easily from their strands.
safetyNote: Fruit is edible; be aware Ribes host white pine blister rust, so gathering or growing near white pines may be locally regulated.

sources.title: White Pine Blister Rust on Currants and Gooseberries - Ohioline (OSU)
White Pine Blister Rust and Ribes Species - UMass Amherst

summary: Eastern huckleberries (Gaylussacia) are acid-soil shrubs closely resembling blueberries but with fewer, larger, crunchier seeds. On this map they share habitat and recipes with blueberries, including a combined water-bath-canned jam.
identification: A low-to-medium shrub of dry acidic ground with dark blue-to-black berries. The reliable tell versus blueberries: split the fruit - a Gaylussacia huckleberry has about 10 large, hard, crunchy seeds, while a blueberry has many tiny soft seeds. Huckleberry leaves often carry tiny golden resin dots visible with a hand lens, and berries tend to form singly at leaf axils. Both huckleberries and blueberries are edible, so that confusion is culinary. The map's 'lookalikes' tag also underscores the general rule: only eat dark berries you can tie to a huckleberry/blueberry shrub - not to unfamiliar plants like buckthorn, nightshade, pokeweed, or Virginia creeper, which are toxic. (Note: in the Pacific Northwest, 'huckleberry' usually means a Vaccinium, not Gaylussacia.)
habitat: Gaylussacia is native to eastern and southeastern North America. It favors dry, acidic, well-drained soils - sandy or rocky ridges, pine and oak woods, heaths, and clearings - often on drier sites than blueberries. Fruit ripens in summer, roughly July through September in this range.
craftUse: Used like wild blueberries but with a firmer, seedier bite. The map cooks them with blueberries into a water-bath-canned jam; they also freeze and bake well. The hard seeds are noticeable in fresh eating and can be strained out for smoother spreads. Pick only fully dark, ripe berries.
safetyNote: Edible, but positively identify the shrub - do not gather dark berries (buckthorn, nightshade, pokeweed, Virginia creeper) from unfamiliar plants.

sources.title: The Difference Between Blueberries and Huckleberries - Intl. Wild Huckleberry Assoc.
Gaylussacia baccata (Black Huckleberry) - Go Botany, Native Plant Trust

summary: Black cherry (Prunus serotina) is a common eastern tree bearing small dark cherries. The ripe flesh is edible and used in cordials, but the pits, leaves, and especially wilted foliage contain cyanide compounds - so this profile centers on using only the ripe pulp.
identification: A tree (often tall) with lance-shaped, finely toothed glossy leaves whose midrib underside bears rusty-brown hairs, and mature bark that looks like dark, scaly 'burnt potato chips.' Fruit hangs in slender drooping clusters (racemes) of small cherries ripening dark red to nearly black, each with a single stone. Related Prunus - chokecherry (a shrub, rounder leaves, astringent fruit) and pin cherry (bright red fruit) - are also used cooked; the 'toxic parts' tag applies across the group because seeds, bark, leaves, and wilted foliage release hydrogen cyanide.
habitat: Native and widespread across the eastern and central US, extending west; a common tree of woodland edges, old fields, fencerows, and second-growth forest. Fruit ripens in late summer, roughly July through September in this range.
craftUse: Only the ripe fruit FLESH is used, and it is thin, tart, and slightly bitter - better cooked and sweetened than eaten fresh. It goes into jelly, syrup, and cordials (the map's cherry-bounce-style cordial draws on wild cherries). Do NOT crush or eat the pits, and keep leaves, bark, and wilted foliage out entirely, since damaged/wilted plant tissue is the most cyanide-rich. Swallowing a stray whole intact pit is not a poisoning risk; chewing, cracking, or grinding pits is.
safetyNote: Use only ripe flesh; never crush the pits or include leaves, bark, or wilted foliage - they release cyanide.

sources.title: Prunus serotina (Black Cherry) - NC State Extension
Are cherry pits really poisonous? - Poison Control

summary: Sweet cherry (Prunus avium) is the familiar orchard cherry, often found wild as an escape near old homesteads and plantings. Ripe fruit is sweet and eaten fresh or preserved; as with all Prunus, pits and foliage are toxic.
identification: A tree with smooth, reddish-brown, horizontally banded (lenticelled) bark, oval toothed leaves, and clusters of long-stalked cherries ripening yellow to deep red or nearly black, each with a single hard stone. It is sweeter and larger-fruited than wild black cherry or sour cherry. Because it is usually an escaped cultivar, expect it near old orchards, yards, and roadsides. The map's 'toxic parts' tag reflects the standard Prunus hazard: pits/seeds, leaves, and wilted foliage contain cyanogenic compounds - only the flesh is food.
habitat: Not native (introduced from Europe/western Asia) but naturalized across parts of the contiguous US, especially near cultivation. Found along old homestead sites, fencerows, roadsides, and woodland edges where birds have spread pits. Fruit ripens early, roughly June through July in this range. Many finds are on or near private land - confirm access before harvesting.
craftUse: The easiest wild-found cherry to eat fresh when fully ripe and sweet. Also good pitted for jams, preserves, pies, and cordials. Pit the fruit before cooking or eating in quantity, and discard pits, stems, and leaves; do not crush pits into recipes.
safetyNote: Eat only ripe flesh; do not crush or eat pits, leaves, or wilted foliage (cyanide). Confirm land access first.

sources.title: Prunus avium - USDA PLANTS Database
Prunus avium (Sweet Cherry) - NC State Extension

summary: Sour (tart) cherry (Prunus cerasus) is a small orchard tree, often found wild as an escape, bearing bright red acidic cherries. Too tart for most fresh eating, it shines in cooked preserves and cordials; standard Prunus toxicity applies to pits and foliage.
identification: A small, often shrubby tree with oval, glossy, finely toothed leaves and clusters of round, bright translucent red cherries with soft juicy flesh and a single stone; the fruit is distinctly sour. It is typically an escaped cultivar found near old plantings, distinguishing it from tree-sized wild black cherry. The 'toxic parts' tag covers the usual Prunus hazard - pits, leaves, and wilted foliage contain cyanogenic compounds, so only the flesh is used.
habitat: Not native (of Eurasian origin) but naturalized in scattered spots across the contiguous US near cultivation. Found around old orchards, homesteads, hedgerows, and neighborhood edges. Fruit ripens early summer, roughly June through July in this range. Many trees are on or beside private property - check access before picking.
craftUse: Prized for cooking precisely because it is tart - it holds bright cherry flavor and acidity that sweet cherries lose. On this map it flavors a cherry-bounce-style cordial with rose hips; it is also classic for pies, jams, and syrups. Pit before cooking, and keep pits, stems, and leaves out of any preparation.
safetyNote: Use only ripe flesh; never crush pits or include leaves or wilted foliage (cyanide). Confirm land access first.

sources.title: Prunus cerasus - USDA PLANTS Database
Prunus cerasus (Sour Cherry) - Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder

summary: Wild plums (Prunus americana and Prunus mexicana) are small thicket-forming trees bearing tart, juicy plums that ripen yellow to red-purple. They are a classic preserve fruit on this map, cooked into jam; as with all Prunus, only the flesh is food.
identification: Small trees or large shrubs, often thorny and thicket-forming (American plum especially), with oval toothed leaves and clusters of white spring flowers before or with the leaves. Fruit is a round plum up to about an inch, ripening yellow, red, or purple, with a waxy bloom and a single flattened stone. Prunus americana usually has smooth twigs; P. mexicana tends toward fuzzy young growth and grows as a single-trunked tree. The map's 'toxic parts' tag reflects the Prunus rule: pits, leaves, and wilted foliage contain cyanogenic compounds - use only ripe flesh.
habitat: Native and widespread across the central and eastern US. American plum forms thickets in fencerows, woodland edges, bottomlands, and old fields; Mexican plum favors dry-to-moist thin woods, river bottoms, and prairie edges, more toward the south-central US. Fruit ripens across summer into early fall, roughly June through September in this range.
craftUse: Wild plums are often tart and skins can be astringent, so they are usually better cooked than eaten fresh. They are excellent for jam, jelly, and sauce - the map cooks wild and beach plums into jam - and their natural pectin and acidity help preserves set. Pit before cooking; discard pits, leaves, and any wilted foliage.
safetyNote: Eat only ripe flesh; do not crush pits or include leaves or wilted foliage (cyanide).

sources.title: Prunus americana (American Plum) - NC State Extension
Prunus mexicana (Mexican Plum) - Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center

summary: Cornelian cherry (Cornus mas) is not a true cherry but a dogwood, grown as an ornamental/edible shrub whose oblong red drupes make a tart preserve fruit. On this map it is a planting-associated forage used for preserves and sharbat, and it falls outside the encoded Shenandoah harvest allowance.
identification: A large shrub or small tree with opposite, smooth-margined oval leaves and clusters of small yellow flowers that open in late winter/early spring before the leaves. Fruit is an oblong red (sometimes yellow) drupe about 2 cm long with a single elongated stone - resembling a small olive or coffee berry more than a round cherry. Opposite leaves and early yellow flowers separate it from true cherries (Prunus, alternate leaves, white/pink flowers). Unripe fruit is very astringent; the fully ripe flesh is edible and has no dangerous toxic look-alikes, though the map tags it 'remove seeds' since the single hard stone is inedible.
habitat: Not native to North America (native to Europe and western Asia); in the contiguous US it occurs almost entirely as a cultivated shrub in gardens, campuses, parks, and hedges, occasionally spreading nearby. Fruit ripens mid to late summer, roughly July through September. Because it is typically planted, confirm access and local rules before harvesting - and note the map records no Shenandoah allowance for it.
craftUse: Best used fully ripe and soft, when it is sweet-tart (compared to a cranberry-sour cherry blend); underripe fruit is mouth-puckeringly astringent. Classic uses are jam, preserves, and syrups/sharbat (as in the map's Cornelian cherry preserves & sharbat), plus fruit leathers and drinks. The single large stone must be removed - cook and strain, or pit before use.
safetyNote: Remove the hard central stone; use only fully ripe fruit (unripe is very astringent). Confirm access - often a planted shrub, and no encoded Shenandoah allowance.

sources.title: Cornus mas - Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder
Cornelian Cherry (Cornus mas) - Philadelphia Orchard Project

summary: Serviceberries (Amelanchier), also called juneberries, saskatoons, or shadbush, are small trees and shrubs bearing sweet, blueberry-like purple pomes in early summer. They are an early, beginner-friendly fruit on this map with no dangerous look-alikes.
identification: A small tree or shrub with oval, finely toothed alternate leaves (young leaves silvery and downy), showy white five-petaled spring flowers, and small round fruit that ripens green to red to deep purple-blue, carrying a five-lobed crown at the tip like a tiny apple/blueberry. The soft several seeds have a mild almond flavor when chewed. When the whole plant is examined, Amelanchier has no dangerous look-alikes. The nearest source of confusion is chokecherry (Prunus virginiana), which also bears dark clustered fruit - but chokecherry fruit hangs in longer cylindrical racemes, its single hard pit is toxic (cyanogenic, as in all Prunus), and it lacks the calyx crown; that is a reason to confirm the crowned pome, white spring flowers, and shrub/tree habit rather than a warning about a deadly twin.
habitat: Native and widespread across the contiguous US, from the East and Midwest to the mountain West. Grows along stream banks, dry hillsides, open woods, thickets, forest edges, and swamp margins. Flowers very early in spring; fruit ripens in early summer - hence 'juneberry' - roughly May through July in this range.
craftUse: Sweet and mild, good fresh off the shrub and excellent in jams, pies, syrups, and dried like raisins. The faint almond note from the soft seeds adds character to preserves. Fruit is soft and ripens fast, and birds compete heavily for it, so pick promptly when berries turn deep purple-blue.
safetyNote: No dangerous look-alikes when the whole plant is confirmed (crowned pome, white five-petaled flowers); don't confuse with chokecherry, whose hard pit is toxic. The soft serviceberry seeds are fine in normal eating.

sources.title: Serviceberry in the Garden and in the Kitchen - Penn State Extension
Serviceberry, Amelanchier spp. - University of Wisconsin Horticulture

summary: Wild strawberries (Fragaria virginiana and the smaller F. vesca) are low, running herbs of the rose family that produce tiny, intensely aromatic red berries far more flavorful than their size suggests. On this map they mark the earliest fresh fruit of the season, best eaten on the spot or in small preserves. True wild strawberries are edible and safe; the whole point of learning them is separating them from a bland (but non-toxic) mimic and from look-alike cinquefoils.
identification: Look for a low plant with three toothed leaflets, five-petaled WHITE (sometimes faintly pink) flowers, and runners (stolons). Ripe fruit is small, red, seedy on the surface, and strongly fragrant. The classic look-alike is mock or 'Indian' strawberry (Potentilla/Duchesnea indica): it has YELLOW flowers, fruit held upright on the stalk rather than nodding, and dry, seedy berries that are edible but bland (mildly sweet to watery, not toxic). The other frequent confusion is with true cinquefoils (other Potentilla species), which have YELLOW flowers and usually FIVE leaflets rather than three, and never form a strawberry-like fleshy fruit. The reliable field rule: yellow flowers mean it is not a true strawberry, and true Fragaria fruit typically nods below the leaves with reddish sepals above. When a plant is not in fruit, count leaflets (three for strawberry) and note flower color.
habitat: Native across much of the contiguous US in sunny, open ground: meadows, field edges, roadsides, trail margins, open woods, and lawns. Flowers roughly April to June, with fruit ripening a few weeks after bloom, so this is a spring-to-early-summer window that varies with latitude and elevation.
craftUse: In this food-map context wild strawberries are a fresh-use fruit: eaten raw, macerated with a little sugar, or folded into a small batch of jam or shrub. The honest limit is yield. The berries are minute and pickable only a few at a time, so they suit garnishes, infusions, and tiny preserve batches rather than volume canning. Their aroma is fragile and fades with heavy cooking, so light handling preserves what makes them special.
safetyNote: Confirm WHITE flowers, three leaflets, and fragrant red fruit so you are eating a true wild strawberry. The main lookalikes (mock strawberry and cinquefoils) are not toxic, but they are not worth eating; there is no dangerous poisonous lookalike for a fruiting wild strawberry.

Potentilla indica (Mock Strawberry), Minnesota Wildflowers
Indian Strawberry (Mock Strawberry), Missouri Department of Conservation

summary: The American persimmon (Diospyros virginiana) is a native tree whose orange fruit ripens in fall to a soft, sweet, date-like pulp. On this map it is a late-season fresh and preserving fruit driving recipes like wild persimmon pudding and hoshigaki dried persimmon. The single most important thing to know is that unripe fruit is mouth-puckeringly astringent and should never be eaten firm.
identification: A small-to-medium tree with distinctive blocky, dark gray bark broken into thick square plates (often described as alligator-hide). Leaves are simple, oval, and glossy; the round orange fruit is 1 to 2 inches across with a persistent four-lobed calyx (cap) at the stem end. Ripe native persimmons are soft, wrinkled, and often best after they drop. There is no dangerous toxic lookalike, but do not confuse the small native fruit with the large firm Asian/kaki persimmons sold in stores; and never judge ripeness by color alone, since fully orange fruit can still be astringent until it turns soft and jelly-like.
habitat: Native across the southeastern and south-central US, roughly from southern Connecticut (its northern edge, where it is uncommon) south to Florida and west to Texas, Oklahoma, Kansas, and Iowa. It grows in old fields, fencerows, woodland edges, clearings, and disturbed ground. Fruit ripens in fall and often hangs or drops from about September through the first hard frosts into early winter.
craftUse: Ripe persimmon pulp is the workhorse: pressed through a sieve to remove skins and seeds, it becomes the base for puddings, breads, and butters, or is banked in the freezer to bridge the short season. It also dries into candy-like hoshigaki. The real limitation is astringency management. Firm or barely-soft fruit carries soluble tannins that pucker the mouth, so recipes depend on using genuinely soft, ripe fruit and discarding anything still firm.
safetyNote: Eat only fully softened fruit. Unripe persimmon is sharply astringent, and large quantities of unripe fruit have in rare cases been linked to hard stomach masses (diospyrobezoars), especially in people with delayed stomach emptying or prior stomach surgery.

Diospyros virginiana (common persimmon), Go Botany / Native Plant Trust

summary: Pawpaw (Asimina triloba) is the largest edible fruit native to North America, with soft custard-like pulp tasting of banana, mango, and vanilla. On this map it is a prized short-season fresh fruit behind recipes like pawpaw custard ice cream, valued precisely because ripe fruit is too perishable to ship. Only the ripe pulp is eaten; skin and seeds are not, and even the ripe pulp is best enjoyed in moderation.
identification: An understory tree or thicket-forming clonal patch with large, drooping, tropical-looking leaves (6 to 12 inches, unpleasant-smelling when crushed) and smooth green fruit in clusters that ripens yellowish-green to brown and softens like a ripe avocado. The pulp holds two rows of large brown lima-bean-shaped seeds. There is no dangerous toxic tree lookalike, but the fruit itself carries hazards: the SEEDS contain toxic acetogenins (notably asimicin) and were traditionally used as an emetic and are inedible; some people react to the SKIN, especially on underripe fruit.
habitat: Native across the eastern US, roughly from New York and the Great Lakes south to the Gulf and Florida panhandle and west to eastern Nebraska, Oklahoma, and Texas. It grows in rich, moist bottomlands, floodplains, stream banks, and shaded slopes as a forest-understory tree. Fruit ripens in late summer into fall, roughly August through October.
craftUse: Pawpaw is used only as ripe pulp: peeled, de-seeded, and folded raw into custards, ice cream, quick breads, or frozen to extend the glut. Because ripe fruit browns and ferments within days of falling, the whole craft is about speed. Fresh, gentle handling preserves the perfume, which is why it is added raw rather than heavily cooked. A note on scale: it fruits heavily but ripens all at once and only briefly.
safetyNote: Eat only ripe pulp, and treat pawpaw as an occasional seasonal treat rather than a daily staple. Do not eat the skin or the large seeds (seeds are emetic and contain toxic acetogenins). Some people get GI upset or a skin reaction, more so with unripe fruit. The ripe pulp also contains neurotoxic acetogenins (annonacin) that, like the related soursop/custard-apple, have been associated with atypical Parkinsonism where these fruits are eaten heavily and chronically; regulators regard ordinary, moderate consumption as safe, but heavy daily intake is not advised.

summary: Edible sumacs are the true Rhus species (staghorn, smooth, and relatives) whose upright clusters of fuzzy red berries are cold-steeped for a tart, lemony 'sumac-ade' or dried into a tangy spice on this map. They are safe and useful, but sumac is the one fruit here where a genuinely dangerous lookalike shares the common name, so identification is everything.
identification: Eat only Rhus sumacs with dense, cone-shaped clusters of RED, fuzzy (hairy) berries held UPRIGHT above the foliage, on plants of dry, open, sunny ground; their compound leaves have SERRATED (toothed) leaflets. The dangerous lookalike is poison sumac (Toxicodendron vernix), which is in the poison-ivy genus and causes severe skin reactions: it has WHITE-to-cream berries in loose, DROOPING clusters, SMOOTH (untoothed, hairless) leaflets often with a reddish leaf stalk, and grows in WET ground: swamps, bogs, and saturated soils. The rule of thumb: red, fuzzy, upright, toothed leaflets, on dry ground is a true edible sumac; white/pale, drooping berries with smooth leaflets in a wetland is poison sumac; when uncertain, do not harvest.
habitat: True Rhus sumacs are native and widespread across the contiguous US in dry, sunny, disturbed places: roadsides, old fields, fencerows, and sunny hillsides. Berry clusters color up in late summer and persist into fall and winter, giving a long picking window roughly August through November (drier clusters early, before rain leaches the tart surface coating).
craftUse: The tart flavor lives in a coating on the berry surface, so sumac is used gently: whole clusters are cold-steeped in water (not boiled, which pulls bitterness from the seeds) and strained to make a pink lemonade-like drink, or the berry clusters are air-dried and rubbed/ground into a tangy red spice. The limit is that there is little pulp; you are extracting a surface tang, not a juicy fruit, and clusters lose flavor after heavy rain.
safetyNote: Never harvest sumac unless you can positively rule out poison sumac. Use only Rhus with RED, fuzzy, upright berry clusters and toothed leaflets on dry ground; poison sumac has pale, drooping berries and smooth leaflets in wetlands and causes a serious rash on contact.

Rhus typhina (Staghorn Sumac), Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder
Toxicodendron vernix (Poison Sumac), Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center

summary: Black walnut (Juglans nigra) is a large native hardwood whose fall nuts have a rich, bold flavor unlike store walnuts. On this map it drives preserving recipes: cured-and-frozen nuts and green-walnut nocino liqueur. The catch is labor: the green husks stain everything and the nuts must be hulled, washed, cured, and cracked before use.
identification: A tall tree with deeply furrowed dark bark and large pinnately compound leaves (many paired leaflets along a central stalk). The fruit is a round green ball 2 to 2.5 inches across; inside the thick husk is an intensely hard, sculptured shell around the edible nutmeat. Crushed foliage and husks are strongly aromatic. Distinguish black walnut's PINNATELY compound leaves and multi-chambered nut from buckeyes/horse chestnuts (Aesculus), which have PALMATELY compound leaves (leaflets radiating from one point) and a single smooth toxic seed inside a warty husk.
habitat: Native across the eastern and central US into the Great Plains, in rich, moist soils of bottomlands, floodplains, old fields, fencerows, and yards. Nuts drop in fall, roughly September through November.
craftUse: Green husks must be removed first (traditionally by stomping on gravel with gloves and boots), then the nuts are washed and cured/dried for several weeks to develop flavor before cracking. The husks contain juglone along with plumbagin and tannins, which oxidize to a deep brown that indelibly stains hands, clothes, tools, and pavement, so hulling is a messy outdoor job. Nocino instead uses whole GREEN (immature) walnuts steeped in spirit. The reward is a distinctive nut worth the effort; the honest limit is that shells are famously hard and yields per hour of cracking are low.
safetyNote: Wear gloves when hulling: the husks stain skin, clothing, and surfaces a deep, long-lasting brown and can irritate skin. Use the right tools for the very hard shell, and keep the husks and hulls away from horses (juglone-rich black walnut is toxic to them).

sources.title: How to Harvest, Crack, and Store Black Walnuts, Iowa State University Extension
Juglans nigra (Black Walnut), Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder
Black Walnut Toxicity, The Morton Arboretum

summary: Hickories (Carya) are native nut trees; the sweet-kerneled species like shagbark and mockernut yield an excellent wild nut, while the map also uses shells and bark to brew a smoky hickory-bark syrup. Flavor and shell hardness vary widely by species, and the crucial safety skill is telling a true multi-chambered hickory nut from a toxic buckeye.
identification: Hickories have PINNATELY compound leaves (leaflets in pairs along a stalk) and nuts in a husk that splits into segments; the inner nutshell is multi-chambered like a walnut. Shagbark hickory is unmistakable in its shaggy, plate-peeling bark. All hickory nuts are technically safe to eat, but bitternut and pignut can be too bitter to enjoy, while shagbark, shellbark, and mockernut are sweet. The important toxic lookalike is buckeye/horse chestnut (Aesculus): those have PALMATELY compound leaves, a warty/spiny husk, and a single, solid, smooth seed (no chambers) containing toxic aesculin and aescin. If a cracked 'nut' is one solid piece rather than chambered, discard it.
habitat: Various Carya species are native across the eastern and central US, in upland and bottomland forests, ridges, and old homesteads. Nuts drop in fall, roughly September through November, with individual trees varying year to year in crop size.
craftUse: Sweet hickory nuts are cracked for fresh eating or baking, and can be cured and frozen alongside other wild nuts. Separately, the map brews cleaned hickory shells and bark into a smoky, maple-adjacent syrup by simmering and reducing with sugar, a whole-use way to capture the wood's aroma without the tedious nut-cracking. The limits: sweet-species nuts have very hard shells and small kernels, and bitter species are edible but unpleasant, so sorting by species matters.
safetyNote: Verify a chambered nutshell and pinnately compound leaves so you never mistake a toxic single-seeded buckeye (palmate leaves, warty husk, one solid smooth seed) for a hickory. Hickory shells are hard; use proper tools to avoid hand injury.

sources.title: Carya ovata (Shagbark Hickory), Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder
Aesculus glabra (Ohio Buckeye) toxicity, Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder
American hazelnut and hickory nut identification, University of Minnesota / Morton Arboretum plant descriptions

summary: The American hazelnut (Corylus americana) is a native thicket-forming shrub whose small nuts taste much like the European filbert. On this map it is a fresh-use nut, gathered in late summer to early fall. It has no dangerous toxic lookalike; the main challenges are beating wildlife to the crop and freeing the nut from its ragged papery husk.
identification: A multi-stemmed deciduous shrub, typically 8 to 16 feet, that spreads by rhizomes into thickets. Leaves are alternate, oval, and doubly toothed; catkins hang in late winter/early spring. The nuts ripen in clusters, each nut wrapped in a leafy, ragged-edged pair of bracts (a husk with a fringed margin, often with sticky red glandular hairs). Bark is gray-brown and smooth. There is no toxic tree that closely mimics an edible hazelnut cluster; the only practical confusion is with the closely related, also-edible beaked hazelnut (Corylus cornuta, whose husk extends into a long tubular beak) and with other shrub thickets before the distinctive husked nuts form.
habitat: Native across the eastern and central US into the Great Plains, in dry to moist thickets, woodland edges, fencerows, old fields, and prairie margins. Nuts ripen from roughly late July into fall (about August to October), but wildlife strips them fast, so timing the harvest just as husks brown is key.
craftUse: Hazelnuts are gathered while still in the husk (often picked slightly early to beat squirrels and jays), dried, then rubbed free of the bracts and cracked. The kernels are eaten raw or roasted, or ground into meal for baking. The honest limits are that native hazelnuts are smaller than commercial filberts, crops vary year to year, and the fringed husks are fiddly to remove, so this is a modest-yield, hands-on nut rather than a bulk staple.
safetyNote: No notable toxicity and no dangerous poisonous lookalike; the goal of identification is simply to confirm the leafy fringed (or beaked) husk and true hazelnut kernel before eating. Both American and beaked hazelnut are edible.

Corylus cornuta (beaked hazelnut), Go Botany / Native Plant Trust

summary: Most 'wild' apples in the contiguous US are feral Malus domestica: escaped or persisting descendants of planted trees found at old homesteads, fencerows, and roadsides. On this map they drive preserving recipes (apple butter, dried rings), plus whole-use projects making pectin stock and scrap vinegar from cores and peels. The flesh is fully edible; only the seeds carry a minor caution.
identification: A small tree with simple toothed leaves, five-petaled white-to-pink spring blossoms, and familiar rounded pome fruit. Escaped seedling apples tend to be smaller, tarter, and more variable than orchard fruit. There is no dangerous toxic lookalike for the fruit of a recognizable apple tree; the only internal caution is the seeds, which contain amygdalin (a cyanogenic compound). Whole seeds swallowed intact pass through without releasing cyanide; the realistic risk requires deliberately crushing and eating a large number of seeds. The flesh is safe.
habitat: Naturalized nearly everywhere apples are grown across the contiguous US: abandoned orchards, old home sites, fencerows, woodland borders, roadsides, and riverbanks. Old homestead trees and urban plantings are the likeliest sources. Fruit ripens late summer into fall, roughly August through November depending on variety and region.
craftUse: Tart wild apples are ideal for cooking down: slow-simmered into apple butter, sliced and dried into rings, or pressed for cider. Because they are naturally high in pectin and acid, the cores, peels, and windfalls that would otherwise be waste are simmered into a homemade pectin stock for setting other jams, or fermented into scrap vinegar. The main limits are variability (some feral fruit is small, tart, or scabby) and the need to check property access before picking in neighborhoods.
safetyNote: Enjoy the flesh. Do not deliberately crush and eat apple seeds in quantity (they contain cyanogenic amygdalin), but swallowing the odd whole seed is harmless because intact seeds pass through undigested; accidental poisoning from apples is effectively impossible under normal eating.

sources.title: Malus domestica (Apple) as a naturalized tree, Illinois Wildflowers
Are apple seeds poisonous?, Britannica
Amygdalin: Toxicity, Anticancer Activity and Analytical Procedures, PMC review

summary: Wild-found pears (Pyrus communis and its relatives) are usually feral or persisting plantings, most famously the tough old 'homestead' pears still fruiting at long-abandoned house sites. On this map pears are a light-harvest fresh and preserving fruit. The fruit is fully edible; the main practical constraints are access and gritty, sometimes hard flesh.
identification: A small-to-medium tree with glossy simple leaves, white five-petaled spring flowers, and the classic tapered pome fruit. Feral pears are often the durable Kieffer-type: hard, gritty (stone-cell) flesh that keeps well and survives neglect. There is no dangerous toxic lookalike for the fruit of a recognizable pear tree. As with apples, the seeds contain amygdalin and are not meant to be crushed and eaten in quantity, but whole seeds pass harmlessly and the flesh is safe.
habitat: Naturalized and persisting across the contiguous US at old home sites, fencerows, field edges, and roadsides; homestead pears are notably long-lived and often outlast the buildings around them. Fruit ripens in late summer into fall, roughly August through October.
craftUse: Pears are eaten fresh when soft, but hard homestead types are better cooked: poached, canned, or cooked down into butters and preserves, where their grit and firmness soften. They can be under-ripe and rock-hard on the tree and are often best picked slightly early and ripened off the branch. The honest limits are texture (gritty stone cells in feral fruit) and access, since neighborhood and yard trees require permission before harvesting.
safetyNote: Pear flesh has no notable hazard; as with other pome fruit, do not crush and eat the seeds in quantity (they contain cyanogenic amygdalin), though swallowing an occasional whole seed is harmless.

Pear (Pyrus spp.) fire blight, Pacific Northwest Pest Management Handbooks

summary: Peaches (Prunus persica) appear on this map mostly as planted or park trees rather than true wild forage, offering a summer fresh fruit. The soft flesh is a straightforward edible; the one firm rule is to remove and discard the pit, whose kernel contains cyanogenic compounds.
identification: A small tree with long, narrow, willow-like leaves, showy pink spring flowers, and fuzzy-skinned round fruit with a single large, deeply pitted stone. There is no dangerous toxic tree lookalike for a recognizable peach in fruit. The safety point is internal: the hard pit, and especially the almond-like kernel inside it, contains amygdalin, which can release cyanide, so the pit is removed and not eaten.
habitat: Widely planted across the warmer contiguous US and included here in park and homestead settings; it is less common as a genuinely wild self-seeded forage than apples or mulberries. Fruit ripens in summer, roughly June through August depending on region and variety.
craftUse: As a fresh fruit, peaches are eaten out of hand or sliced into desserts, and preserve readily into jams, canned halves, and dried slices, always after pitting. Because they are usually cultivated or park trees, harvesting is a light, permission-first activity rather than bulk foraging. The main limit is that ripe peaches bruise and spoil quickly, so they are handled gently and used soon after picking.
safetyNote: Eat the flesh but discard the stone. The kernel inside the pit contains cyanogenic amygdalin and should not be eaten; do not crack pits to eat the kernels.

sources.title: Prunus persica (Peach), Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder
Are peach pits poisonous?, National Capital Poison Center (Poison Control)
Amygdalin toxicity in stone-fruit kernels, PMC review

summary: The common fig (Ficus carica) is usually a cultivated or escaped planting rather than true wild forage, prized for its soft, syrupy-sweet fruit. On this map it is a light-harvest, permission-first fresh fruit. The ripe fruit is a straightforward edible, but the plant's milky latex sap is a real skin irritant that deserves care while picking.
identification: A large shrub or small tree with distinctive deeply lobed, hand-shaped rough leaves and soft teardrop fruit (a syconium) that ripens green to brown or purple and softens when ready. There is no toxic tree that closely mimics a fruiting fig. The hazard is the plant itself: broken stems, unripe fruit, and especially the leaves and shoots ooze a white LATEX containing furocoumarins (psoralens, such as 8-methoxypsoralen) plus enzymes like ficin. Contact with the leaf and stem sap, followed by sun exposure, can cause phytophotodermatitis (delayed burning, blistering, and long-lasting dark marks); the sap can also cause plain contact irritation.
habitat: Grown and sometimes escaped in the warmer parts of the contiguous US (notably the South, California, and other mild regions), in yards, old plantings, and disturbed edges. Fruit ripens in late summer into fall, roughly August through October, and ripe figs must be picked soft since they do not ripen well off the tree.
craftUse: Ripe figs are eaten fresh or cooked down into jams, syrups, and dried fruit; only the soft, fully ripe fruit is worth picking. The practical craft limits are handling and access. Because the leaf and stem sap irritates skin and is phototoxic, harvest by clipping stems, avoid getting the milky latex on your skin, and wash off any sap and stay out of strong sun afterward. Because figs are usually someone's planting, confirm property access first. Ripe fruit is also highly perishable and best used quickly.
safetyNote: Avoid contact with the milky sap from leaves, stems, and unripe fruit: it can cause a rash and, with sunlight, blistering phytophotodermatitis (the phototoxic psoralens are concentrated in leaf and stem sap). Wash exposed skin and avoid sun exposure after contact. The ripe fruit itself is fine to eat.

Severe phytophotodermatitis from fig sap, PMC case report
Fig Tree-Induced Phytophotodermatitis, PMC case report

summary: Red mulberry (Morus rubra) is a native bottomland tree with sweet, deep purple-black aggregate fruit that ripens in early summer. On this map it is a native, conservation-minded fresh berry: take only fully ripe or fallen fruit and do not cut the tree. Ripe fruit is delicious, but unripe fruit, raw shoots, and the milky sap are best avoided.
identification: Identify by leaves that are sandpaper-ROUGH on top (a hallmark separating native red mulberry from the smoother-leaved, often invasive white mulberry, Morus alba) and can be unlobed or mitten/multi-lobed on the same tree. Ripe fruit is an elongated, blackberry-like cluster that ripens green to red to nearly black. Do not confuse the dark hanging fruit with POKEWEED (Phytolacca americana), a soft herbaceous plant (not a tree) with a thick magenta/red stem and round purple-black berries in hanging clusters; pokeweed is toxic. Also distinguish native red mulberry from white mulberry, which readily hybridizes with it. (Mulberries grow on trees with a single seed structure per drupelet and rough or smooth leaves; if the plant is a low herb with a colored stem, it is pokeweed, not mulberry.)
habitat: Native across the eastern and central US, roughly from New England and the Great Lakes south to Florida and west to eastern South Dakota, Nebraska, Kansas, and central Texas, in rich, moist bottomlands, floodplains, and woodland edges. Fruit ripens in early summer, roughly June through August. Note it is a species of conservation concern in parts of its range (threatened/endangered in several states and in Canada), partly due to hybridization with invasive white mulberry.
craftUse: Fully ripe deep purple-black fruit is eaten fresh or cooked into jams, syrups, and pies; ripe berries stain readily, so many pickers spread a sheet and shake the branches for the fruit that is ready to drop. Consistent with this map's ethic, harvest only ripe or fallen fruit and never damage or cut this native, locally protected tree. The limit is a short season and messy, tender fruit that spoils fast.
safetyNote: Eat only fully ripe fruit. Unripe fruit and the milky sap can cause stomach upset, and the sap may irritate skin, so avoid green fruit and leaf/stem sap. Do not confuse the fruit with toxic pokeweed (a herbaceous plant with a colored stem).

sources.title: Red vs. White Mulberry (Morus rubra vs. Morus alba), Clemson Cooperative Extension HGIC
Native Fruits: Red Mulberry (Morus rubra), Alabama Cooperative Extension System
Phytolacca americana (American Pokeweed) toxicity, Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center

summary: Thimbleberry (Rubus parviflorus) is a thornless bramble with large maple-shaped leaves and a soft, seedy red fruit that slips off its core like a little cap. On this map it is a fresh, tender berry of the Pacific Northwest and northern forests. Only the ripe fruit is eaten, and picking areas overlap with a couple of dangerous or must-cook lookalikes worth knowing.
identification: Look for a thornless (no prickles) shrub with big, fuzzy, maple-like palmate leaves and white flowers, whose ripe fruit is a shallow, aggregate red 'cap' of many tiny druplets that pulls off hollow like a thimble. The aggregate-cap structure is the safety key. Do NOT confuse it with red baneberry (Actaea rubra), a highly toxic herb of the same moist woods bearing SINGLE, shiny, smooth red (or white) berries on a stalk (not an aggregate cap) with a dark eye-like scar, or with red elderberry (Sambucus racemosa), a woody shrub with pinnately compound leaves and conical clusters of small red berries that are toxic raw. Also avoid poison oak/ivy contact while picking.
habitat: Native in moist forests, edges, roadsides, and riparian areas of the Pacific Northwest (west of and into the Cascades) and around the northern Great Lakes/upper Midwest, generally in cooler, temperate and montane settings. Fruit ripens in mid-to-late summer, roughly July through September.
craftUse: Thimbleberries are a fresh-use fruit: eaten on the spot, or made into small batches of soft jam and preserves, often blended with firmer berries because they are so seedy and delicate. The honest limit is fragility and yield. The fruit is very soft, does not keep or transport well, and comes off the plant in tiny quantities, so it suits immediate eating and tiny preserve batches rather than bulk harvest.
safetyNote: Eat only the ripe aggregate 'cap' fruit that pulls off hollow. Confirm that hollow multi-druplet cap to avoid highly toxic red baneberry (single shiny berries on a stalk, dangerous even in small amounts) and toxic-raw red elderberry that share the same habitat.

sources.title: Rubus parviflorus (Thimbleberry), Native Plants PNW
Actaea rubra (Red Baneberry) toxicity, Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center
Sambucus racemosa (Red Elderberry) fact sheet, USDA NRCS PLANTS

summary: Salmonberry (Rubus spectabilis) is a Pacific Northwest bramble of moist coastal forests and stream banks, with early raspberry-like fruit ranging from orange to deep red that detaches like a cap. On this map it is a fresh-use spring/summer berry. The ripe fruit is edible; the key skill is separating it from the toxic-raw red elderberry that grows nearby.
identification: A tall bramble with fine prickles, compound leaves of three leaflets, and bright magenta-pink flowers early in spring, followed by soft aggregate fruit (like a raspberry) that pulls off hollow. The important lookalike is red elderberry (Sambucus racemosa), a woody shrub with pinnately COMPOUND leaves and small red berries in dense CONICAL clusters that are toxic when eaten raw. Salmonberry's individual raspberry-like aggregate fruit is quite different from elderberry's clustered tiny berries; when in doubt, confirm the hollow raspberry-style cap. (Red baneberry, another toxic red-berried plant of these woods, is a low herb with single shiny berries on a stalk, never a raspberry-like cap.)
habitat: Native to the Pacific Northwest coast, from southern Alaska down the coast mostly west of the Cascades through Washington and Oregon into northwestern California, with disjunct inland populations reaching into northern Idaho, in moist coastal forests, stream banks, and wet edges. It is one of the earliest brambles to fruit, roughly May through August depending on latitude and elevation.
craftUse: Salmonberries are eaten fresh and made into small batches of jam, syrup, or preserves. Because the fruit is watery and mild (less sweet and less intense than a raspberry) and very soft, it is best used fresh or combined with more flavorful berries, and it does not store or ship well. Its main appeal is timing: it is one of the first wild fruits of the season.
safetyNote: Eat ripe salmonberry, but distinguish it from red elderberry (woody shrub, compound leaves, conical clusters of tiny red berries) which is toxic raw, and from red baneberry (a herb with single shiny berries). Confirm the hollow raspberry-style aggregate cap before eating.

sources.title: Rubus spectabilis (Salmonberry), Native Plants PNW
Rubus spectabilis, Oregon State University Landscape Plants
Sambucus racemosa (Red Elderberry) fact sheet, USDA NRCS PLANTS

summary: Himalayan blackberry (Rubus armeniacus) is an aggressive West Coast invasive that forms dense, thorny thickets and produces abundant sweet black fruit in late summer. On this map it is the rare case where heavy harvesting is encouraged as invasive removal. The ripe berries are excellent and safe; the cautions are the vicious thorns and a few smooth-skinned toxic lookalikes.
identification: A sprawling bramble with stout, arching canes bearing large RECURVED (hooked) thorns, leaves usually in fives (palmately compound) with distinctly whitish/silvery undersides, and clusters of aggregate berries that ripen red to glossy black. The safety key is the BUMPY, multi-bead aggregate structure of a true blackberry (an aggregate of many small druplets, each with its own tiny seed). Smooth-skinned single berries are a warning: avoid POKEWEED (toxic, magenta-stemmed herb with round purple berries), nightshades (Solanum, smooth round berries), Virginia creeper (dark berries on a woody vine with five-parted leaves), and moonseed (Menispermum canadense, a smooth-fruited vine whose single crescent-shaped seed and toothless leaves distinguish it), all of which are toxic and are single, smooth berries rather than a bumpy aggregate.
habitat: A non-native invasive widespread on the West Coast (California, Oregon, Washington) and other mild regions, dominating roadsides, stream banks, fencerows, vacant lots, and disturbed ground where it crowds out native vegetation. Fruit ripens in late summer into fall, roughly July through October.
craftUse: Because it is invasive, you can harvest freely and heavily. The abundant ripe black fruit is eaten fresh and is ideal for jams, syrups, pies, cordials, and freezing in volume, one of the highest-yield wild fruits on this map. The practical limit is the thicket itself: stout recurved thorns tear skin and clothing, so long sleeves, gloves, and care are essential, and berries near roadsides or sprayed areas should be avoided.
safetyNote: Confirm the bumpy, multi-bead aggregate berry (not a smooth single berry) to rule out toxic pokeweed, nightshade, Virginia creeper, and moonseed, and mind the stout recurved thorns. Skip berries from roadsides or areas that may be sprayed with herbicide.

summary: Wild cranberries are the firm, tart red fruit of two trailing bog vines, the large cranberry (Vaccinium macrocarpon) and the small cranberry (V. oxycoccos). On this map they matter as a classic late-fall, cook-them-down foraging fruit of acidic peatlands, harvested through fall and turned into sauce, jelly, and preserves.
identification: Look for a thin, wiry, trailing evergreen vine with tiny leathery leaves (margins rolled under on small cranberry) creeping over sphagnum moss, bearing firm red berries on threadlike stalks; each berry keeps the small dried crown (calyx) of the old flower. The single most important safety rule: cranberry fruit hangs from a low trailing VINE, never from an upright woody shrub. On the same moss hummocks grow bog rosemary (Andromeda) and bog laurel (Kalmia polifolia), upright shrubs whose every part is toxic, so reject any berry borne on a standing bush. Note also that 'bearberry' (Arctostaphylos uva-ursi) is a name sometimes applied to cranberry in old references but is a different, mealy-fruited mat plant. This reconciles with the entry's lookalikes and drug-interactions tags: cranberry can interact with the blood thinner warfarin.
habitat: Native to cold, acidic bogs and fens across the northern contiguous US, the Northeast, Great Lakes states, and scattered northern mountain and Pacific Northwest peatlands. Fruit ripens roughly September through November; berries are often gathered after the first frost, and small cranberry can also overwinter and be picked in early spring.
craftUse: Too sharply tart and firm to enjoy raw, wild cranberries are a cooking and preserving fruit: simmered with sweetener into sauce, jelly, syrup, or fruit leather, or dried. The berries store and freeze well. The honest limits are that wild stands are small and slow-yielding compared with commercial bogs, and the peatlands they grow in are fragile, tread lightly and take modestly.
safetyNote: Only pick berries on a low trailing vine over bog moss; never take fruit from an upright shrub, which may be toxic bog laurel or bog rosemary. Cranberry may interact with warfarin.

summary: Red elderberry (Sambucus racemosa) bears bright-red berries in cone-shaped clusters and is the most toxic of the common elderberries. On this map it is included only as a cautionary, cook-thoroughly, seeds-strained-out preserve item for experienced foragers, and mainly as a cautionary neighbor to safer berries. When in doubt, leave it.
identification: A woody shrub with opposite compound (pinnate) leaves and small red berries in a rounded-to-conical (pyramidal) cluster, unlike the flat-topped clusters of blue/black elderberry. Confirm the compound leaves and woody stems to separate it from its DEADLY lookalike red baneberry (Actaea rubra), a low herbaceous plant (usually under 2 ft) whose glossy red berries on a single short stalk can be fatal; baneberry berries often carry a persistent dark dot at the tip. Also do not confuse the whole shrub with pokeweed (a soft-stemmed herb with dark-purple berries). This matches the entry's deadly-lookalike, toxic-parts, cook-before-eating, and remove-seeds tags.
habitat: Native across much of the northern and mountainous contiguous US, the Northeast, Great Lakes, Rockies, and Pacific Northwest, in moist woods, clearings, and stream banks. Berries redden in summer, roughly June through August depending on elevation.
craftUse: The raw fruit, and especially the seeds, plus the stems, leaves, and bark, contain cyanogenic glycosides; the seeds carry the highest load and red elderberry is considerably higher in these compounds than blue/black elderberry. Traditional Indigenous practice removed or strained out all seeds and thoroughly cooked the strained juice or pulp into jelly or paste, and some groups leached cooked berries in running water to further reduce toxicity. Cooked seeds are still not considered safe to eat, so the seeds must be removed, not merely heated. It is not a snacking berry, treat it as an advanced, fully-cooked, seed-strained preserve only, or skip it.
safetyNote: Never eat raw. Cook thoroughly and strain out ALL seeds (heat alone does not make the seeds safe). Do not confuse with deadly red baneberry (a low herb with single-stalk red berries). If uncertain, do not eat.

summary: Silver buffaloberry (Shepherdia argentea) is a thorny, silvery-leaved shrub of the northern plains bearing clustered tart red berries. On this map it is a fall foraging fruit best gathered after frost and cooked into sauce, jelly, or syrup, long central to Plains foodways.
identification: Look for a thorny shrub with distinctly silvery, scaly leaves arranged OPPOSITE on the twigs, and small red-orange berries clustered along the branches. The opposite leaves and thorns separate it from the invasive Russian olive (Elaeagnus angustifolia), which has ALTERNATE silvery leaves and silver-to-brown fruit, and from bush honeysuckles (no thorns, no silvery scales; mildly toxic berries). This supports the entry's lookalikes and eat-in-moderation tags.
habitat: Native to stream banks, coulees, and moist draws across the northern Great Plains and Intermountain West. Berries ripen mid-to-late summer into fall (roughly July through October) and are traditionally picked after a frost sweetens them.
craftUse: The berries contain saponins, which make the juice foam when whipped; the whipped-berry dessert sometimes called 'Indian ice cream' is most strongly associated with the related soapberry / Canada buffaloberry (Shepherdia canadensis, higher in saponins), but silver buffaloberry is used for cooked sauces, jellies, and syrups. The honest caution: eaten raw in quantity the saponins can cause digestive upset and act as a mild laxative, so eat in moderation or cook. Saponins are poorly absorbed and cooking breaks them down.
safetyNote: Saponins make raw berries foamy and can cause stomach upset or act as a mild laxative in quantity, eat in moderation or cook. Don't confuse with invasive Russian olive (alternate leaves).

summary: Juniper 'berries' are the soft, ripe blue-black seed cones of certain junipers (Juniperus), used sparingly as a resinous culinary spice, most famously the defining flavor of gin. On this map they are a foraged seasoning, gathered ripe in fall, and one where getting the species right matters.
identification: Culinary junipers include common juniper (J. communis) and eastern red cedar (J. virginiana), plus southwestern species like one-seed, alligator, and California juniper; use only species you have positively identified. NEVER use ornamental savin juniper (Juniperus sabina), its foliage and oil are toxic (traditionally an abortifacient and a kidney poison), it is a common low, spreading landscaping shrub, and its small cones have been mistaken for edible juniper 'berries' with serious poisonings. Do not rely on leaf shape alone as a savin test: common juniper has sharp, spreading needle-like leaves, while savin has soft scale-like foliage, but eastern red cedar (edible) is ALSO scale-leaved, so scale foliage does not by itself mean toxic. Identify to species (ideally with a regional field guide or expert), avoid harvesting unknown ornamental junipers from yards and parks entirely, and never use any juniper whose species you cannot confirm. This matches the entry's positively-identify-species and deadly-lookalike tags.
habitat: Junipers occur across nearly the entire contiguous US, common juniper in the cold north and mountains, eastern red cedar across the East and Midwest, and several drought-hardy species throughout the Southwest and Great Basin. Ripe soft blue-black cones are gathered in fall, roughly September through November.
craftUse: A handful of crushed ripe berries seasons game, sauerkraut, marinades, and preserves; they are always used as a spice, never eaten as a fruit, and always in small amounts (large quantities can irritate the kidneys, and the essential oil is toxic and not for ingestion). Reconciling with the entry's cautions: juniper is traditionally avoided in pregnancy and breastfeeding and with kidney disease, and it can interact with diabetes, diuretic, lithium, and blood-thinning medications.
safetyNote: Identify to species first; never harvest unknown ornamental junipers, toxic savin juniper (J. sabina) has been mistaken for edible juniper. Use only small culinary amounts, never the oil; avoid in pregnancy and with kidney disease.

summary: Kinnikinnick, or bearberry (Arctostaphylos uva-ursi), is a low trailing evergreen mat-shrub with dry, mealy red berries. On this map it is a minor cooked fall/winter fruit; its leaves have a history of traditional medicinal and smoking use and are NOT a food.
identification: A creeping, mat-forming evergreen with small, thick, paddle-shaped (spoon-shaped) leathery leaves rolled under at the edges, urn-shaped white-to-pink flowers, and firm red drupes that persist into winter. The berries are edible but bland, dry, and mealy, best cooked. Two cautions: the mealy red berries superficially resemble other red mat-plant fruits, so confirm the spoon-shaped evergreen leaves and urn-shaped flowers; and this entry's toxic-parts tag refers to the LEAVES, which contain arbutin that metabolizes to hydroquinone, a compound of concern in quantity and for people with liver or kidney disease and in pregnancy.
habitat: Native to cold, sandy, rocky, well-drained sites, northern forests, dunes, and mountain slopes across the northern and western contiguous US. The red berries ripen in fall and hold on the plant through winter, roughly September into December.
craftUse: The dry, seedy, mealy berries are cooked into sauces or added to other fruit rather than eaten fresh; some traditions fried or stewed them. The plant is slow-growing and forms fragile mats, so harvest lightly. Historically the dried leaves were used as a smoking-mixture ingredient and in folk medicine, that is a documented traditional leaf use, not a food, and the leaves should not be eaten.
safetyNote: Eat only the (cooked) berries; the leaves are not food, they have historical medicinal/smoking use and contain hydroquinone-forming arbutin. Confirm the spoon-shaped evergreen leaves and urn-shaped flowers.
