summary: Wintergreen, or eastern teaberry (Gaultheria procumbens), is a low evergreen woodland groundcover with aromatic red berries and the classic wintergreen scent. On this map it is a small, mostly fall-through-spring nibble and tea plant, used in tiny amounts because of its aspirin-like chemistry.
identification: A short (4–6 in) evergreen groundcover with glossy, oval, alternate leaves that smell strongly of wintergreen when crushed, plus bright red berries that persist through winter. The scent is the key test: crush a leaf or berry, true wintergreen smells unmistakably of wintergreen. The similar-looking partridgeberry (Mitchella repens) has OPPOSITE, smooth, whitish-veined leaves, twin/double-eyed berries, and NO wintergreen fragrance (it's harmless but a different plant). Far more important, in the same woods watch for baneberry (Actaea), a taller herb with compound leaves and clustered red or white berries that is DEADLY; wintergreen's low stature, simple aromatic leaves, and single berries distinguish it. This matches the entry's deadly-lookalike and salicylate tags.
habitat: Native to acidic woodland floors, often under conifers and in mixed forests, across the northeastern and Great Lakes states and south in the Appalachians. Berries ripen in fall and persist through winter into spring, so they are gathered roughly September through April.
craftUse: The berries and leaves flavor teas, and the berries can be nibbled or added to preserves, always in small amounts. The honest limit is chemistry: wintergreen contains methyl salicylate, closely related to aspirin, which is concentrated further in distilled wintergreen oil (the oil is toxic in small volumes and must never be ingested). Reconciling with the entry's tags, eat only small amounts of the plant, and avoid it with aspirin/salicylate sensitivity or blood thinners, in pregnancy and breastfeeding, and for children.
safetyNote: Contains aspirin-like methyl salicylate, use small amounts of the plant only and never ingest wintergreen essential oil; avoid with blood thinners/aspirin sensitivity, in pregnancy, and for children. Confirm the wintergreen scent and avoid deadly baneberry.

sources.title: Wild Adirondacks: Wintergreen (Gaultheria procumbens)
Practical Self Reliance: Teaberries (Wintergreen) Identification & Uses

summary: Chokecherries (Prunus virginiana) are pea-sized dark cherries hung in long drooping clusters, mouth-puckeringly astringent raw but transformed by cooking. On this map they are a signature cook-and-strain fruit for syrup and jelly, culturally central across the Plains and northern states.
identification: A shrub or small tree with alternate, finely toothed oval leaves and elongated, drooping, bottlebrush-like clusters (racemes) of small round fruit ripening dark red to nearly black, each holding a single hard stone. Confirming that single pit and the raceme separates it from toxic lookalikes: common and glossy buckthorn (berries in tight axillary clusters, 2–4 seeds, a strong laxative), red elderberry, and, critically, pokeweed and nightshades, whose soft, multi-seeded berries grow on non-woody plants and can be fatal. Watch too for moonseed (Menispermum canadense), a toxic vine whose dark clustered fruit each hold one crescent-shaped seed. This reconciles with the entry's toxic-parts, remove-seeds, cook-before-eating, and lookalikes tags.
habitat: Native and widespread across most of the contiguous US, especially the Great Plains, Midwest, Rockies, and Northeast, along woodland edges, fencerows, draws, and roadsides. Fruit ripens roughly July through September.
craftUse: Chokecherries are a cooked fruit: the ripe flesh is simmered and strained (a food mill or jelly bag keeps the pits whole and out of the food) to make the deep-garnet syrup, jelly, and wine the plains are known for, see this map's 'Chokecherry Syrup (No Pits)' recipe. The firm rule is the stone: like all wild cherries, the pits, leaves, twigs, and bark are cyanogenic, so never crush, blend, or eat the pits and never use the foliage. Wilted leaves are especially dangerous to livestock.
safetyNote: Cook the ripe flesh; never crush or eat the pits, leaves, or bark (cyanogenic). Don't confuse with fatal pokeweed/nightshade/moonseed berries on non-woody plants, or laxative buckthorn.

sources.title: USDA ARS Poisonous Plant Research: Chokecherry (Prunus virginiana)
Colorado State University Guide to Poisonous Plants: Prunus virginiana

summary: Pin cherry (Prunus pensylvanica), also called fire cherry or bird cherry, is a fast-growing pioneer tree bearing small, bright-red, single-seeded cherries. On this map it is a tart cook-down fruit for jelly, syrup, and wine.
identification: A small tree or shrub with alternate, finely serrated, shiny lance-shaped leaves and small (about 1/4 in) bright-red, translucent round cherries borne on long individual stalks (not in the drooping clusters of chokecherry), each with a single stone. It often springs up in dense stands after fire or logging (hence 'fire cherry'). Distinguish the single-stoned cherries from buckthorn (multiple seeds, laxative) and bush honeysuckle berries (paired, mildly toxic). Like all Prunus, the leaves, bark, and seeds are cyanogenic, matching the entry's toxic-parts and remove-seeds tags.
habitat: Native across the northern and Appalachian contiguous US, an early-successional colonizer of burns, clearings, and disturbed well-drained sites. Fruit ripens in mid-to-late summer, roughly July through September.
craftUse: The ripe flesh is edible but very tart, with a large pit relative to the small fruit, so it is almost always cooked and strained into jelly, syrup, or wine rather than eaten fresh. As with every wild cherry, never crush or eat the pits, leaves, or bark, they contain cyanogenic compounds (amygdalin/prunasin) that release hydrogen cyanide when chewed.
safetyNote: Use only the ripe flesh; the pits, leaves, and bark are cyanogenic, never crush or eat them.

sources.title: North Carolina Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox: Prunus pensylvanica
Go Botany (Native Plant Trust): Prunus pensylvanica (pin cherry)

summary: Rose hips are the firm red-orange fruits of wild and naturalized roses (Rosa), left behind after the petals drop. On this map they are a tart, vitamin-C-rich fall fruit for syrup, jelly, and tea, used flesh-only, with the seeds and irritant hairs removed.
identification: Look for firm red-to-orange fruits on thorny (prickly) canes with pinnate (feather-divided) leaves, each hip tipped by the dried five-pointed crown (calyx) of the old flower. Confirming thorns + pinnate leaves + calyx crown rules out toxic red-berried lookalikes: bittersweet nightshade (twining stems, no thorns, soft berries), yew arils (needle foliage, single deadly seed inside a red cup), and honeysuckle berries. All true Rosa hips are edible flesh; the entry's remove-seeds and thorns tags apply. Naturalized multiflora rose is invasive, so harvesting it freely is welcome; native roses warrant a lighter hand.
habitat: Wild and naturalized roses grow across the entire contiguous US in fields, hedgerows, dunes, thickets, and roadsides. Hips ripen in late summer and persist into winter (roughly August through December) and are often sweetest after a frost.
craftUse: Rose hips make bright, tangy syrup, jelly, and tea, see this map's 'Rose-Hip Flesh Syrup (Seeds Removed)' recipe. The essential technique is separation: split each hip and scrape out the seeds and the fine internal hairs (historically used as itching powder) before use, or strain them out thoroughly after cooking through cloth or a fine filter. The hairs, not the flesh, are the irritant hazard.
safetyNote: Remove or strain out the seeds and fine internal hairs, which irritate the digestive tract; mind the thorns. Confirm thorny canes + pinnate leaves + calyx crown to rule out nightshade and yew.

sources.title: Practical Self Reliance: Rose Hips Identification, Uses, and Benefits
Grow Forage Cook Ferment: Foraging Rose Hips & Wild Rose

summary: Hawthorn haws (Crataegus) are small red-to-black apple-like pomes on thorny shrubs and small trees, mild, seedy hedgerow fruit. On this map they are a flesh-only preserve fruit for jelly and a savory fruit 'ketchup.'
identification: Look for small (pea- to cherry-sized) red, orange, or blackish pomes ('haws'), each containing one or more hard stony seeds, on branches armed with stout sharp thorns, with lobed or toothed leaves. Crataegus haws are edible flesh; you needn't key to species. Distinguish from blackthorn/sloe (Prunus spinosa, blue-black single-stone fruit), thornless buckthorn (laxative), and firethorn (Pyracantha). The seeds are cyanogenic and the thorns pose a puncture/infection risk, matching the entry's remove-seeds, thorns, lookalikes, and drug-interactions tags, hawthorn is reported to interact with heart medications such as digoxin and beta-blockers, so people on cardiac drugs should be cautious.
habitat: Native and naturalized hawthorns grow across the contiguous US in hedgerows, old fields, forest edges, and open woods. Haws ripen in fall, roughly September through November.
craftUse: The mild flesh makes a classic clear hedgerow jelly and a fruity brown sauce ('haw ketchup'), see this map's 'Hawthorn haw jelly & haw ketchup' recipe. The whole technique is flesh-only: simmer, then sieve the pulp so the hard seeds stay behind and are discarded. Never crush or eat the seeds (cyanogenic), and take care with the thorns.
safetyNote: Use flesh only and discard the cyanogenic seeds; mind the thorns. Hawthorn may interact with heart medications (digoxin, beta-blockers), be cautious if on cardiac drugs.

sources.title: USDA Forest Service / NSL: Crataegus (hawthorn) fact sheet
Practical Self Reliance: Hawthorn Identification, Benefits & Uses

summary: Mayapple (Podophyllum peltatum) is an umbrella-leaved woodland colony plant whose single ripe fruit is edible, while every other part is seriously poisonous. On this map it is an advanced, ripe-fruit-only foraging item that demands strict discipline.
identification: Recognize the colony of large, deeply lobed, umbrella-like leaves on a low unbranched stem; fruiting stems fork into two leaves with a single lemon-shaped fruit tucked in the crotch. Harvest ONLY the fully ripe fruit, soft, yellow, and often fallen or easily detached. Green unripe fruit, the leaves, stem, roots/rhizomes, and seeds all contain podophyllotoxin and are highly toxic, potentially fatal, with no antidote. The hazard is chiefly within the plant rather than a benign lookalike, which is why the entry carries toxic-parts, ripe-fruit-only, remove-seeds, and avoid-pregnancy tags.
habitat: Native to rich, moist deciduous woods, floodplains, and shaded clearings across the eastern and midwestern contiguous US. The fruit ripens in mid-to-late summer, roughly July through September, usually after the foliage begins to yellow.
craftUse: Only the soft, ripe, yellow pulp is used, deseeded and peeled, eaten in small amounts fresh or made into jelly, jam, or marmalade, with a mild lemon-like flavor. The honest limits are severe: even ripe fruit can act as a laxative in excess; the seeds and skin are not eaten; and podophyllotoxin is toxic and traditionally regarded as an abortifacient, so avoid it in pregnancy and with cell-cycle/chemotherapy drugs. Handling the foliage or root can irritate skin. Harvest sparingly from slow-spreading colonies.
safetyNote: Eat ONLY the fully ripe (soft, yellow) fruit, deseeded and peeled, in small amounts, leaves, stem, root, seeds, and unripe fruit contain podophyllotoxin and can be fatal. Avoid in pregnancy.
