North Carolina Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox: Podophyllum peltatum

summary: Prickly pear fruits ('tunas') are the sweet red-to-purple fruits of Opuntia cacti, borne on the pad edges. On this map they are a desert and dry-country foraging fruit for syrup, jelly, and juice, with the young pads (nopales) also edible.
identification: Ripe tunas are red-to-purple, twist free of flat, jointed cactus pads, and lose their large spines when ripe, but still carry tiny barbed glochids. The defining safety cue: prickly pear is a true jointed-pad cactus with clear (not milky) sap. Never eat a succulent with MILKY sap, that signals a toxic non-cactus (e.g., a spurge, Euphorbia, whose milky latex is irritant and poisonous). This matches the entry's spines/glochids, ripe-fruit-only, lookalikes, and eat-in-moderation tags.
habitat: Opuntia species occur across most of the contiguous US, densest in the Southwest and Great Plains but reaching the East Coast, in deserts, grasslands, sandy soils, and rocky slopes. Tunas ripen in late summer into fall, roughly August through October.
craftUse: The magenta juice makes syrup, jelly, and drinks, and the young pads (nopales) are a cooked vegetable, this map's 'Whole-Plant Prickly Pear' and 'Pulp-and-Seed Scrap Crackers' recipes use both. Preparation is the whole job: singe, scrub, and peel to remove the barbed glochids before eating, and either strain out or spit the hard seeds (they are indigestible and can cause impaction if swallowed in quantity). Pads carry oxalates, so eat in moderation. Some native Opuntia are protected, favor abundant or naturalized stands and check local status.
safetyNote: Remove the tiny barbed glochids (singe, scrub, peel) before eating and don't swallow the hard seeds in quantity; never eat a milky-sapped succulent (toxic non-cactus such as spurge).

sources.title: University of Arizona Cooperative Extension: Prickly Pear Cactus: Food of the Desert (az1800)
University of Nevada, Reno Extension: Eating Cactus: Prickly Pear for Food

summary: Pinyon pine nuts are the large, wingless, richly flavored seeds of pinyon pines (Pinus edulis, P. monophylla), the classic Southwestern pine nut. On this map they are a fall nut harvest of the pinyon-juniper woodland and one of the most culturally significant Indigenous foods in the region.
identification: Pinyons are small, rounded pines of dry uplands; ripe cones open to release big, plump, wingless brown seeds edible raw or roasted. Two safety notes: don't confuse the pine seeds with the blue juniper 'berries' growing right alongside them in the same woodland, and never eat toxic ornamental savin juniper cones or yew arils (whose single seed is deadly). This reconciles with the entry's lookalikes and over-harvest-concern tags.
habitat: Native to pinyon-juniper woodlands of the Southwest and Great Basin, Colorado, Utah, Arizona, New Mexico, and Nevada, at intermediate elevations. Nuts are gathered in fall, roughly September through November, with large crops only every few years.
craftUse: The rich, oily seeds are shelled and eaten raw, roasted, or ground into meal, traditionally knocked from cones with poles, then cleaned, lightly scorched to remove resin, roasted, and stored, a multi-day effort. The honest limits are ecological and ethical: pinyon woodlands face drought die-off, and the nuts are a vital food for Indigenous peoples (Shoshone, Paiute, Washoe, Navajo, Hopi, and others) and for wildlife. Take only a share, honor tribal collecting rights, and remember that occurrence is not permission.
safetyNote: Distinguish the edible pine seeds from the juniper berries growing alongside; never eat toxic savin juniper or deadly yew arils.

sources.title: USU Forestry Extension: Native American Uses of Utah Forest Trees
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center: Pinus edulis (Colorado pinyon)

summary: Mesquite pods are the sweet, dry seedpods of mesquite trees (Prosopis, now often placed in Neltuma), whose pulp mills into a naturally sweet, cinnamon-caramel flour. On this map they are a keystone desert food, and the card where correct dry harvesting is a food-safety mandate.
identification: Confirm true mesquite before harvesting: it is a thorny desert legume with feathery bipinnate leaves and long, straight-to-curved bean-like pods whose sweetness lives in the pulp around (not in) the hard seeds. Many other desert legumes are toxic, mescal bean (Sophora/Dermatophyllum), coral bean (Erythrina), rattlebox (Sesbania), and some senna, while catclaw acacia and paloverde are look-similar legumes to key out; identify carefully. This matches the entry's lookalikes and mold/aflatoxin-risk tags.
habitat: Native across the Southwest and southern Great Plains, Arizona, New Mexico, Texas, and into California, in deserts, washes, and grasslands. Pods ripen and dry on the tree in summer, roughly July through September, and are picked BEFORE the summer monsoon rains.
craftUse: The dry pod pulp is ground into flour for atole, breads, and drinks, see this map's 'Mesquite Pod Atole' and 'Mesquite Pod Flour (Dry-Milled)' recipes; the hard seeds are not milled. The critical food-safety rule, established by University of Arizona research, is that rain-dampened pods can grow Aspergillus flavus and carry carcinogenic aflatoxin even when they look clean: pick bone-dry, brittle, undamaged pods straight off the tree (not off the ground) before the rains, never wet or wash them, keep them dry, and reject any bitter, chalky, discolored, mold-spotted, or insect-damaged pods.
safetyNote: Harvest only bone-dry, brittle pods off the tree before the summer rains and keep them dry, rain-dampened pods can carry carcinogenic aflatoxin. Reject bitter, chalky, moldy, or insect-damaged pods.

sources.title: Ravishankar/Cotty et al., Phytopathology (APS): Aspergillus flavus and Aflatoxin Contamination of Leguminous Trees of the Sonoran Desert in Arizona
Desert Harvesters / University of Arizona: Aflatoxin in mesquite pod flour (research poster)

summary: Chinquapins are small, sweet chestnut relatives, the Allegheny chinquapin (Castanea pumila) in the East and bush/golden chinquapins (Chrysolepis) in the West, each nut wrapped in a dense, spiny bur. On this map they are a fall nut foraged from opened, fallen burs.
identification: Look for a single (Castanea pumila) or 1–3 (Chrysolepis) sweet brown nuts inside a densely spiny, sea-urchin-like bur, on shrubs or small trees with alternate, toothed leaves (often silvery or golden beneath in Chrysolepis). The DANGEROUS lookalike is buckeye / horse chestnut (Aesculus): its husk is smooth or merely warty/leathery with few blunt spikes, never a dense sea-urchin bur, and holds one or two large, rounded, tassel-less nuts with a pale 'buckeye' scar; its toxin (aesculin) is not removed by ordinary cooking. True chinquapin/chestnut nuts have a point or tassel and come in a genuinely spiny bur. Also note the name collision with 'chinquapin oak,' which produces an ACORN, not a bur. This matches the entry's spines, lookalikes, and conservation-concern tags.
habitat: Allegheny chinquapin is native to dry, acidic, well-drained woods and slopes across the central and southeastern contiguous US (roughly Massachusetts to Texas); bush chinquapin grows on rocky slopes and chaparral in California's and southern Oregon's mountains. Burs ripen and drop in fall, roughly August through October.
craftUse: The small nuts are sweet, often likened to a mild chestnut or hazelnut, and eaten raw, roasted, or ground; collect fallen, opened burs and wear gloves against the spines. The honest limits: the nuts are small and fiddly and were never a commercial crop, and some chinquapin lineages are rare or declining (Allegheny chinquapin is affected by chestnut blight, though more resistant than American chestnut). Harvest only from abundant stands and leave plenty of seed for wildlife.
safetyNote: Never confuse the spiny-burred, pointed chinquapin nut with smooth/warty-husked, rounded buckeye/horse chestnut (Aesculus), which is toxic and stays toxic when cooked.

sources.title: North Carolina Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox: Castanea pumila (Allegheny Chinquapin)
USDA Forest Service FEIS: Chrysolepis sempervirens (bush chinquapin)
Missouri Poison Center: Horse Chestnut / Buckeye toxicity

summary: Black walnut (Juglans nigra) is an eastern hardwood whose fallen green-to-black nut hulls are loaded with juglone and tannin. On the ink map it is the classic 'waste-stream' brown pigment: hulls picked up off the ground simmer down into a rich brown-to-black ink with no mordant or chemistry kit required.
identification: A tall hardwood with pinnately compound leaves (usually 15-23 toothed leaflets) and deeply furrowed dark bark; crushed leaves and husks smell spicy. The nut is a round green husk about 1.5-2.5 in across that blackens as it rots on the ground. The main confusion is with butternut (Juglans cinerea, more egg-shaped, sticky-hairy husk) and hickories, harmless here since all Juglans/Carya hulls are usable for brown ink. Do not confuse the tree with tree-of-heaven (Ailanthus), whose leaflets bear only 1-2 basal glandular teeth and smell rank, but you would not be collecting Ailanthus fruit for this anyway. There is no dangerous toxic lookalike for the hull itself; the real hazards are juglone-staining and irritation, not misidentification.
habitat: Native across the eastern and central contiguous US, from the eastern Great Plains eastward; common in rich bottomland soils, fencerows, old farmsteads, and open woods. Hulls drop and blacken roughly September through November.
craftUse: Gather fallen hulls (blackened ones often give the darkest color), simmer gently for a few hours, reduce, and strain twice for a brown ink that needs no added mordant because the hulls are tannin-rich. A little iron water shifts it toward grey-black via the iron-tannate reaction that also underlies iron-gall ink; gum arabic adds pen flow. Honest limits: on paper it is only moderately lightfast and slightly acidic, so keep finished work out of sun; it performs better as a substantive wool/silk brown dye. Use a dedicated non-food pot and jars, walnut stains cookware and skin persistently.
safetyNote: Treat as non-food craft ink and wear gloves throughout: juglone deeply stains skin (brown-black for days to weeks) and can cause irritant contact dermatitis. Black walnut is an FDA tree-nut allergen, so anyone with a tree-nut allergy should avoid handling and processing it and should not breathe the steam or any dust; work in a ventilated space.

sources.title: Juglans nigra (Black Walnut), North Carolina Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox
Why Black Walnuts Can Cause Stained and Blistered Hands, Cleveland Clinic
Black Walnut Toxicity (juglone), The Morton Arboretum

summary: Oak (Quercus) galls and acorn caps are exceptionally tannin-rich and are the raw material for iron-gall ink, the blue-black manuscript ink that dominated Western writing for over a thousand years. On the ink map they matter because, uniquely among natural inks here, iron-gall ink bonds chemically to paper rather than merely staining it.
identification: Oaks are recognized by lobed or bristle-tipped leaves, clustered buds at twig tips, and acorns with scaly caps. 'Oak galls' are round woody swellings made by gall wasps; collect dry, firm ones (ideally with a tiny exit hole showing the wasp has left). There is no toxic lookalike for the gall or cap material itself, but do not substitute galls, fruits, or nuts of other trees, only true oak galls and caps carry the tannin the recipe needs. Acorns of some oaks are bitter, which is irrelevant to ink use. Do not collect galls sprayed with insecticide.
habitat: Oaks occur throughout the contiguous US in nearly every wooded region, upland forests, savannas, parks, and street plantings. Fallen galls and acorn caps are gathered roughly September through December.
craftUse: Crush fallen galls and caps, simmer or steep into a strong dark tannin 'tea,' strain, then add homemade iron water (or a measured amount of ferrous sulfate) a little at a time to 'strike' the black; the line goes on pale grey and darkens as it oxidizes. Galls push toward true black, caps toward brown-grey; gum arabic improves flow. It is the most lightfast natural writing ink here and effectively permanent once dry, but it is acidic and slowly corrodes paper over decades, a gall-heavy, low-iron recipe ages best, and it is not for irreplaceable substrates without buffering.
safetyNote: The finished ink and the iron/vinegar solution are acidic, wear gloves and eye protection when handling ferrous sulfate or adding iron, keep everything labeled non-food and away from children (iron salts are a genuine poisoning hazard for kids), and leave any steel-wool/vinegar jar loosely covered in a ventilated spot since it can off-gas flammable hydrogen.

sources.title: Iron Gall Ink, Recipes and Instructions (irongallink.org)
Making Ink from Oak Galls, The Huntington
Iron Gall Ink research, Library of Congress Preservation

summary: Hickory (Carya) drops tannin-rich outer nut husks every autumn that simmer into a soft, warm brown ink. On the ink map it is the gentler cousin of black walnut, lighter, less aggressive, and a true off-the-ground waste-stream pigment that touches no living tree.
identification: Hickories have pinnately compound leaves (usually 5-9 toothed leaflets), tight or shaggy bark (shagbark hickory peels in long strips), and a husk that splits into four parts to release the nut. The husk is the material. It shares the genus with pecan and other Carya, all fine for brown ink, so lookalike risk is low. Keep hickory husks separate from black walnut's thicker, non-splitting husk (also usable but more heavily staining and higher in juglone). No toxic confusions apply to the husks themselves.
habitat: Native across the eastern and central contiguous US in upland and bottomland hardwood forests, fencerows, and old farms. Husks fall and can be gathered roughly September through November; green or just-fallen husks give the most color.
craftUse: Cover about a quart of husks with water, soak a few days and/or simmer, then strain through cloth and a coffee filter for a weak-coffee brown. A few drops of iron water sadden it toward grey-brown; gum arabic adds body. It is reasonably stable for a tannin brown but not permanent, expect gradual lightening over years, so keep work out of direct sun. Dried, weathered brown husks yield only a weak tan, so favor fresh material. Use a dedicated non-food pot; the dye stains skin for days.
safetyNote: Wear gloves (the dye stains skin brown for days) and reserve the pot and utensils for craft use. Hickory and pecan are tree nuts, so anyone with a tree-nut allergy should avoid handling and processing the husks and avoid the steam/dust. If you add iron water, keep it labeled non-food, off skin and eyes, and its jar loosely covered.

sources.title: Carya ovata (Shagbark Hickory), North Carolina Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox
Walnut Powder / hull-tannin dye method, Botanical Colors
Dyeing With Black Walnut Hull (hull/tannin dye chemistry), Shepherd Textiles

summary: True sumac (Rhus, e.g. staghorn and smooth) carries upright red berry cones rich in both tannin and acidic red pigment. On the ink map it behaves like a small chemistry set: the berry steep is a tart pink-red that shifts with alkali and crashes to grey-black with iron via its tannin.
identification: This is the safety-critical field. Use ONLY true Rhus: upright, cone-shaped clusters of fuzzy RED fruit at branch tips, with toothed (serrated) leaflets, growing on dry sunny roadsides and hillsides. NEVER use poison sumac (Toxicodendron vernix): its berries are white-to-pale-green and DROOP in loose clusters like a wilted grape bunch, its leaflets are smooth and untoothed, and it grows in wet, boggy, swampy ground near standing water. Poison sumac's urushiol causes severe rashes and its smoke is dangerous if inhaled. Rule of thumb: red and upright on dry ground can be true sumac; white/greenish and drooping in a wet area, walk away, and if you are not certain, do not harvest.
habitat: Rhus sumacs are native across much of the contiguous US on dry, sunny, disturbed edges, roadsides, old fields, hillsides, and fence lines. Ripe red drupes are gathered roughly August through November.
craftUse: Steep the red berry cones for a pink-red ink; a pinch of baking soda shifts the hue and iron water strikes it toward cool grey, grey-brown, or near-black via the iron-tannate reaction. Honest limit: the bright red is fugitive (dulls and browns within months in light), for work that lasts, go the iron route, which leans on the stable tannin. Add iron gradually; too much yields a flat, strongly acidic black that can corrode paper and nibs.
safetyNote: Identification IS the safety point: never use poison sumac (white/greenish drooping berries, smooth untoothed leaflets, wet/swampy habitat; causes severe urushiol rash and toxic smoke). Use only true Rhus with upright red fuzzy fruit and toothed leaflets, never burn unidentified sumac brush, and do not harvest if you cannot positively distinguish the two.

sources.title: Poison Sumac (Toxicodendron vernix) identification, NY State Dept. of Environmental Conservation
Toxicodendron vernix (Poison Sumac), North Carolina Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox
Mordant Monday: Spotlight on Sumac (tannin dye), Botanical Colors

summary: Goldenrod (Solidago) is a native late-summer wildflower whose showy flowering tops carry flavonoid pigments (quercetin, kaempferol and related flavonols). On the ink map it is one of North America's most reliable folk yellows, giving a warm honeyed ink from flowers picked in full bloom.
identification: Tall stems topped with dense plumes or sprays of tiny bright-yellow flowers, alternate lance-shaped leaves. Goldenrod is wrongly blamed for hay fever, its pollen is sticky and insect-carried; the real airborne allergen is wind-pollinated ragweed blooming alongside it. Goldenrod's plume of showy yellow flowers is easy to tell from ragweed's inconspicuous greenish spikes. No toxic lookalike is of harvest concern for the flowering tops, but goldenrod is in the aster/daisy family, so handling it can trigger Asteraceae/ragweed contact allergies in sensitive people.
habitat: Native and abundant across the contiguous US in fields, prairies, roadsides, and woodland edges. Collect flowering tops in bloom, roughly August through October.
craftUse: Simmer (don't hard-boil) the flowering tops for a golden-yellow ink or dye. Alum makes the yellow clearer and more durable; a few drops of iron water sadden it to olive/sage or khaki (overdoing it turns muddy brown). The color comes from flavonols, so it holds better than fugitive berry reds but fades faster than weld, expect a bright gold to soften toward muted tan-yellow over months of direct sun, so keep work out of light. As a textile dye it is mordant-dependent: without alum it washes out.
safetyNote: Not considered toxic to people, but goldenrod is in the aster/daisy family and can cause allergic contact dermatitis, anyone with ragweed or Asteraceae allergies should wear gloves and consider a mask, or skip it. Keep the ink and any iron/alum modifiers non-food and away from children and pets.

sources.title: Solidago (Goldenrod), North Carolina Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox
Dyeing With Goldenrod (alum yellow, iron shift, fading), Salt in My Coffee
Allergic contact dermatitis from goldenrod, PubMed (Contact Dermatitis)

summary: Osage orange (Maclura pomifera) is a thorny dioecious tree whose female trees drop many softball-sized, brain-textured green 'hedge apples' each fall. On the ink map the fallen fruit simmers into a soft, experimental yellow that swings toward avocado-green with a pinch of baking soda.
identification: Look for the unmistakable fruit: roughly spherical, bumpy/brain-textured, 3-6 in across, bright yellow-green, oozing sticky milky latex when cut, produced only by female trees. Twigs bear sharp thorns and the bark is orange-brown and furrowed; leaves are glossy, alternate, ovate with a drawn-out tip. The fruit is distinctive with no dangerous lookalike, but the milky latex is a skin and mucous-membrane irritant. Important craft caveat: Osage's famous durable, lightfast yellow lives mainly in the HEARTWOOD (the flavonol morin), so fruit ink will not match wood-dyed color.
habitat: Native to a small south-central range but widely naturalized across the contiguous US from historic hedgerow plantings, fencerows, old farms, roadsides, and pastures. Female trees drop fruit roughly September through November.
craftUse: Simmer chopped fallen fruit for a soft yellow ink; a pinch of baking soda nudges it toward a muted avocado-green (the signature trick), and a few drops of iron water push it toward olive/khaki. Be honest about limits: the fruit's flavonoids (osajin, pomiferin) are not the same lightfast wood dye and are essentially untested for fade, so treat fruit ink as delicate, sketchbook/ephemeral color and keep it out of sun. For a dependable lightfast Osage yellow on fiber, use responsibly-sourced heartwood sawdust rather than the fruit.
safetyNote: Wear gloves and avoid eye contact, the fruit exudes a sticky milky latex that stains yellow, is hard to wash off, and can irritate skin and mucous membranes. Treat the ink as non-food, use craft-only tools, and take only fallen fruit so no living tree is harmed.

sources.title: Maclura pomifera (Osage-orange), The Morton Arboretum
Maclura pomifera, Wikipedia (fruit, latex, dioecious; morin/pomiferin/osajin)
Natural Dyes – Osage Orange (dye from wood/root bark), Folk Fibers

summary: Pokeweed (Phytolacca americana) is a large native herb whose ripe dark berries crush into a vivid fuchsia-magenta juice, the 'pokeberry ink' famously used for Civil War-era letters. On the ink map it delivers the brightest magenta available, and is exactly why it must be handled strictly as a poison.
identification: A robust herb 4-10 ft tall with a thick, dramatically magenta-to-purple stem, large simple oval leaves up to a foot long, and berries hanging in a long grape-like raceme (a straight spike on a single stem); each berry has a distinctive flattened, dented shape. Do NOT confuse it with elderberry, which has compound leaves and flat-topped clusters, pokeweed's magenta stem, simple leaves, and drooping raceme are the tells. All parts are toxic (roots most of all), and the toxins are not destroyed by crushing, so handle it as poisonous throughout.
habitat: Native across the eastern, central, and southern contiguous US and naturalized elsewhere; thrives in disturbed ground, field edges, fencerows, clearings, and yards. Ripe purple-black berries appear roughly August through October.
craftUse: Crush and lightly process ripe berries for a magenta ink; unlike anthocyanin berries, pokeberry color is a betalain (phytolaccanin/betanin), so it does NOT do the acid/base rainbow, it stays magenta (mild acid keeps it brighter, strong alkali dulls it). It is strongly fugitive: the magenta drifts to mauve then tan-brown within months of light, so keep work dark or under UV-filtering glass and treat it as ephemeral. As a textile dye it is poor and short-lived.
safetyNote: Toxic plant, all parts are poisonous (roots most). The juice and sap can irritate and may be absorbed through broken skin, so always wear waterproof gloves and eye protection, never taste it, keep the labeled bottle from children and pets, and never burn pokeweed. If ingested or a reaction occurs, contact US Poison Control: 1-800-222-1222.

sources.title: Phytolacca americana (Pokeweed), North Carolina Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox
Pokeweed: all parts toxic, sap causes rash, UC ANR
Phytolacca americana, Wikipedia (phytolaccanin/betacyanin pigment; toxicity)

summary: Autumn olive (Elaeagnus umbellata) is a widespread invasive shrub whose ripe red, silver-speckled berries are rich in lycopene. On the ink map it gives a pale pink-crimson wash, and picking it does double duty, every cluster stripped is seed birds won't scatter across the next hillside.
identification: A shrub of edges and old fields with silvery-scaled leaf undersides, silvery-to-brown-speckled young twigs (often thorny), and small round RED berries flecked with tiny silver/brown speckles. The speckled red fruit and silvery foliage are distinctive; it has no common deadly lookalike, but never substitute an unidentified red berry, if you cannot confirm the silvery scales and speckles, do not harvest. Use only fully ripe red fruit; unripe fruit is intensely astringent.
habitat: A non-native invasive naturalized across the eastern and central contiguous US (and spreading west) on roadsides, reclaimed land, pastures, and forest edges. Ripe fruit is gathered roughly September through November.
craftUse: Crush ripe berries for a pale pink wash; the red is lycopene (a carotenoid), NOT a pH indicator, so the dramatic acid/base flips of anthocyanin inks don't reliably occur, vinegar only slightly brightens, baking soda dulls it, and iron saddens it toward brown-purple. It is strongly fugitive: a fresh pink pales toward dull tan within weeks of light. It is effectively ink/wash only, lycopene is fat-soluble and won't bond into washed cloth, so it fails as a durable textile dye (the leaves can be dyed instead for brown/gold).
safetyNote: The ripe berries are edible, but the finished ink is not food (it may contain preservatives or gum arabic, or come from a craft-dedicated pot), label the jar and keep it from children and pets. Harvest only where permitted: invasive status encourages removal but does not override land access or property rules.

sources.title: Invasive Plant Factsheet: Autumn olive, UConn Integrated Pest Management
Autumn Olive: Identification and Uses, Practical Self Reliance
Fruit of Autumn Olive: A Rich Source of Lycopene, ResearchGate

summary: Wineberry (Rubus phoenicolasius) is an aggressive Asian raspberry, introduced around 1890 for breeding, now blanketing eastern woodland edges. On the ink map its ripe berries crush into a magenta-rose juice that writes beautifully and, like other Rubus berries, fades almost as fast as you can admire it.
identification: A bramble whose canes and unripe fruit-cups are furred in distinctive sticky RED glandular hairs (its most reliable tell), with three-parted leaves that are white-woolly beneath and bristly canes. Ripe berries are bright orange-red and cup-shaped. It is a beginner-safe edible with no toxic berry lookalike in North America, but don't confuse it with the toxic ink plants on this site (pokeweed, privet, elderberry leaves/stems, Boston ivy). Canes are bristly, wear long sleeves.
habitat: A non-native invasive naturalized across the eastern contiguous US on woodland edges, streambanks, fields, and disturbed ground. Ripe fruit is picked roughly June through August.
craftUse: Crush and gently process ripe berries into a rose-pink ink. The color is anthocyanin (a pH indicator): mild acid (vinegar/lemon) keeps it in its brightest pink-red range, baking soda shifts it toward a dull purple-blue-grey, and iron saddens it to grey-brown. It is notoriously fugitive, bright rose-magenta dulls toward tan/grey within weeks of light, so store work in the dark and treat it as ephemeral. Not a durable textile dye; the juice doesn't bond to cellulose and washes out even with mordant.
safetyNote: The berries are edible, but the finished ink (with vinegar/preservative/gum arabic, or from a craft-dedicated pot) is not food, label it and keep from children and pets. Wear long sleeves and gloves against the bristly canes and its strong staining.

sources.title: Rubus phoenicolasius (Wineberry), North Carolina Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox
Rubus phoenicolasius, Wikipedia (range, invasive status, glandular hairs, edibility)
Making Berry Inks (crush/simmer/strain, vinegar, gum arabic, fading), The Druid's Garden

summary: Elderberry (Sambucus) is a shrub whose ripe blue-black berries crush into a wine-dark, anthocyanin-rich juice long used for ink, dye, and manuscript color. On the ink map it gives a soft purple that doubles as a living pH paint, but the plant's toxic parts and toxic lookalikes make careful identification and preparation essential.
identification: A large shrub with pinnately compound, finely toothed, opposite leaflets and broad FLAT-topped clusters of tiny cream flowers that ripen to small purplish-black berries in flat clusters. Rule out several hazards before harvesting: (1) pokeweed (simple leaves, magenta stem, drooping grape-like berry racemes); (2) red elderberry (Sambucus racemosa), with red berries in pyramidal (not flat) clusters, considered more toxic; (3) water hemlock (Cicuta) and devil's walking stick (Aralia spinosa), both cited elderberry lookalikes, note that elderberry never has thorns or prickles, which immediately eliminates Aralia, and water hemlock is an herbaceous wetland plant with a very different form. If you cannot positively confirm Sambucus canadensis/nigra, do not harvest.
habitat: Common (S. canadensis/nigra) across the contiguous US along streambanks, moist thickets, fencerows, and roadsides. Use only ripe, fully colored berries, roughly July through September.
craftUse: Cook ripe, fully de-stemmed berries into a purple ink. Being anthocyanin-based, it is strongly pH-reactive: mild acid pushes it red/magenta and slightly stabilizes it, baking soda tips it blue-grey then murky green, iron saddens it to slate grey-purple, and alum brightens the purple. It is classically fugitive, expect shift and fading within weeks to months of light, so store finished work in the dark. As a textile dye it is soft but not washfast.
safetyNote: Elderberry is toxic and this is not food: leaves, stems, bark, roots, and unripe (green) berries contain cyanogenic glycosides, never include them, use only cooked ripe berries, avoid the more-toxic red elderberry, and rule out water hemlock (one of North America's most lethal plants) before you pick. Keep the labeled ink from children and pets.

sources.title: Sambucus canadensis (Common Elderberry), North Carolina Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox
Play it safe when preserving elderberries (toxicity, cook berries), OSU Extension EM-9446
Sambucus canadensis identification, Minnesota Wildflowers

summary: Privet (Ligustrum) is a widely naturalized invasive hedge shrub whose ripe blue-black berries once colored 18th-century French playing cards. On the ink map it gives a moody red-purple ink that lurches toward green when you add a pinch of baking soda, and every berry picked is invasive seed removed.
identification: An evergreen-to-semievergreen shrub with OPPOSITE, dark-green glossy oval leaves, smooth grayish-tan bark, and oblong blue-black drupe fruit in clusters. Common invasive species are Chinese privet (L. sinense), European/common privet (L. vulgare), and glossy/Japanese privet (L. lucidum). The berries are toxic and not edible; do not confuse the fruit with any edible berry, and note the opposite glossy leaves and clustered blue-black drupes. The plant is toxic to dogs, cats, horses, and livestock as well as people.
habitat: A non-native invasive naturalized across the eastern, southern, and parts of the western contiguous US in old hedges, forest edges, floodplains, fencerows, and abandoned lots. Ripe berries persist September through winter; frost-touched fruit often gives better color.
craftUse: Crush or simmer ripe black berries (about an hour) and strain for a muddy red-purple ink. pH is the main lever: alkali (baking soda/washing soda) walks it toward purple, blue, teal, and green, the historic 'sap green' trick, while acid tends to strip the color and iron saddens it to grey/grey-green. Like all anthocyanin berry inks it is fugitive, fading and browning within weeks to months of light, so keep work dark. As a wool dye it needs scouring, alum, and a pH shift, and still fades.
safetyNote: Toxic, do not ingest: privet berries and leaves contain terpenoid/secoiridoid glycosides that can cause mouth and throat irritation, stomach pain, vomiting, and diarrhea. Wear gloves, keep the labeled non-food ink away from children, pets, and livestock, and bag spent pulp for the trash.

sources.title: Ligustrum sinense (Chinese Privet), North Carolina Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox
Privet (Ligustrum) toxicity, Queensland Poisons Information Centre
Natural Dye: Privet Berries (simmer, pH shift to green/teal, iron to grey), Myra Made Color

summary: Wild grape (Vitis), grouped here with the inedible ornamental Boston ivy (Parthenocissus tricuspidata) for pigment mapping, yields a moody blue-purple ink from anthocyanins. On the ink map the juice is a living pH meter, but the grape's dangerous lookalikes make identification the critical step.
identification: This is the safety-critical field. Wild grape has forked (branching) tendrils opposite the leaves, ROUND seeds inside the berry, shredding bark, and lobed/toothed leaves. Two poisonous lookalikes must be ruled out: (1) Canada moonseed (Menispermum canadense) mimics grape clusters but is toxic and can be fatal, it has a single flat CRESCENT-shaped seed, NO tendrils, smooth-edged (non-toothed) leaves attached near the leaf edge (peltate), and its berries are dull rather than translucent. (2) Boston ivy / Parthenocissus berries are toxic (calcium oxalate raphides, also a skin/mucous-membrane irritant), included here for color only, never eaten. Porcelainberry is another non-grape climber to distinguish. If seeds, tendrils, and leaf shape don't all confirm Vitis, do not harvest.
habitat: Wild Vitis is native across the contiguous US in woodland edges, thickets, riverbanks, and fencerows; Boston ivy is a planted/naturalized ornamental on walls and structures. Dark ripe fruit is gathered roughly August through October.
craftUse: Crush ripe dark fruit for a blue-purple ink. As anthocyanin, it is reactive: mild acid shifts it magenta/pink, baking soda toward blue-green (but dulls it and shortens shelf life), and iron reacts with the fruit's tannins for greyed violets, slate, and grey-black. Anthocyanins are among the most fugitive natural colorants, it shifts and dulls within weeks of light and keeps fading even in the dark, drifting toward muddy grey-brown, so scan or photograph finished work. Not a durable textile dye.
safetyNote: Identification is the hazard: distinguish wild grape (forked tendrils, round seeds, toothed leaves) from poisonous Canada moonseed (single crescent seed, no tendrils, smooth leaves, potentially fatal), and treat toxic Boston ivy berries as color-only with gloves and eye protection. This ink is not food. If ingestion is suspected, call US Poison Control: 1-800-222-1222.

sources.title: Menispermum canadense (Canada Moonseed), North Carolina Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox
Moonseed vs Wild Grape: Plant ID, Birds and Blooms
An artistic introduction to anthocyanin inks (pH color shifts), Science in School

summary: Black tupelo / black gum (Nyssa sylvatica) is a native tree famous for its brilliant scarlet fall color and for tupelo honey. On the ink map its small blue-black drupes, edible but mouth-puckeringly sour, yield a beautiful, fleeting violet ink from their anthocyanins.
identification: A medium tree with glossy, obovate, mostly untoothed leaves that turn early and intensely red in autumn, horizontally tiered branching, and small (about 1/2 in) blue-black drupes on long stalks, usually 1-3 per cluster, each with a single grooved stone. The fruit is edible (very sour), so there is no deadly berry confusion, but distinguish it from other blue-black-fruited trees; the fiery-red early foliage and tiered limbs are good tells. Leave plenty, the fruit is key fall food for migrating birds.
habitat: Native across the eastern and southeastern contiguous US in moist woods, bottomlands, swamp margins, and upland slopes. Ripe blue-black drupes are gathered roughly September through November.
craftUse: Crush ripe drupes for a violet-purple ink or watercolor. As anthocyanin it is strongly pH-sensitive: mild acid shifts it warmer (pink/magenta/reddish-purple), baking soda cooler (blue-purple, then dull grey with too much), and iron darkens it toward grey-black rather than brightening it. It is genuinely fugitive, the bright violet dulls toward grey-brown within weeks to months of light, so keep it out of sun. Unreliable as a textile dye; it washes and fades even on wool.
safetyNote: The fruit is edible but the finished ink has non-food additives and is made in a craft-dedicated pot, so don't ingest it. Anthocyanins stain readily, so wear gloves and protect surfaces, and harvest lightly since the drupes are important fall food for birds.

sources.title: Nyssa sylvatica (Black Tupelo), North Carolina Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox
Black tupelo fruit: ID & use (deep purple, anthocyanin-rich, edible/sour), Tyrant Farms
Anthocyanidins and anthocyanins: pH-dependent color, NIH/PMC

summary: Himalayan blackberry (Rubus armeniacus) is an aggressive feral bramble, from a single Luther Burbank cultivar gone wild, that now buries Pacific Northwest lowlands in thorny thickets. On the ink map its abundant glossy black late-summer fruit crushes into an immediate violet-to-lavender ink with no cooking, generous but thoroughly fugitive.
identification: A robust cane-forming bramble with stout, recurved (hooked) spines on ridged (angled) canes, palmately compound leaves (usually 5 leaflets) that are white-woolly beneath, and clusters of glossy black aggregate berries. It is a beginner-safe edible with no toxic berry lookalike, but the canes are heavily armed, the real hazard is the thorns, not misidentification. Wear long sleeves, sturdy gloves, and eye protection when reaching into thickets.
habitat: A non-native invasive dominant west of the Cascades in the Pacific Northwest and naturalized elsewhere in the western and eastern contiguous US, smothering riparian banks, fence lines, and vacant lots. Ripe black berries are picked roughly July through September.
craftUse: Crush ripe berries for an immediate violet-lavender wash, no cooking needed. It is almost pure anthocyanin and acts as a living pH indicator: mild acid (vinegar/lemon) swings it toward magenta-red, baking soda toward blue-grey then grey-green, and iron water dulls it to slate grey-violet while slightly slowing the fade. Be honest, it is fugitive, fading and shifting toward grey-brown within weeks to months in daylight; excellent for sketchbooks, casual lettering, and pH demonstrations, never for archival work or anything meant to last.
safetyNote: The plain juice is edible, but any batch touched by iron water, retired non-food tools, or essential oils is strictly craft-only and must be labeled and kept out of the kitchen. Mind the stout recurved thorns with long sleeves, gloves, and eye protection, and dispose of seeds/pulp responsibly so you don't spread this invasive.

sources.title: Rubus armeniacus (Himalayan blackberry), California Invasive Plant Council (Cal-IPC)
Fugitive Colour: plants to never use for natural dyes (berries), Wearing Woad
Making Berry Inks (crush/strain, vinegar, gum arabic, fading), The Druid's Garden

summary: Prickly pear (Opuntia polyacantha, O. engelmannii) is a spiny native cactus whose ripe fruits (tunas) are packed with red-magenta betalain pigments (betacyanins such as betanin/indicaxanthin). On this map it is a plant-pigment ink source, distinct from cochineal, the scale insect that lives on the pads.
identification: Flat, paddle-shaped green pads studded with clusters of tiny barbed glochids (and often longer fixed spines); showy yellow-to-pink spring flowers; egg-shaped fruits that ripen deep red-purple. The main field hazard is the glochids, not a poisonous lookalike: the near-invisible, hair-like glochids detach on the lightest contact and are miserable to remove from skin, lips, and eyes, so never touch a pad or fruit bare-handed. There is no deadly plant lookalike, but do not confuse the plant's own fruit pigment with cochineal (the crimson carminic-acid dye from Dactylopius scale insects that encrust the pads under a white waxy fluff), they are entirely different sources.
habitat: Widespread across the arid and semi-arid West, the Great Plains, and Southwest, on dry rocky slopes, grasslands, and desert scrub; various Opuntia occur in nearly every contiguous state. Tunas ripen and color up in late summer into fall (roughly August-October).
craftUse: Ripe tunas are split and the pulp pressed and strained to give a vivid magenta betalain juice used as a fresh ink or wash. Honesty about limits matters: these betacyanins are water-soluble and fugitive, degraded by light, heat above roughly 60 C, oxygen, and pH shifts, so the color fades and is best treated as an ephemeral, short-life ink, not an archival one. Handle fruit with tongs and gloves throughout, and singe or scrub off glochids before processing.
safetyNote: Glochids: never touch pads or fruit bare-handed, use tongs and gloves and remove glochids before processing. The finished color is fugitive and not lightfast.

sources.title: Opuntia engelmannii (Engelmann's prickly pear), USDA Forest Service, Fire Effects Information System
Color Properties and Stability of Betacyanins from Opuntia Fruits, J. Agric. Food Chem.

summary: Rouge plant / pigeonberry (Rivina humilis) is a soft-stemmed perennial of shady Southern woods whose translucent red berries hold a beet-like betalain pigment (rivianin) long used as a folk dye and ink. On this map it is a fugitive red ink source, a craft material, never food.
identification: A slender 1-3 ft herb of shade, with thin wavy-edged leaves 1-3 inches long, small white-to-pink flowers on 2-3 inch spikes, and numerous small, glossy, almost translucent red berries strung along the lower stem while the tip still flowers. The berries are reported to be poisonous to humans, so the key safety point is behavioral: do not taste or eat any part, and keep the bright berries away from children and pets, who may mistake them for food. There is no lethal lookalike, but its red berries can be confused with other small woodland berries of unknown edibility, collect only for craft, never to eat.
habitat: Native to the southern and southwestern US (Florida and the Gulf states through Texas and into Arizona), growing beneath trees and shrubs in shaded, often disturbed ground. Berries ripen and persist through late summer and fall (roughly August-November).
craftUse: Ripe berries are crushed and strained for a rouge-red juice used as ink or dye. It is fugitive, expect the color to shift and fade, and it stains skin, fabric, wood, and grout aggressively, so work over a tray, wear gloves, and keep all tools craft-only. This is decidedly a craft ink, not a food colorant: the berries are reported toxic and it should never be used to color anything edible.
safetyNote: Berries are reported toxic if ingested, do not taste, eat, or use to color food; wear gloves and keep tools craft-only, away from children and pets.

sources.title: Rivina humilis (pigeonberry), Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center, Native Plant Database
Rivina humilis (bloodberry), CABI Compendium

summary: Rocky Mountain bee plant (Cleomella serrulata; long known as Cleome serrulata and Peritoma serrulata) is a self-seeding native annual whose whole flowering plant is boiled down to a black pigment cake used as the binder/paint in ancestral and modern Pueblo pottery painting. On this map it is the classic plant-based black for paint and ink.
identification: An erect annual to several feet with palmately trifoliate leaves (three slender leaflets) and showy rounded clusters of pink-to-purple four-petaled flowers with long protruding stamens, followed by dangling slender pods; the foliage is pungent when crushed. The most relevant confusion is yellow bee plant (Cleomella lutea / Peritoma lutea), which has yellow flowers and typically five to seven leaflets rather than three and is not the pottery-paint plant. Note also that beeplant foliage can accumulate nitrates and is reported toxic to livestock in quantity, so treat it strictly as a paint/dye plant, not forage. Genus names have shifted; Cleomella serrulata is the currently accepted name, with Cleome serrulata and Peritoma serrulata as synonyms.
habitat: Native across the Rocky Mountain West, Great Plains, and Southwest on disturbed ground, roadsides, sandy soils, and old fields. It is harvested while flowering in early-to-mid summer (roughly June-August).
craftUse: Following documented Ancestral Puebloan, Pueblo, and Hopi practice, whole flowering plants are boiled for a long time until the liquid thickens and blackens, then dried into hard cakes ('guaco') that are later rehydrated in hot water to a paintable consistency used as the black paint/binder for pottery and basketry designs. The same plant also yields a yellow-green dye used in weaving. Use it for paint and dye only; it is pungent and not for ingestion.
safetyNote: Paint/dye use only, do not ingest; foliage can accumulate nitrates and is reported toxic to livestock in quantity.

sources.title: Rocky Mountain Beeplant, Utah State University Extension, Range Plants
Rocky Mountain Beeplant (Peritoma serrulata) plant guide, USDA NRCS

summary: Brazilian pepper (Schinus terebinthifolia) is a South American tree, a prohibited Category I invasive in Florida, whose ripe red berries give a red stain. On this map it is an invasive-removal ink source, and, critically, a poison-ivy/cashew-family relative to handle with care.
identification: A multi-stemmed, tangled evergreen with alternate, odd-pinnately compound leaves (usually 3-11 finely toothed leaflets), a reddish, often narrowly winged leaf stalk (rachis), a resinous/turpentine smell when crushed, and dense clusters of small bright-red berries in fall and winter. Safety-critical: it is in the Anacardiaceae, the poison-ivy/poison-oak/cashew family, and its sap contains urushiol-type alkenylphenols (cardol, cardanol) that cause allergic contact dermatitis in sensitive people; airborne irritants from crushing, flowering, and especially burning it can also affect skin, eyes, and lungs. Distinguish it from harmless red-berried shrubs by its compound leaves, reddish winged rachis, and resinous smell, and do not confuse it with true poison sumac or poison ivy, which share the family and hazard.
habitat: Naturalized and aggressively invasive in central and south Florida, with populations also in Texas, coastal Alabama, California, and Hawaii; it forms impenetrable thickets in disturbed and natural areas. Berries ripen roughly November-January.
craftUse: Ripe red berry clusters are simmered/steeped for a red stain; cutting and clearing the plant is ecologically positive removal in Florida rather than foraging. Wear nitrile gloves and long sleeves throughout because of the urushiol-type sap, work upwind if crushing quantities, and NEVER burn any part, the smoke releases irritants that can trigger severe respiratory, eye, and skin reactions.
safetyNote: Poison-ivy/cashew-family relative, sap urushiol-type compounds cause contact dermatitis; wear gloves and long sleeves, avoid the smoke, and never burn it.

sources.title: Schinus terebinthifolia (Brazilian pepper), Atlas of Florida Plants, University of South Florida
Brazilian Peppertree: Watch Out for This Lesser-Known Relative of Poison Ivy, MDedge Dermatology

summary: Oregon grape and barberry (Berberis/Mahonia aquifolium, B. repens, B. fremontii, B. trifoliolata) are spiny-leaved native shrubs whose ripe dusky-blue berries make a pH-shifting blue-to-purple ink. On this map the ink comes from the berries, deliberately not the bright-yellow berberine roots.
identification: Holly-like pinnately compound leaves with stiff, spine-tipped leaflets (5-9 in tall Oregon grape; more in Cascade Oregon grape, B. nervosa), dense yellow spring flower clusters, and clusters of dusty blue-black grape-like berries. Scratch a root or inner bark and it is vivid yellow, the berberine. There is no deadly lookalike; the tart blue berries are in fact edible (traditionally eaten and made into jelly and wine), so this is a safe plant to gather, the spiny, holly-like evergreen leaves distinguish it from unrelated true grapes. The berries are safe as food in moderation, but the concentrated berberine-bearing root/bark dye should not be swallowed and is very bitter.
habitat: Across the western US (and B. trifoliolata in Texas/the Southwest) in dry woodlands, chaparral, foothills, and rocky slopes; the berries ripen and darken in mid-to-late summer (roughly July-September).
craftUse: Ripe dusky-blue berries are crushed and strained for a blue-purple ink whose hue shifts with pH (leaning pink/red in acid, blue/purple in base). The famous bright-yellow berberine dye lives in the roots and bark, but digging kills these slow-growing native shrubs, so this map uses berries only and treats the root-dye as off-limits. The berry ink is on the fugitive side; expect some shift over time.
safetyNote: Use berries, not roots, digging destroys slow-growing shrubs, and the concentrated root/bark berberine dye is very bitter and should not be swallowed.

sources.title: Berberis aquifolium (Oregon grape), North Carolina Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox
Tall Oregon Grape, Mahonia aquifolium, Native Plants PNW

summary: Sagebrush and mugwort (Artemisia tridentata, A. californica, A. douglasiana) are aromatic Asteraceae shrubs and herbs whose foliage yields yellow-to-olive dye. On this map they are a light-harvest yellow/green dye source, and explicitly NOT true white sage (Salvia).
identification: Big sagebrush has silvery-gray, wedge-shaped leaves tipped with three lobes (the 'tridentata' trident) and a strong sage-like scent; California sagebrush and mugwort are more finely divided and green. The critical distinction is identity, not toxicity: these Artemisia are in the daisy family (Asteraceae), NOT the Salvia (true sage, family Lamiaceae) used in smudging, the two smell similar and share the name 'sage' but are unrelated genera. Do not substitute one for the other. Note that Artemisia pollen/foliage can trigger allergy and the plants contain aromatic compounds (e.g., thujone); handle for dye only.
habitat: Big sagebrush dominates the arid Intermountain West and Great Basin; California sagebrush occupies coastal scrub; mugwort (A. douglasiana) follows streambanks and moist disturbed ground in the West. Foliage is harvestable through the growing season (roughly May-October).
craftUse: Leaves and soft stems are simmered to give a yellow-to-olive dyebath on wool and other protein fibers; an iron mordant/afterbath pushes the color greener and grayer. Light tip-harvest from vigorous plants has negligible impact. It is aromatic and for dye use only, not ingestion.
safetyNote: Aromatic, dye use only, do not ingest; this is Artemisia (daisy family), not white sage (Salvia).

sources.title: Artemisia tridentata (big sagebrush), Wikipedia (Asteraceae; yellow/gold dye)
Summer Herb Study, Artemisia (Artemisia vs. Salvia), UC ANR

summary: Mormon tea (Ephedra viridis) is a broom-like green desert shrub whose jointed twigs give a soft tan-to-yellowish-green dye documented in Navajo, Paiute, and other tribal use. On this map it is a light-harvest desert-tan dye source; unlike Asian Ephedra, this species carries little to no stimulant alkaloid.
identification: An upright shrub to about 3 ft of stiff, thin, jointed green (aging yellow-green) photosynthetic stems, with tiny scale-like leaves at the nodes that quickly drop, leaving a broom-like form; it bears small cone-like reproductive structures rather than flowers. It resembles other Ephedra species and, superficially, unrelated rush-like or jointfir plants, the jointed green stems and paired node sheaths identify the genus. Unlike the Asian medicinal species (E. sinica, 'ma huang'), the North American Ephedra viridis contains only trace or undetectable ephedrine/pseudoephedrine, but positive ID to species is not something a beginner should rely on for consumption, so keep it to dye use.
habitat: Deserts and dry slopes of the Intermountain West and Southwest, favoring sandy soils derived from sandstone. Green twigs can be clipped across the growing season (roughly April-September).
craftUse: Green jointed twigs are boiled, historically with alum, to produce a light tan on wool; the crushed mature stems have also been used for a yellowish-green dye. Clip a share of twigs from across the abundant stem mass and leave the shrub rooted. Treat it as dye-only on this map: while E. viridis has traditionally been steeped as a mild beverage tea, alkaloid content varies across the genus and species ID is easy to get wrong, so do not brew unknown Ephedra.
safetyNote: Dye use on this map; E. viridis itself carries little to no ephedrine, but other Ephedra do, so do not brew or ingest unidentified plants.

sources.title: Ephedra viridis (green ephedra / Mormon tea), Native American Ethnobotany Database (BRIT)
Ephedra viridis, Wikipedia (trace alkaloid content; traditional tea and dye use)

summary: Camphor tree (Cinnamomum camphora, now often placed in Camphora officinarum) is an East Asian evergreen, a Florida Category I invasive, whose aged leaves and leaf litter give a dark brown dye. On this map it is an invasive-removal dye source, worked with ventilation because of its strong camphor volatiles.
identification: A broad evergreen whose glossy dark-green leaves smell strongly of camphor when crushed, the surest ID feature; leaves are alternate, ovate, 4-10 cm, glaucous (pale) beneath, with a slightly wavy margin; bark is grayish-brown and fissured, and it bears small green fruits that turn black. The camphor smell distinguishes it from most other broadleaf evergreens. It is reported toxic to humans (and to dogs and cats) in quantity, so use it strictly as a dye plant. Note taxonomy: recent treatments move it from Cinnamomum to Camphora officinarum, so both names appear in current sources.
habitat: Naturalized and invasive across Florida (and parts of the Gulf/Southeast and California), forming near-monotypic thickets in disturbed and natural areas; it was introduced as an ornamental in the 1800s. Foliage and leaf litter are available year-round.
craftUse: Mature/old leaves and fallen brown leaf litter are simmered for an aged-leaf brown dye; because the tree resprouts vigorously, cutting stems and stripping foliage supports invasive control. Work in good ventilation or outdoors, the volatile camphor released while cutting and simmering is an irritant, and never ingest any part.
safetyNote: Strong camphor volatiles, ventilate or work outdoors; reported toxic to people and pets if ingested.

sources.title: Camphor Tree (Cinnamomum camphora), UF/IFAS Gardening Solutions (invasive status)
Camphora officinarum (camphor tree), Florida Natural Areas Inventory, Invasive Species

summary: Canaigre (Rumex hymenosepalus), also called tanner's dock or wild rhubarb, is a Southwest desert dock whose tannin-rich tubers give a warm brown and act as their own mordant. On this map it is a self-mordanting brown dye root, dug with care to leave the colony intact.
identification: A perennial dock with a basal rosette of large, somewhat fleshy, wavy-margined leaves resembling garden rhubarb, tall reddish-pink flower/seed stalks, and a cluster of brown tuber-like roots below. Because the leaf resembles garden rhubarb (Rheum) and the plant is called 'wild rhubarb,' beginners sometimes treat the leaf stalks as an edible rhubarb substitute, do not: like other docks it is high in oxalic acid. Canaigre's clustered tubers and reddish, papery three-winged seeds identify it. Dye use only, not food.
habitat: Sandy washes, roadsides, and open desert of the Southwest (Arizona, New Mexico, southern California, west Texas, Utah) and northern Mexico. Roots are dug in fall (roughly September-November); older, larger tubers carry the most tannin.
craftUse: The tannin-rich tubers (dried roots run roughly 35-60% tannin) are chopped and simmered for a warm brown-to-tan (yellows, greens, and grays are also achievable); the high tannin content means the dye is effectively self-mordanting, needing no separate metal mordant. Dig from a cluster, take only part, and replant the crown and remaining tubers so the colony regrows. Do not ingest, the tannin and oxalate content is high.
safetyNote: High in tannin and oxalate (leaves too), dye only, do not eat any part; it is not an edible rhubarb.

sources.title: Rumex hymenosepalus (canaigre), Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center Native Plant Database
Rumex hymenosepalus, PFAF Plant Database (35-60% root tannin; self-mordant dye)

summary: Cliffrose (Purshia stansburyana; formerly Cowania) is a slow-growing native shrub of the rose family whose leafy twig tips yield a documented Navajo yellow-to-gold/brown dye. On this map it is a light-harvest gold dye source that is also critical wildlife browse, so tips are clipped sparingly.
identification: An upright, often single-trunked evergreen shrub with small, deeply lobed, resinous, aromatic leaves and pale creamy flowers, followed by fruits bearing single feathery plumes longer than 1 cm. Distinguish it from Apache plume (Fallugia paradoxa, branched from the base, with several plumes clustered per fruit) and from antelope bitterbrush (Purshia tridentata, whose fruits lack the long feathery plume tail); note cliffrose and bitterbrush hybridize where they meet, blurring ID. No toxic lookalike; it is a dye plant, not human forage.
habitat: Rocky, often limestone slopes, cliffs, plateaus, and woodlands of the Southwest and Great Basin (Arizona, Utah, Nevada, New Mexico, Colorado, California). Leafy tips are clipped in the warm months (roughly May-August).
craftUse: Leafy twig tips are simmered, historically pounded and combined with juniper, to give a yellow-to-gold ('Navajo gold', shading toward brown) dye on wool. Clip only soft leafy tips, a few from each shrub and never a whole branch: cliffrose is principal mule deer and bighorn winter browse (the key browse on the Kaibab Plateau), a deep-rooted erosion binder on bare rock, and slow-growing. Dye use only.
safetyNote: Dye only. Clip tips lightly, it is essential winter browse and slow to regrow.

sources.title: Purshia stansburiana (Stansbury cliffrose), USDA Forest Service, Fire Effects Information System
Stansbury Cliffrose, Utah State University Extension, Range Plants

summary: St. John's wort / Klamath weed (Hypericum perforatum) is a Eurasian invasive of Western rangeland whose flowering tops give yellow dye and whose dark glands can yield a red. On this map it is an invasive-removal dye source, handled with gloves because of hypericin.
identification: An erect perennial with paired, stalkless leaves dotted with translucent 'perforations' (oil glands visible when held to light), and clusters of five-petaled bright-yellow flowers whose petals carry tiny black dots along the margins; crushing a flower bud releases a reddish stain. Several native Hypericum (e.g., H. punctatum) look similar, the perforate translucent leaf dots plus black-margined yellow petals point to H. perforatum, the invasive one. It contains hypericin, a photosensitizer: photodermatitis ('hypericism') is best documented from ingestion (notably in grazing livestock), but skin contact with the sap can cause photosensitivity in some people, so wear gloves and cover skin, and do not ingest.
habitat: Widely naturalized and invasive across Western rangeland, roadsides, and disturbed ground (Oregon, California, Washington, Montana, Nevada, Utah, Wyoming, and beyond). Flowering tops are harvested in summer (roughly June-August).
craftUse: Flowering tops give a yellow dyebath; the dark hypericin pigment in the black petal glands can be coaxed toward a red with the right handling, a notable extra for a yellow-flowered plant. Removing the seed-bearing tops of this invasive is ecologically positive, snip and bag the tops rather than scattering seed. Wear gloves and cover skin: hypericin is a photosensitizer, and this is dye-only, not for ingestion.
safetyNote: Contains hypericin, a photosensitizer, wear gloves, cover skin, avoid sun exposure of treated skin, and do not ingest.

sources.title: Hypericum perforatum profile, California Invasive Plant Council (Cal-IPC)
Common St. Johnswort (Hypericum perforatum) plant fact sheet, USDA NRCS

summary: Coyote brush (Baccharis pilularis) is an abundant, fast-resprouting evergreen shrub of California coastal scrub whose leaves and young tips give a yellow-to-khaki dye. On this map it is a resilient, light-harvest yellow dye source.
identification: A much-branched evergreen shrub to 4-8 ft with small, shiny, bright-green, resinous, often toothed wedge-shaped leaves and numerous small whitish-yellow flower heads (it is dioecious, separate male and female plants, females producing fluffy wind-borne seed). The sticky resinous foliage and dense rounded form are distinctive; there is no dangerous lookalike among the coastal shrubs. B. pilularis itself is not considered toxic, but it is low-palatability, avoided by livestock, and some other Baccharis species are toxic, so treat it as a dye plant, not forage.
habitat: Native to California, Oregon, Washington, and Baja California, dominating coastal bluffs, coastal sage scrub, chaparral, grasslands, and open woodland below about 2,000 ft. Leafy tips are harvested in late summer and fall (roughly August-November).
craftUse: Leaves and young branch tips are simmered for a yellow-to-khaki dyebath on wool and other fibers; iron shifts it grayer. Because coyote brush is ubiquitous and resprouts vigorously, a light tip-harvest from several plants has negligible impact. Dye use only.
safetyNote: Dye only, not forage, B. pilularis is low-palatability and some Baccharis species are toxic.

sources.title: Coyotebrush (Baccharis pilularis) plant guide, USDA NRCS
Baccharis pilularis (coyote brush), Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center

summary: Curly dock (Rumex crispus) is a weedy naturalized dock whose long yellow taproot gives a brown/yellow dye. On this map it is an invasive-removal dye root, digging it out doubles as weed control.
identification: A perennial with narrow lance-shaped leaves that have distinctly wavy, curled margins, a stout upright stem topped with dense clusters of small greenish flowers turning rusty-brown in seed, and a long, forking, yellow taproot (scrape it to see the yellow). It resembles broad-leaved dock (R. obtusifolius), which has wider, flatter, heart-based leaves, either dock root works similarly for dye, so the distinction is minor. The leaves are high in oxalic acid; use the root for dye, not the leaves as food.
habitat: Naturalized across essentially all of North America on roadsides, fields, ditches, pastures, and disturbed ground. Roots are dug in fall (roughly September-November) after the plant has stored reserves.
craftUse: The yellow taproot is chopped and simmered for a brown dye (yellows, greens, and grays are also achievable, and dock root is high enough in tannin to behave largely as a self-mordant). Levering out the whole forking taproot removes the weed at the same time, clip and bag the rusty seed stalks first so you don't scatter dock's long-lived seed. Use the root; the leaves are high in oxalic acid.
safetyNote: Leaves are high in oxalic acid, use the root for dye, not the leaves.

sources.title: Rumex crispus (curly / yellow dock), North Carolina Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox
Rumex crispus, Wikipedia (yellow taproot; brown/yellow dyes; oxalate content)

summary: Douglas-fir (Pseudotsuga menziesii) is a major western conifer whose fallen cones and shed bark give a peach-to-tan dye. On this map it is a waste-stream pigment, gathered from the forest floor and mill trimmings with zero impact on living trees.
identification: A tall conifer with soft, flat, single needles set all around the twig, and unmistakable hanging cones bearing three-pointed 'mouse-tail' bracts protruding between the scales, the clearest ID feature; cones ripen from green through yellow-pink to light brown. The dangerous confusion is Pacific yew (Taxus brevifolia), a small shade-understory tree with flat needles that superficially resemble a young conifer's; crucially, yew produces NO woody cones, instead it bears single seeds in soft red fleshy cups (arils), and its foliage, bark, and seeds are highly toxic (taxine alkaloids). Do not gather any flat-needled understory tree that lacks the three-bracted woody cones and bears red arils, that is yew. Douglas-fir's three-pronged cone bracts are also absent on true firs and spruces.
habitat: Dominant across the Pacific Northwest and mountain West in conifer forests, with a Rocky Mountain variety extending south and east. Fallen cones and shed bark accumulate on the forest floor year-round; the map's harvest window is fall into winter (roughly September-December).
craftUse: Fallen cones, shed bark, and mill/trimming waste are broken up and simmered for a peach-to-tan dye (the tannin-bearing bark leans browner). Gathering only dropped material and mill offcuts means zero impact on living trees, never strip bark from a standing tree, which can girdle and kill it. A clean, low-impact waste-stream dye.
safetyNote: Gather only fallen cones/bark and mill waste, never strip living bark. Do not gather red-arilled, cone-less yew (Taxus), which is highly toxic.

sources.title: Coast Douglas-fir, U.S. National Park Service
Douglas-fir | Pseudotsuga menziesii, The Morton Arboretum (three-bracted cones; bark)

summary: Eucalyptus is a genus of naturalized Australian gum trees, invasive along coastal California, whose leaves and shed bark are prized in natural dyeing for the uncommon rusts and reds they can yield. On this ink map it is a brown/rust-red dye gathered from litter and removal cuttings.
identification: Evergreen trees with aromatic, often blue-green or sickle-shaped leaves that smell strongly of eucalyptus oil when crushed, and bark that peels or sheds to litter the ground. Species that tend to throw redder color include red ironbark (E. sideroxylon) and silver-dollar gum (E. cinerea). There is no dangerous toxic tree lookalike, but do not confuse eucalyptus with the unrelated invasive camphor tree (Cinnamomum camphora), which smells of camphor/mothballs rather than eucalyptus oil. Reconcile with the existing safety tags: the leaves and oil are toxic if ingested, so this is strictly a dye plant.
habitat: Widely planted and naturalized across the contiguous US, most heavily in coastal and central California where several species have escaped cultivation; Cal-IPC flags them for fire risk and the deep leaf-and-bark litter mats they build. Leaf and shed-bark litter is available year-round, with the catalog favoring roughly May through October.
craftUse: The leaves and shed bark are simmered in water; because they are tannin-rich, they can bond to protein fibers (wool, silk) without a separate mordant, and the redder species can throw uncommon rust, brick, terracotta, and coral tones alongside more usual browns (a long, hot bath or steam is usually needed to develop the reds). This is an intermediate dye because the color is genuinely unpredictable, swinging with species, season, and water chemistry; the brighter reds and yellows are the most fugitive while the tannin browns hold up best. Because the trees are invasive, working fallen litter and sanctioned removal cuttings is a low-impact way to get color. Ventilate when simmering, since the volatile oils are irritating and the dust of dried leaves is best not inhaled.
safetyNote: Leaves and eucalyptus oil are toxic if swallowed; this is a dye only, and you should ventilate the space when simmering.

sources.title: Cal-IPC, Eucalyptus globulus (invasive plant profile)
USDA Forest Service, Eucalyptus globulus, Fire Effects Information System

summary: Greenthread (Thelesperma megapotamicum), also called Navajo or Hopi tea, is a wiry aster-family perennial of the Southwest whose whole flowering plant gives warm yellows to oranges. It carries a long living Dine, Hopi, and Pueblo history as both a dye and a beverage; on this map it is treated as a dye.
identification: A slender, near-leafless perennial 8 to 30 inches tall with smooth wiry stems and threadlike, pinnately divided leaves, topped by thumbnail-sized yellow-to-orange flower heads sitting in a cuplike involucre whose phyllaries have purple-tinged, white-edged lobes. It resembles other Thelesperma greenthreads (such as T. filifolium) and small yellow desert composites, but has no toxic lookalike that poses ingestion danger; distinguish it by the wiry, sparsely leafy stems and cuplike involucre. The existing tag notes it is a traditional beverage but is presented here strictly as a dye.
habitat: Grows across the southern Great Plains and Colorado Plateau, abundant on Navajo, Hopi, and Pueblo lands and throughout New Mexico, Arizona, west Texas, and Colorado, reaching north into Wyoming, Nebraska, and South Dakota. It favors sandy or clay soils in prairie, rocky slopes, roadsides, and disturbed ground from about 1,000 to 9,500 feet, flowering roughly June through September. Because it readily takes up metals from soil, avoid gathering along heavy-traffic roadsides or near old mining ground.
craftUse: The dried whole flowering plant is simmered to give warm yellows that shift to bright orange under an alkaline dip and toward olive with iron. Because the plant is mostly stem with little leaf, a generous armful is needed for strong color, but it reseeds readily and cutting a few inches above the soil (the traditional tea-harvest method) lets the perennial crown regrow, a genuinely light harvest. This is a beginner-friendly, reliable Southwest yellow.
safetyNote: No notable hazard as a dye; harvest away from busy roadsides and mining ground, and honor its living Indigenous history rather than treating it as a novelty.

sources.title: Southwest Desert Flora, Thelesperma megapotamicum, Hopi Tea Greenthread
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center, Thelesperma megapotamicum

summary: Honey mesquite (Neltuma glandulosa, formerly Prosopis glandulosa) is a thorny legume tree of the arid Southwest whose shed bark and dark exuded gum give brown-to-black dyes. On this map it is a brown/black dye worked entirely from fallen and shed material.
identification: A spreading, often multi-stemmed tree or large shrub with rough dark bark, sharp straight thorns, feathery bipinnate leaves, cream catkin flowers, and long constricted seed pods; dark gum weeps from old wounds. It can be confused with other mesquites (honey mesquite is distinguished by its longer leaflets and single pair of pinnae) and with thorny acacias, but none of these are dangerously toxic to the dyer, the real hazard is the sharp thorns, whose puncture wounds can become infected. Consistent with the existing tags: the thorns cause injury and the tannin-rich sap stains skin.
habitat: Native across the southern US from Texas and Oklahoma west through New Mexico, Arizona, and into southern California, dominating rangeland, washes, and disturbed brush country where much of South Texas treats it as an aggressive nuisance. Shed bark, fallen limbs, and exuded gum are available spring through early fall.
craftUse: The bark is a good source of tannins and the near-black gum is pigment-rich; simmered and strained, fallen bark and gum give warm tan-to-brown on wool, and meeting that tannin with iron water strikes a deep grey-black via the same iron-tannate chemistry behind iron-gall ink. It is a beginner-friendly, substantive dye that holds moderately well. Critically, you never wound a living tree: the shed bark, fallen limbs, and naturally exuded gum lying beneath any mesquite are dye enough.
safetyNote: Watch the sharp thorns while gathering (puncture wounds can become infected), and expect the tannin-rich sap to stain skin.

sources.title: USDA NRCS Plant Guide, Honey Mesquite (Prosopis glandulosa)
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center, Prosopis glandulosa (Honey mesquite)

summary: Manzanita (Arctostaphylos) is a genus of chaparral shrubs known for smooth red bark that peels in thin curls each summer. Its shed bark and fallen leaves give rosy-browns; on this map it is a brown dye gleaned only from material the plant has already dropped.
identification: Evergreen shrubs with crooked, muscular limbs, smooth mahogany-red bark that peels in tight curls in early summer, leathery gray-green leaves, and small urn-shaped pink-white flowers followed by berry-like fruit. The genus is listed at genus level because manzanitas range from common chaparral workhorses to extremely narrow endemics, several California species are threatened or endangered (for example Presidio/Raven's manzanita, Arctostaphylos montana ssp. ravenii, is one of the rarest plants in the mainland US, and Vine Hill manzanita, A. densiflora, survives as a tiny wild population). There is no toxic lookalike hazard for the dyer; the safety concern is conservation. Consistent with the existing tag: use shed and fallen material of common species only, never dig or cut whole plants, and if you cannot confidently identify a shrub as a common species, do not gather from it.
habitat: Concentrated in California (where the great majority of species occur) and extending through the West Coast and Southwest chaparral, coast ranges, and low-elevation conifer forests. Shed bark curls and fallen leaves accumulate through summer, with the catalog window roughly June through September.
craftUse: The bark curls and leathery fallen leaves are tannin-rich and bind directly onto wool and cotton without a separate mordant, giving warm tans, fawns, and rosy browns. It is a beginner-friendly, substantive, forgiving dye of moderate lightfastness, drawn entirely from gleaned material. Because a good number of manzanitas are rare endemics, treat the genus as a gift to glean rather than to cut, and take only shed bark and fallen leaves of clearly common species.
safetyNote: Never dig or cut whole plants, some manzanitas are rare or endangered endemics; use only shed bark and fallen leaves of species you can confidently identify as common.

sources.title: Calscape (California Native Plant Society), Arctostaphylos (Manzanita)
