summary: One-seed juniper (Juniperus monosperma) is an abundant, slow-growing juniper of the Southwest pinyon-juniper country. Its shed bark, twigs, and berries give tan and brown, and its green-needle ash is a traditional Navajo alkaline mordant. On this map it is a brown dye plus an ash mordant.
identification: A shrubby, often multi-trunked juniper with stringy shredding bark, scale-like blue-green foliage, and small single-seeded, waxy blue-green female cones (the single seed per cone is diagnostic). Crucially, do NOT substitute the toxic ornamental savin juniper (Juniperus sabina), whose foliage and berries are rich in sabinyl acetate and related compounds and are genuinely poisonous (savin oil is a documented abortifacient and can cause severe gastrointestinal and kidney injury); one-seed juniper is simply unpalatable rather than acutely toxic, but its resinous foliage should still never be eaten. Also do not confuse the ash-mordant step with burning savin foliage, savin smoke and oil are hazardous. Consistent with the existing tags: this is a dye only, do not ingest, and confirm it is not savin juniper.
habitat: Dominant across the Colorado Plateau and Southwest, New Mexico, Arizona, and adjacent Colorado, Utah, and west Texas, in pinyon-juniper woodland on dry, rocky, harsh country, much of it tribal land, national forest, or BLM ground with its own collecting rules. Shed bark, twigs, and ripe cones are gathered in fall, roughly September through November.
craftUse: Shed bark, dropped twigs, and fallen cones are simmered to give tan and brown of moderate lightfastness on wool, deepening with an iron dip; the berry-derived colors are the most fugitive. Separately, the ash of burned green needles is a traditional Dine (Navajo) alkaline mordant that shifts and fixes color. Favor fallen material, stringy shed bark, dropped twigs, and fallen cones, over stripping or cutting living trees, since the species is abundant but slow to recover in hard country.
safetyNote: Dye only, never ingest, and be certain you have one-seed juniper, not the toxic ornamental savin juniper (J. sabina), whose oil is a documented abortifacient.

sources.title: USDA Forest Service, Juniperus monosperma, Fire Effects Information System
NC State Extension, Juniperus monosperma (One-seed Juniper)

summary: Pacific madrone (Arbutus menziesii) is a West Coast broadleaf evergreen that sheds thin sheets of cinnamon-red bark every summer. That shed bark gives soft pink-to-peach browns; on this map it is a brown dye gathered entirely off the ground.
identification: A tree with smooth, sinuous limbs whose upper reddish-orange bark peels each summer in thin papery sheets to reveal cool greenish new bark, plus leathery oval evergreen leaves, urn-shaped white flowers, and clusters of orange-red berries. It is distinguished from Arizona madrone (Arbutus arizonica), a Sky Islands species with gray checkered trunks, by its dramatic red peeling bark and coastal range, and from the ornamental strawberry tree (A. unedo) by its larger size. No toxic lookalike endangers the dyer; the concern is the tree's fragility. Consistent with the existing tag: use naturally shed bark sheets only.
habitat: A coastal-belt tree from southern British Columbia south through Washington, Oregon, and California, typically within about 100 miles of the Pacific and up to roughly 5,000 feet in California. Shed bark sheets litter the forest floor from mid-summer until the autumn rains, the catalog window being July through September.
craftUse: The tannin-rich shed bark is simmered to give soft peach-to-pink-brown tones on protein fibers, an unusual warm pink from a tree dye, and beginner-friendly. Lightfastness is moderate; an alum mordant noticeably improves it, and the warm pinks still soften toward brown over prolonged light. Because madrone is famously touchy about disturbance and prone to root rot, never strip living bark, gather only the sheets the tree has already sloughed onto the ground.
safetyNote: Collect only naturally shed bark off the ground; stripping living bark can harm this root-rot-sensitive tree.

sources.title: Washington State University, About Pacific Madrone
USDA Forest Service, Arbutus menziesii, Fire Effects Information System

summary: Plains coreopsis (Coreopsis tinctoria), or dyer's tickseed, is a heavy-reseeding annual wildflower whose flower heads give some of the most reliable gold-to-orange color a beginner can pull from a plant. Its species name 'tinctoria' means 'used for dyeing.' On this map it is a yellow/orange dye.
identification: An annual up to about three feet tall with slender stems, finely divided leaves, and abundant daisy-like flower heads of yellow rays with a distinctive maroon or red-brown eye at the base. It reads clearly as a garden and roadside tickseed; there is no dangerous toxic lookalike. The catalog note directs you to use this common species rather than the range-restricted giant coreopsis (Coreopsis gigantea, also placed in Leptosyne) of the California coast. Consistent with the existing tag: it is a dye only.
habitat: Native and widely naturalized across much of the contiguous US, thriving in disturbed ground, roadsides, ditches, moist prairies, and cultivated patches. Flower heads are harvested in bloom, roughly June through September.
craftUse: The blossoms are rich in flavonoids and related pigments that give saturated gold-to-orange color, deepening to rust and bronze on wool and silk, or shifting to warm red-brown with iron. It is documented as a dye plant used by several Indigenous nations, including the Zuni and other Southwestern peoples, who worked the blossoms for reds and other colors distinct from the alum-yellow shown here. As a heavy self-seeder of disturbed ground, flower harvest and garden patches are genuinely low-impact.
safetyNote: No notable hazard; use plains coreopsis rather than the range-restricted giant coreopsis.

sources.title: USDA PLANTS Database, Coreopsis tinctoria
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center, Coreopsis tinctoria

summary: Alder (Alnus rubra and A. incana) is a fast-growing pioneer tree whose inner bark gives orange-red to deep red-brown dyes. On this map it is a brown dye taken from the bark of fallen limbs and coppice prunings.
identification: Streamside and disturbance-loving trees with smooth gray bark, doubly toothed leaves, and small woody cone-like female catkins that persist through winter; red alder's cut inner bark flushes rusty orange within minutes as it oxidizes. Red alder (A. rubra) is a West Coast species; thinleaf/mountain alder (A. incana ssp. tenuifolia) grows in the Rockies and Sierra. No toxic lookalike threatens the dyer. Consistent with the existing tag: it is a dye only.
habitat: Red alder is one of the fastest pioneer trees on the West Coast, a nitrogen-fixing colonizer of road-cuts, slides, and clearings from British Columbia south through Washington, Oregon, and northern California; mountain alder occupies streambanks in the interior mountains. Bark from fallen limbs and prunings is available through the growing season, roughly April through September.
craftUse: The tannin- and phenol-rich inner bark, which reddens on exposure to air, is simmered to dye wool, basketry, and hide gold through orange-red to deep red-brown. Coast Salish and other Northwest nations worked red alder bark as a source of red-brown color for fishnets, cedar bark, paddles, and regalia, and interior peoples used mountain alder to color hides; that knowledge is theirs and is credited here. Because both alders coppice vigorously, a forager working fallen limbs and prunings takes from a tree built to regrow. It is a beginner-friendly, substantive dye.
safetyNote: No notable hazard; take bark from fallen limbs and prunings rather than live trunks.

sources.title: USDA Forest Service, Alnus rubra, Fire Effects Information System
USDA NRCS Plant Guide, Red Alder (Alnus rubra)

summary: Rabbitbrush (Ericameria nauseosa and Chrysothamnus viscidiflorus) is a staggeringly abundant high-desert shrub whose late-summer flowering tops give the Southwest's most sustainable bright yellow. On this map it is a yellow dye, a well-documented Hopi and Navajo color.
identification: Rounded shrubs blazing gold in late summer. Rubber rabbitbrush (E. nauseosa) grows 2 to 7 feet with whitish, felt-matted flexible stems and narrow gray-green non-sticky leaves; green rabbitbrush (C. viscidiflorus) stays under 3 feet with bright green, shiny, sticky foliage, an easy field split. Both grow with sagebrush, which is grayer and differently aromatic; there is no dangerous toxic lookalike, though the existing tag flags possible contact sensitivity for some people. Consistent with the tags: possible contact sensitivity, dye only.
habitat: Blankets the high-desert roadsides, plains, valley bottoms, and foothills of the Great Basin and Southwest, Arizona, New Mexico, Nevada, Utah, and beyond, in sagebrush, pinyon-juniper, and bunchgrass communities on dry sandy or clay soils. Flowering tops are gathered at peak bloom, roughly August through October.
craftUse: The flavonoid-rich flowering tops give a clear lemon-to-gold yellow on handspun wool, with twigs and a switch of mordant pushing the bath toward deep gold and even olive green. It is one of the best-documented dye plants of the Southwest, long used by Dine (Navajo) and Hopi dyers, and is beginner-friendly with moderate lightfastness. Because you snip only the flowering tops and leave the whole shrub standing, and the plant is enormously abundant, it is among the most low-impact yellows a dyer can gather in arid North America.
safetyNote: Some people report contact sensitivity to rabbitbrush; handle with care, and use it as a dye only.

sources.title: USDA Forest Service, Ericameria nauseosa (Rubber Rabbitbrush)
USDA NRCS Plant Guide, Rubber Rabbitbrush (Ericameria nauseosa)

summary: Scotch broom (Cytisus scoparius) is a top-priority invasive shrub of the West Coast whose blossoms give a soft, buttery yellow. On this map it is a yellow dye, and cutting it for the dyepot supports weed control. It carries toxic alkaloids and must never be ingested.
identification: A wiry, evergreen-stemmed leguminous shrub with green angular stems, small trifoliate leaves, and abundant pea-shaped bright-yellow flowers in spring, followed by hairy-edged pods that snap open to fling seed. It resembles other brooms and gorse (gorse is fiercely spiny; broom is not). The hazard is not a lookalike but the plant itself: seeds and foliage carry quinolizidine alkaloids (including sparteine) that are toxic if eaten. Consistent with the existing tag: alkaloids, do not ingest.
habitat: A Cal-IPC 'High'-impact invasive and, in Washington, a Class B noxious weed on the state quarantine list (sale, transport, and distribution prohibited). It carpets clearcuts, roadsides, and disturbed ground from British Columbia through Washington and Oregon into the northern California coast; single shrubs throw thousands of long-lived seeds per season, and the seedbank can persist for decades. Flowers are harvested at peak bloom, roughly April through June.
craftUse: Stripped blossoms give a soft, buttery flavonoid (genistein-type) yellow on alum-mordanted wool, and tossing in the green flowering tips pulls the bath toward olive. It is not the highly stable luteolin yellow of true dyer's broom (Genista tinctoria), so it is gentler and less permanent, an intermediate dye whose clear yellows want shade rather than a sunny window, while iron-saddened olives are more stable. Cutting it is genuinely useful invasive control that draws down the seedbank; bag and dispose of any seedpods carefully so you do not spread it. Wear gloves and avoid inhaling the fine dust of dried material, given the alkaloid content.
safetyNote: Seeds and foliage contain toxic alkaloids, never ingest any part; this is strictly a dye.

sources.title: Cal-IPC, Cytisus scoparius (Scotch broom) plant profile
Washington State Noxious Weed Control Board, Scotch broom (Cytisus scoparius)

summary: Southern wax myrtle (Morella cerifera) is a tough, fast-resprouting evergreen shrub of the Southeast whose leaves give a pale, lemony gold that shifts to gray-green with iron. On this map it is a yellow dye; its waxy berries are set aside for candle wax.
identification: An evergreen shrub or small tree with narrow, aromatic olive-green leaves that release a warm, resinous bay scent when crushed, and clusters of small powder-blue waxy berries on female plants. It is distinguished from northern bayberry (Morella pensylvanica), a more cold-hardy, deciduous-to-semi-evergreen northeastern relative with shorter, wider leaves and larger fruit; the two overlap in the mid-Atlantic. There is no dangerous toxic lookalike. Consistent with the existing tag: it is a dye only.
habitat: Native to the coastal Southeast, roughly New Jersey south to Florida and west to Texas (hardy in zones 7 to 11), thriving on hammock edges, pine flatwoods, wetland margins, and disturbed ground. Leaves are harvested through the growing season, roughly May through September.
craftUse: The leaves' tannins and other pigments give a soft, lemony gold on alum-mordanted wool that saddens quickly to a quiet, sage-toned gray-green when dropped into iron water. It is a beginner-friendly Southeast yellow, though on the fugitive end, it softens and grays with sun and washing, so alum mordanting and an iron afterbath help both light- and washfastness. The plant resprouts fast and shrugs off light leaf harvest. Save the waxy berries for rendering bayberry candle wax, not the dyepot.
safetyNote: No notable hazard as a dye; keep the leaf dye separate from any berry-wax use.

sources.title: Clemson Cooperative Extension, Waxmyrtle (Morella cerifera)
NC State Extension, Myrica cerifera (Southern Wax Myrtle)

summary: Spanish moss (Tillandsia usneoides) is not a moss but a rootless epiphytic bromeliad draping live oaks and cypress across the Gulf South. Storm-fallen strands gathered off the ground give a quiet tan-to-soft-brown; on this map it is a low-impact brown dye.
identification: Long, gray-green threadlike festoons of curling, scale-covered strands hanging from tree limbs, with tiny inconspicuous flowers, a bromeliad, a cousin of the pineapple, that lives on rain, dust, and air rather than as a parasite. It is superficially like the unrelated lichen old-man's-beard (Usnea), but its habitat and bromeliad structure are distinct; there is no toxic lookalike. The gathering hazard, per the existing tag, is the arthropods and debris that fallen strands can pick up.
habitat: Ranges across the coastal Gulf South and Southeast, Texas to Florida and up the Atlantic coast, draping live oaks and bald cypress in warm, humid lowlands. Because every windstorm drops fresh festoons, storm-fallen strands can be gathered off the ground most of the year, roughly March through November.
craftUse: The strands carry enough tannin to give wool a quiet, regional tan-to-soft-brown of moderate lightfastness. This is a near-zero-impact, beginner-friendly dye, you gather only wind-fallen material off the ground and never strip a living tree. The catch is in the gathering: strands lying on the ground can carry mites, spiders, ants, and woodland debris, so the moss needs a thorough rinse, and boiling or drying it before use both cleans it and, incidentally, deters pests. (Contrary to a widespread belief, well-documented studies have not found chiggers living in tree-borne Spanish moss; the real reason to rinse is general debris and ground-picked arthropods.)
safetyNote: Rinse thoroughly and boil or fully dry before use, fallen Spanish moss can carry mites, ants, and woodland debris; the common 'chiggers in the moss' claim is largely a myth, but cleaning is still warranted.

sources.title: University of Florida IFAS Extension, Spanish Moss (Tillandsia usneoides)
Southeastern Naturalist, Spanish Moss, the Unfinished Chigger Story

summary: Toyon (Heteromeles arbutifolia), or California holly, is a chaparral shrub of the California coast whose leaves and bark give warm red-browns. On this map it is an advanced red-brown dye that should be simmered outdoors or under strong ventilation, because as a rose-family plant its heated bath can release small amounts of cyanide.
identification: An evergreen shrub or small tree with thick, glossy, leathery dark-green leaves that are sharply toothed, clusters of small white flowers, and bright red berries in winter that inspired the 'California holly' name. It is distinct from true hollies (Ilex) and from the toothed-leaf shrubs it grows among; the danger is not a lookalike but its chemistry. Consistent with the existing tags: all parts contain cyanogenic glycosides, so heating the crushed plant material can liberate hydrogen cyanide and the plant must not be ingested.
habitat: Native to California's coastal hills, canyons, and chaparral (extending into Baja and the Sierra foothills), abundant and resprouting strongly from a basal burl after cutting or fire. Leaves and bark are gathered roughly May through September.
craftUse: The leaves, bark, small branches, and fruit yield warm tans and rusty red-browns, and the tannin-rich bark helps the color bite without heavy mordanting; iron-shifted browns and grays are the most stable, while the rustier reds soften first in sun. This is treated as an advanced recipe because of the cyanide hazard: as a member of the rose family, its cyanogenic glycosides can release hydrogen cyanide when the crushed material is heated in the bath, so simmer it outdoors or under strong ventilation, never in a small enclosed kitchen, and avoid leaning over the steaming pot. Toyon resprouts vigorously, so a light tip harvest across several shrubs heals quickly.
safetyNote: Simmer outdoors or under strong ventilation, the heated bath can release hydrogen cyanide, and never ingest any part.

sources.title: Calscape (California Native Plant Society), Toyon (Heteromeles arbutifolia)
UC ANR, Heteromeles arbutifolia (Toyon), plant safety profile

summary: Wild fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) is a licorice-scented invasive of coastal California whose fronds, flowers, and seed heads give a clear luteolin yellow. On this map it is a yellow dye, and pulling it aids native habitat, but it must be confirmed by smell because it grows beside deadly poison hemlock.
identification: A tall perennial with feathery, threadlike bright-green fronds, flat-topped clusters of small yellow flowers, and licorice-scented seed heads; every part smells strongly and sweetly of anise when crushed. THE CRITICAL LOOKALIKE is poison hemlock (Conium maculatum), which is deadly toxic and grows shoulder-to-shoulder with fennel: hemlock has WHITE flowers, smooth hairless stems with purple-red blotches or streaks, broader lacy triangular leaves, and a rank, musty, mouse-urine smell rather than sweet anise. A second dangerous lookalike in this family is poison water hemlock (Cicuta), also white-flowered and deadly. If the sweet licorice scent is absent, if the flowers are white, or if the stem is spotted, discard the plant immediately, and note that no yellow color is worth the risk of a misidentification, so leave it if you are unsure. Consistent with the existing tags: poison-hemlock lookalike, confirm ID; do not ingest.
habitat: A Cal-IPC-listed invasive that blankets coastal California hillsides, grassland, and coastal scrub (and naturalizes elsewhere in the US), crowding out native plants. Fronds, flowers, and seed heads are gathered roughly June through October.
craftUse: The flavonoids in the plant, chiefly luteolin, give a clear lemon-to-olive yellow on alum-mordanted wool and cotton, pushing toward forest green and dark khaki with a splash of iron. It is an intermediate dye of moderate lightfastness, and pulling it for the dyepot does real ecological good by removing an invasive. The one non-negotiable is identity: confirm the strong sweet anise smell, the yellow flowers, and an unspotted stem before any material goes in the pot, because of the poison-hemlock and water-hemlock hazard. Wear gloves when handling unfamiliar umbellifers.
safetyNote: Confirm the sweet anise smell, yellow flowers, and an unspotted stem before use, deadly poison hemlock (and water hemlock) are close lookalikes; if in doubt, discard, and never ingest the dye material.

sources.title: Cal-IPC, Foeniculum vulgare (fennel) plant profile
USDA Forest Service, Conium maculatum (poison hemlock), Fire Effects Information System

summary: Dyer's woad (Isatis tinctoria) is a European mustard whose leaves yield an indigo-type blue built on the same indigotin molecule as true indigo, a genuinely lightfast plant color, though woad leaves hold far less pigment than tropical indigo. On this map it is a removal-only species: an aggressive noxious weed harvested strictly to help eradicate stands, never planted or propagated.
identification: A biennial mustard (Brassicaceae): first-year plants form a basal rosette of blue-green leaves with a distinctive pale/whitish midrib; in the second year it bolts to a 2-4 ft stalk topped with clouds of small four-petaled yellow flowers, followed by pendant, teardrop- to tongue-shaped, purple-black seed pods (each holding a single seed). Because it is a mustard, it has many native and non-native lookalikes, wild mustards, other Isatis, and the young rosettes resemble several other roadside crucifers, so confirm ID before pulling, both to get the dye and to avoid mistakenly removing native mustards. It is not confused with any deadly plant; the mapped occurrence is never harvest permission, and pulling is only appropriate where noxious-weed removal is actually allowed.
habitat: A serious rangeland invader across the Intermountain and mountain West, Utah is the epicenter, with major infestations through Idaho, Nevada, Montana, Wyoming, Oregon, Washington, and northern California; it is a California 'B'-listed noxious weed worst in the far north (Siskiyou, Modoc, Shasta) and through the Klamath, Cascade, and Modoc country, spreading into dry rangeland, foothills, roadsides, and disturbed ground. Harvest window is spring into early summer (roughly April-June): take first-year rosette leaves before plants bolt, since bolted plants divert energy into the seed heads that must be bagged.
craftUse: The leaves hold a colorless precursor that oxidizes in air to indigotin (indigo blue). Leaves are steeped in hot (not boiling) water, the strained liquid is alkalized (soda ash) and aerated until it shifts from yellow-green to blue, and the pigment is settled out; the blue is then applied from a reduced vat, the caustic, oxygen-free step. Yield is honestly low (woad gives on the order of a few grams of pigment per kilogram of fresh leaves, well below tropical indigo), which is exactly why eradication harvests suit it: the infestation supplies plant material for free. Dye/pigment use only, with dedicated non-food tools.
safetyNote: Removal-only noxious weed and craft dye, not food: never plant, transplant, or propagate it; bag all seed heads for the trash (never compost); and treat the reducing vat as hazardous, caustic alkali (soda ash/lye) plus a reducing agent. If using sodium hydrosulfite (thiourea dioxide is a milder alternative), work with good ventilation for the sulfur fumes, keep it dry, and never mix it with acids.

sources.title: Cal-IPC, Isatis tinctoria (dyer's woad) profile
UC IPM, Dyer's Woad pest note (Pub. 74175)

summary: Alderleaf/true mountain mahogany (Cercocarpus montanus) is a slow-growing shrub of Southwestern and interior-West foothills whose inner bark gives a deep reddish-brown, one of the oldest regional dye colors, central to Navajo and Pueblo handspun wool, moccasins, and basketry. On this map only bark from pruned or fallen branches is used; the root is left in the ground.
identification: A dense, slow-growing shrub of dry foothills with small, wedge-shaped, toothed, deciduous leaves clustered on short spur shoots and, in fruit, a striking long feathery-plumed seed tail that twists like a corkscrew. Note the naming: the closely related curl-leaf mountain mahogany (C. ledifolius) is a separate evergreen species with narrow, rolled, untoothed leaves, the two share the plumed fruit but are distinct plants, so confirm you have C. montanus. There is no deadly lookalike, but the foliage of Cercocarpus is cyanogenic (it releases hydrogen cyanide, and cut, wilted, or drying leaves are the most dangerous), do not chew, ingest, or steep the leaves. Take bark only from cut, pruned, or fallen branch wood; do not dig the taproot, which usually kills this erratically seeding, slow-growing shrub.
habitat: Grows across the interior and Southwestern US in pinyon-juniper foothills, dry rocky slopes, canyons, and mountain shrublands (roughly the Great Basin, southern Rockies, and Colorado Plateau). Bark for dye is gathered from prunings or fallen/fresh-cut branches; traditional root-bark digging was done in fall, but the low-impact approach here gathers branch bark over time rather than harvesting one plant heavily.
craftUse: The reddish inner bark is simmered, traditionally in juniper-ash (alkaline) water, to pull a russet-to-reddish brown on wool and other protein fibers; an optional iron modifier shifts it darker. Color comes from tannins and condensed pigments, making it moderately durable, steadier than most berry or flower dyes, but expect gradual warming and lightening in strong sun, so don't oversell it as permanent. Plan on roughly equal dry weights of bark and fiber for a medium brown. This is a culturally significant dye; present any work with credit to the Navajo (Diné) and Pueblo dyers who carry the knowledge.
safetyNote: Foliage is cyanogenic, do not ingest, chew, or steep any leaf material; keep the dye to bark only and use dedicated non-food tools. Harvest bark only from pruned or fallen branches; never dig the taproot, which kills the slow-growing shrub.

Colorado State University, Guide to Poisonous Plants: mountain mahogany

summary: Carolina redroot (Lachnanthes caroliniana) is a Southeastern coastal-plain wetland perennial whose rhizomes hold a vivid orange-red, a documented Indigenous red dye of the Southeast. It is listed here as observe-only: the color lives in the root, the plant is regarded as toxic to graze, and it grows in fragile bog habitat, so harvest is off the table.
identification: A bloodwort-family (Haemodoraceae) perennial of wet places with narrow, iris-like basal leaves and a woolly, branched flower cluster of small yellowish blooms in summer; the diagnostic feature is the rhizome, which is stained deep red-orange (the color of dried blood) when broken open. It is regarded as toxic to livestock and is not a food plant, its pigments have been linked to photosensitization (phototoxic skin/organ reactions after sun exposure) in grazing animals, so it is never a casual material. No safe-vs-deadly foraging confusion applies here because the plant is observe-only: the correct action is a photograph and a record, not a dig.
habitat: A wetland obligate of the Southeastern coastal plain, bogs, wet pine savannas, pond and pool margins, seepage areas, and lakeshores. It becomes less common and is listed as threatened, endangered, or of special concern in several states toward the northern edge of its range. Flowering runs through summer (roughly June-September), but there is no harvest season here: the plant is mapped to be identified and honored, not gathered.
craftUse: Historically the red rhizome pigment dyed textiles and craft materials for Native peoples of the Southeastern coastal plain. Crucially, botanical references note the actual method of use for the dye has been lost, and there is no modern lightfastness testing, so any color should be assumed fugitive, there is no reliable, repeatable, low-impact way to get lasting color from it. Digging the root kills the plant and tears open slow-healing wetland habitat. For an actual usable red-brown, the map points to abundant fallen-material substitutes (e.g. one-seed juniper bark) instead.
safetyNote: Regarded as toxic to grazing livestock (photosensitizing root pigments), do not ingest the root or any part. Observe only: do not dig or harvest this habitat-sensitive wetland plant, which is protected in parts of its range.

sources.title: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center, Lachnanthes caroliniana
COSEWIC assessment, Redroot (Lachnanthes caroliniana)
Florida Wildflower Foundation, Lachnanthes caroliniana

summary: Tanoak (Notholithocarpus densiflorus) carries one of the highest bark-tannin loads of any West Coast tree; its bark steeps into a warm brown and, with iron, an iron-gall black. It is observe-only on this map: only already-dead or already-felled wood may be used, because Sudden Oak Death has killed the tree at massive scale and it is an Indigenous cultural keystone.
identification: An evergreen tree of the coastal forests with hard, leathery, sharply toothed leaves that are pale/woolly beneath when young, and acorns set in a distinctive bristly (spiny-scaled) cap, hence 'tanbark oak,' though it is not a true Quercus (it is the sole Notholithocarpus). It has no toxic dye lookalike, but the real hazard is regulatory and ecological, not a poisonous twin: tanoak is the species most susceptible to Sudden Oak Death (Phytophthora ramorum), so cutting or moving live tanoak risks spreading the pathogen. Use only wood that is already dead or has been felled for other reasons, and follow SOD quarantine rules on moving bark, wood, and even soil.
habitat: Native to the coastal forests of California and southwestern Oregon, in mixed evergreen and redwood/Douglas-fir stands. There is no true harvest season for a live tree here; bark for dye comes only from wood already dead or felled for other reasons, worked with attention to quarantine boundaries.
craftUse: Bark tannins give a warm, substantive brown on wool and silk at roughly 1:1 bark-to-fiber; met with iron they form iron-tannate and slide through gray to a deep iron-gall black, the same chemistry as centuries-old iron-gall manuscript ink, so the plain-tannin brown is moderately lightfast and the iron black is durable. The catch is the fiber, not the hue: iron-tannate is acidic, and iron accelerates acid hydrolysis of cellulose and protein, so heavily iron-dyed cloth grows brittle over time, go light on iron and neutralize after. Thickened with gum arabic, the liquor also behaves as a traditional brown-to-black drawing ink. Tanoak's industrial history was leather tanning; treat it as craft-only, with dedicated non-food tools, and honor its standing as a Karuk, Yurok, Hupa, and other Indigenous acorn staple.
safetyNote: Use only already-dead or already-felled wood, never cut a living tanoak, and follow Sudden Oak Death quarantine rules for moving bark, wood, and soil. Iron modifiers are staining and, used heavily, weaken the finished fiber over time.

sources.title: UC IPM, Sudden Oak Death pest notes
California Oak Mortality Task Force, SOD regulations
USDA Forest Service FEIS, Notholithocarpus densiflorus

summary: Western redcedar (Thuja plicata) is the 'tree of life' of the Pacific Northwest coast, a cultural cornerstone of the Coast Salish and other Northwest nations. Its reddish bark and tannins can stain wool a soft red-brown, but this map lists it observe-only, to honor the tree, not to teach stripping it.
identification: A large evergreen conifer with flat, scale-like sprays of foliage, fibrous stringy reddish-brown bark that peels in long vertical strips, and small woody cones; crushed foliage is aromatic. There is no poisonous dye lookalike, but two cautions apply: the foliage's essential oil is rich in thujones and related monoterpenoids, which are toxic if ingested (do not steep or ingest the foliage), and harvesting bark from a living tree can girdle and kill it. The species is also dying back across parts of its range as Pacific summers grow hotter and drier. Indigenous bark-pullers take narrow vertical strips with deep skill and ceremony, leaving living 'culturally modified trees', a practice that belongs to those communities and requires community consent.
habitat: Native to the moist forests of the Pacific Northwest coast and the inland wet belt, from northern California up through the coastal PNW and into the northern Rockies. No harvest season is given here because nothing is taken: the tree is mapped to be recognized and respected.
craftUse: The reddish bark and warm tannins can dye wool a soft red-brown to tan, but the color is generally fugitive, early observers noted dye colors made by Northwest peoples faded quickly, and this card gives no recipe to refine. Redcedar's true craft legacy is not dye at all: the soft inner bark is worked into rope, baskets, mats, capes, and the iconic watertight rain hat, and the rot-resistant wood into canoes, longhouses, and totem poles. For your own red-brown, the map routes you to more ethical substitutes such as alder or juniper bark rather than this tree.
safetyNote: Culturally significant and climate-stressed, observe only. Do not strip bark from living trees, and do not ingest or steep the thujone-rich foliage; bark for genuine cultural practice comes through community and consent, not a how-to.

sources.title: USDA Forest Service PNW, Western redcedar dieback
USDA Forest Service, Ethnobotany: dyes

summary: Wolf lichen (Letharia vulpina) is a vivid, electric chartreuse-yellow lichen of old conifers in the dry mountain West. Its color is vulpinic acid, a genuine poison, and it grows only a few millimeters a year, so this map lists it observe-only and points dyers to onion skin or weld instead.
identification: A shrubby, branching (fruticose) lichen forming shocking chartreuse-yellow tufts on the bark and dead wood of conifers; the neon color is diagnostic and looks almost synthetic. It is toxic, vulpinic acid was historically packed into carcasses to poison wolves and foxes, and it is poisonous to carnivores generally, so it is never a casual material. Its close relative L. columbiana looks similar but bears larger cup-shaped fruiting bodies; both are chartreuse lichens of the same country. The important 'lookalike' caution is ecological rather than a poisonous twin: this is an old-growth indicator that creeps outward perhaps a few millimeters a year and can live for centuries, and whole local stands have already been stripped for the dye pot and dried-flower trade.
habitat: Grows on old conifers in the dry, interior mountain forests of the western US, the montane and old-growth conifer country of the Rockies, Cascades, and interior West. It persists year-round, but there is no harvest season here: only wind-fallen branches would ever be considered, and the card's guidance is to observe and record, not collect.
craftUse: Historically the chartreuse extract dyed wool for Tlingit Chilkat dancing blankets and colored porcupine quills for Klamath, Karuk, Yurok, and other Indigenous basketry. Vulpinic acid does bond to wool and silk without a mordant, an unusual trait among natural dyes, but the plain boiling-water yellow most dyers get is only moderately lightfast and dulls under sustained sun. 'Better than fugitive berry reds' is not a reason to harvest a centuries-old lichen: abundant onion-skin or weld yellows give comparable or better fastness with none of the ecological cost, which is exactly why the map keeps this one on the tree.
safetyNote: Toxic (vulpinic acid), do not ingest, and note its historical use as a carnivore poison. Slow-growing old-growth indicator: observe only; use onion skin or weld for yellow instead.

sources.title: Montana Natural History Center, Wolf lichen
Letharia vulpina, overview (Wikipedia, with cited references)

summary: Dyer's polypore (Phaeolus schweinitzii) is the classic dye mushroom, a velvety, rust-brown annual bracket at the base of conifers that yields reliable yellows and golds on alum, with a striking green only from an iron modifier. It is a dye-only material on this map; it is not eaten.
identification: An annual, soft-when-fresh polypore forming rosettes or shelves at the base of, or on the roots of, living or dead conifers; fresh growing margins are bright yellow to orange with a velvety/fuzzy cap and yellow-green pores beneath, aging to a muddy rust- or dark-brown. It bruises brown. There is no deadly lookalike. The most common confusion is with young chicken of the woods (Laetiporus) because of the yellow-orange color, but chicken of the woods has a smooth, suede-like cap (never velvety or hairy), lacks the rusty-brown central zones, and does not grow from a central ground stem; the woolly velvet polypore (Inonotus tomentosus) is similar but smaller, thinner, and never has greenish pores. None of these are food, so identify carefully and treat all of them as dye/craft, not eating. Because it fruits annually, a map pin marks a past fruiting, not a standing harvest.
habitat: Widespread across the US on conifers (a common butt- and root-rot fungus of pines, Douglas-fir, spruce, and other softwoods), fruiting from the base of the trunk or from roots. Fresh fruiting bodies appear mainly in late summer through fall (roughly July-November); best color comes from bright-margined young specimens, though mature ones still give rich golds and browns.
craftUse: Fresh or dried fruiting bodies are simmered to extract pigment for alum-mordanted wool, giving bright golden-yellows to yellow-oranges; a full range of golds to burnt-oranges can come from a single pot depending on the mushroom's age and mordant. Its signature green appears only with an iron modifier, a separate, caustic, staining step, either in the mordant or as an after-dip, and copper pushes toward chocolate brown. A mordant is what makes the colors durable; unmordanted wool takes a paler, less fast yellow. Dye use only, with dedicated non-food tools.
safetyNote: Dye use only, do not eat (a tough, bitter, inedible wood bracket that causes digestive upset). The iron modifier that produces the green is caustic and staining; handle it with care. Annual fruiting means a pin is not a guaranteed standing harvest.

sources.title: North American Mycological Association, Best mushrooms for color
Fungus Fact Friday, Phaeolus schweinitzii

summary: Turkey tail (Trametes versicolor) is the easiest dye bracket to find, thin, banded, year-round shelves on dead hardwood. Its color is modest (tans and golds, greenish with iron), but its abundance makes it a friendly first fungal dye. Dye use only on this map; it is not eaten.
identification: Thin, flexible, fan-shaped brackets in overlapping clusters, zoned in concentric bands of varied color, with a finely fuzzy/velvety cap surface and, critically, a whitish pore surface (tiny pores) underneath, never gills. The key ID and lookalike check is the underside: false turkey tail (Stereum ostrea) has a smooth, leathery grey/yellowish underside with no pores; Trichaptum biforme has a toothed, purplish underside; Lenzites (Trametes) betulina has true gill-like ridges underneath. None of these lookalikes are deadly, but none are food either, so confirm the pored underside before harvesting for dye.
habitat: One of the most common wood-decay fungi across the US, on dead hardwood logs, stumps, and branches (occasionally conifer). It persists and is visible year-round, so it is genuinely mappable in any month.
craftUse: Fresh brackets are simmered for pigment on alum-mordanted wool, giving modest tans and golds; an iron modifier pushes the color toward greenish or rust. Be honest about the limit, turkey tail's palette is subtle compared with dyer's polypore, and its real advantage is reliability and abundance for practice. Dye use only, with dedicated non-food tools.
safetyNote: Dye use only, do not eat. Identify carefully: confirm the pored (non-gilled) underside to separate it from non-toxic but non-food lookalikes like false turkey tail (Stereum ostrea), Trichaptum biforme, and gilled Lenzites betulina.

sources.title: Real Mushrooms, Turkey tail vs false turkey tail
MushroomExpert.com, Trametes versicolor

summary: Artist's conk (Ganoderma applanatum) is a large perennial shelf fungus on dead or dying hardwood that gives warm browns in the dye pot. Because it is perennial and visible year-round, it is genuinely mappable. Dye use only on this map; it is woody and inedible regardless.
identification: A large, hard, perennial bracket (up to ~2 ft across) with a lumpy brown-to-grey upper crust and a flat white pore surface underneath that instantly and permanently bruises dark brown when scratched, the trait behind its name. Being perennial, it adds a new pore layer each year like stacked tree rings. The main lookalikes are the red-belted conk group (Fomitopsis mounceae complex), which shows a reddish/orange margin, and other Ganoderma species (e.g. G. sessile/lucidum group), which can be shinier or reddish. None are casual food, these similar conks are dye or medicinal brackets, so identify carefully before harvesting for color.
habitat: Common across the US on the trunks and stumps of dead and dying hardwoods (and occasionally conifers). As a perennial, the same fruiting body persists and is visible year-round, so it can be mapped in any month.
craftUse: Fresh or dried conk is simmered for pigment on alum-mordanted wool to give warm browns; an iron modifier deepens it toward grey-brown, and an ammonia solution has been reported by dyers to shift it toward rust. It is separately famous as a natural drawing surface, the white pore face is scratched to make lasting brown 'etchings,' the origin of the common name. Dye/craft use only, with dedicated non-food tools.
safetyNote: Dye use only, do not eat (woody and inedible). Identify carefully: similar conks (other Ganoderma, the red-belted Fomitopsis group) are likewise dye or medicinal brackets, not casual food.

sources.title: Minnesota Seasons, Artist's conk (Ganoderma applanatum)
MushroomExpert.com, Ganoderma applanatum

summary: Red-belted conk is the North American Fomitopsis mounceae complex, a perennial banded bracket with a red-orange margin on conifer and hardwood, giving a yellow/tan/brown dye palette. It is visible year-round, so it is mappable in any month. Dye use only; it is woody and not eaten.
identification: A hard, perennial, banded shelf with a shiny crust and a characteristic red-orange to reddish-brown margin above a pale (whitish to cream) pore surface, on dead conifer and hardwood. Taxonomic note that matters for the map: the true 'Fomitopsis pinicola' is a strictly Eurasian species and does not occur in North America, so this entry deliberately maps the North American complex (F. mounceae, F. ochracea, F. schrenkii), split from pinicola by DNA work published in 2019. There is no deadly lookalike, but it resembles artist's conk and other brown/red-margined brackets, all dye/medicinal rather than food, so identify carefully.
habitat: Occurs widely across northern and montane US forests as a perennial wood-decay bracket, favoring conifers (and aspen and other hardwoods) and causing a brown rot on dead and dying wood. The perennial conk persists and is visible year-round, so it can be mapped any month.
craftUse: Fresh or dried conk is simmered for pigment on alum-mordanted wool, giving a yellow/tan/brown range; an iron modifier shifts it toward grey or olive. As with the other wood brackets here, expect muted, earthy color rather than saturated hues. Dye use only, with dedicated non-food tools.
safetyNote: Dye use only, do not eat (woody). Identify carefully: it resembles artist's conk and other brown brackets, none of which are casual food.

sources.title: Fomitopsis mounceae, overview (Wikipedia, cited)
iNaturalist, Northern Red Belt (Fomitopsis mounceae)

summary: Tinder conk (Fomes fomentarius) is a hoof-shaped perennial bracket on hardwoods, especially birch and beech, that yields yellows, golds, and browns in the dye pot. It is visible year-round, so it is mappable in any month. Dye use only; it is woody, and famous instead as amadou/tinder.
identification: A hard, hoof-shaped (roughly triangular in profile) perennial conk, banded and ridged, silvery-grey to almost black but often brown, with a pore surface (no gills). It is most common on birch in the north and beech farther south. The important lookalike is Phellinus igniarius (also called 'false tinder fungus'), a similar birch-loving conk; the names overlap, so separate them by host, shape, and the rusty-brown flesh. There is no deadly lookalike here, but confirm ID before harvesting since these conks are craft/medicinal, not food.
habitat: Found across northern and temperate US forests on hardwoods, most often birch, then beech, and also maple, cherry, poplar, and others (rarely conifers). The perennial conk persists and is visible year-round, so it can be mapped any month.
craftUse: The fresh or dried conk, and its inner trama (flesh), are simmered for pigment on alum-mordanted wool, giving yellows, golds, and browns; iron pushes it browner or greyer. It is far better known for amadou: the inner flesh is soaked, cut, beaten, and stretched into a felt-like tinder that catches a spark and smolders for a long time, a historical fire-starting material (the 'Iceman,' Otzi, carried it). Dye/craft use only, with dedicated non-food tools.
safetyNote: Dye use only, do not eat (woody). Identify carefully; distinguish it from the similar birch-loving Phellinus igniarius ('false tinder fungus').

sources.title: Practical Self Reliance, Foraging & using tinder polypore (Fomes fomentarius)
Fungus Fact Friday, Fomes fomentarius

summary: Chicken of the woods (Laetiporus sulphureus / cincinnatus) is a bright orange-and-yellow shelf fungus listed here for its dye color, not as an eat-me signal. It gives oranges, yellows, and golds in the pot, though the bath can be surprisingly modest. It carries a genuinely toxic lookalike, so identification is safety-critical.
identification: Fleshy, soft, overlapping brackets in vivid sulphur-yellow to bright orange, growing in shelving clusters on wood, with a smooth (suede-like) cap and a pore surface underneath, no gills (L. cincinnatus tends to a pinkish-orange cap with a white pore surface and often grows from roots or the tree base). The safety-critical lookalike is the jack-o'-lantern (Omphalotus illudens/olearius): it is gilled (true, decurrent gills, not pored), grows in clustered tufts at the base of trees, stumps, or buried roots, and causes serious GI illness from illudin toxins. Reports of jack-o'-lantern's faint green glow in the dark are unreliable as a field test, do not depend on it. Laetiporus is always pored: check the underside, and if you see gills it is not chicken of the woods.
habitat: Common across the eastern and central US (and beyond) on hardwoods, oak is classic, with L. cincinnatus often at the base of, or from the roots of, oaks. Fruiting is annual, mainly summer into fall (roughly June-November), so a map pin marks a past fruiting, not a standing harvest.
craftUse: Fresh brackets are simmered for pigment on alum-mordanted wool, giving oranges, yellows, and golds; an iron modifier dulls the color toward olive/brown. Be honest about the limit: despite the vivid flesh, practitioners report the orange pigment can extract poorly, so the dye bath is sometimes surprisingly pale, results are less reliable than dyer's polypore. It is eaten by some foragers only when expertly identified and thoroughly cooked, but adverse reactions are fairly common (especially from brackets grown on conifer, eucalyptus, yew, or cedar); on this map it is framed for dye use, with dedicated non-food tools.
safetyNote: Framed here for dye use, not eating. Its toxic lookalike is the gilled jack-o'-lantern (Omphalotus illudens/olearius), which causes serious GI illness, confirm the pored (non-gilled) underside; do not rely on the unreliable 'glows in the dark' test. Even genuine chicken of the woods causes reactions in some people, especially from conifer/eucalyptus/yew/cedar hosts.

sources.title: Midwest American Mycological Information, Laetiporus sulphureus
North American Mycological Association, Best mushrooms for color

summary: Jewelweed (Impatiens capensis) is a native annual of moist, shady ground whose watery, succulent stems and leaves have a long history of topical folk use for skin irritation. On this map it sits in the 'topical' category and is flagged external-use-only, never to be ingested.
identification: A soft annual 2-5 ft tall with translucent, watery, somewhat swollen stems, alternate oval leaves with toothed margins, and orange flowers spotted reddish-brown that dangle on thin stalks (June-September). Ripe seed pods spring open when touched, the 'touch-me-not' trait. The closest lookalike is its cousin pale/yellow jewelweed (Impatiens pallida), which is also harmless but has yellow, less-spotted flowers. More important for safety: jewelweed very often grows right alongside poison ivy (Toxicodendron radicans), poison sumac in wetter sites, and stinging nettle in the same wet edges, learn to recognize those woody or stinging neighbors before you reach into a patch. Because jewelweed is easiest to confirm in flower, gather for use once the orange spotted blooms are present; young non-flowering plants are harder to distinguish from other seedlings.
habitat: Widespread across the eastern and central contiguous US in moist, shaded places: stream banks, ditches, wet woodland edges, seeps, and floodplains, often in large colonies. It is a wetland annual, so plants germinate in spring and are at their most usable in the summer flowering season (roughly June through September).
craftUse: In the medicine domain it is used strictly externally, as folk tradition describes: the crushed fresh stem and leaf juice, or a cooled water infusion of the aerial parts, is dabbed on skin after contact with poison ivy or other irritants. It does not keep, the fresh, watery tissue is the point, so it is a same-day, small-batch material rather than something to dry and store. This is educational, historical/traditional framing only; it makes no claim that it treats, prevents, or cures anything, and is not medical advice.
safetyNote: External use only; do not ingest (raw plant contains calcium oxalate and can irritate). Gather only from patches you can clearly distinguish from adjacent poison ivy, poison sumac, and nettle.

sources.title: USDA Forest Service, Jewelweed (Impatiens capensis), Plant of the Week
Native Plant Trust, Go Botany, Impatiens capensis (spotted touch-me-not)

summary: Broadleaf plantain (Plantago major) is a low, tough rosette weed of lawns, paths, and trail edges, a Eurasian introduction now common across the contiguous US. Its leaves are one of the most widely used traditional topical (poultice) materials, and on this map it is a topical entry.
identification: A ground-hugging basal rosette of broad, oval leaves with distinctive parallel veins that run leaf-tip to base, on grooved stalks; tiny greenish flowers rise on narrow spikes. Its most important toxic lookalike, especially before it flowers, is foxglove (Digitalis purpurea/lanata), a first-year rosette that is superficially plantain-like but has larger, soft-hairy, finely toothed leaves with net-like (NOT parallel) veins, and forms a tall bell-flower spike in its second year. Foxglove contains cardiac glycosides and is dangerous; never confuse the two. A milder lookalike is narrowleaf plantain (Plantago lanceolata), which is also traditionally used and harmless but has narrow lance-shaped leaves. The reliable tells for true plantain are flat, spreading rosette; smooth or only sparsely hairy leaves; and prominent PARALLEL veins.
habitat: Naturalized across essentially all of the contiguous US in disturbed, compacted ground: lawns, driveways, trail edges, waste places, and cart tracks (one old common name is 'cart track plant'). Leaves are available spring through fall (roughly April-October), most tender in the cooler parts of the season.
craftUse: In folk practice the fresh leaf is the material: bruised or chewed and applied directly as a simple poultice, or infused into oils and salves, for minor skin complaints and insect bites. It is a beginner-friendly topical because the plant is abundant and easy to identify, but because it grows in exactly the places people spray and pollute, sourcing matters more than technique. Educational, traditional framing only, no claim that it treats or cures anything.
safetyNote: Distinguish from toxic foxglove rosettes by the parallel veins, smooth leaves, and lack of soft hairiness. Avoid plants from sprayed lawns, roadsides, and contaminated soil, this weed concentrates in exactly those places.

sources.title: MSU Plant & Pest Diagnostics, Broadleaf plantain (Plantago major)
NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox, Plantago major

summary: Yarrow (Achillea millefolium) is an aromatic composite of dry meadows and roadsides whose feathery leaves and flat white flower clusters have deep roots in Western herbal tradition (its genus name references Achilles and battlefield wound care). It is a topical entry here and carries lookalike, pregnancy, and drug-interaction cautions.
identification: Grows 1-3 ft tall with soft, finely divided, ferny leaves that are hairy and strongly aromatic (sweet/spicy) when crushed, topped by dense flat-topped clusters of small white (sometimes pink) flower heads (May-September). Its safety-critical danger is that, from a distance, the flat white flower cluster resembles deadly members of the carrot family, poison hemlock (Conium maculatum) and water hemlock (Cicuta species). Tell them apart up close: yarrow's leaves and stems are HAIRY, its foliage is soft and feathery, and the plant smells sweet/spicy; the hemlocks have SMOOTH, hairless stems (often with purple blotches), lacy carrot/parsley-like foliage, true compound umbels, are usually much taller (5-10 ft for poison hemlock), and smell foul/musty or carroty. Queen Anne's lace and wild parsnip are other umbel lookalikes to know. Poison and water hemlock are among the most lethal plants in North America, if the stem is smooth or purple-blotched, or the flower is a true umbel, do NOT treat it as yarrow.
habitat: Found across the contiguous US in sunny, well-drained, disturbed ground: fields, meadows, roadsides, pastures, and lawns. Flowering tops and leaves are gathered through the summer bloom (roughly May-September).
craftUse: In tradition the flowering tops and leaves are used chiefly as an external material, fresh crushed leaf as a field poultice, or dried tops infused into oils, salves, and washes for skin care. It dries and stores well, making it a practical topical to keep on hand. This map keeps it in topical use; note the flags below. Educational, historical framing only, no efficacy, dosing, or 'treats/heals' claims.
safetyNote: Confirm the hairy, aromatic, soft feathery foliage to rule out smooth/purple-blotched-stemmed poison and water hemlock (deadly). Traditionally avoided in pregnancy, may cause skin photosensitivity or allergic reaction in Asteraceae-sensitive people, and may interact with medications.

sources.title: University of Minnesota Extension, Poison hemlock
USDA PLANTS Database, Achillea millefolium (common yarrow)

summary: Chickweed (Stellaria media) is a sprawling, tender cool-season groundcover, a Eurasian annual now common across the contiguous US, long used as a soothing topical material. It is a topical entry here with a lookalike caution.
identification: A low, mat-forming annual with small, teardrop-shaped opposite leaves and tiny white star flowers whose five petals are so deeply cleft they look like ten (blooming in the cool months). The definitive ID tell is a single line of fine hairs running along one side of the round stem, switching sides at each leaf node, visible with a hand lens. The important toxic lookalike is scarlet pimpernel (Lysimachia/Anagallis arvensis), which lacks that single hair line, has a squarish stem, and bears flat orange-red (occasionally blue) flowers, not white notched ones; it contains toxic saponins. Prostrate spurges are another lookalike but exude milky sap when broken (chickweed does not), and mouse-ear chickweeds are hairy all over (harmless but coarser). Check for the one-sided hair line, round stem, and white deeply-notched flowers before use.
habitat: Widespread across the contiguous US in cool, moist, disturbed ground: gardens, lawns, field edges, and shaded beds. It is a cool-season plant, most abundant and tender in late fall through early spring (roughly October-May, dying back in summer heat).
craftUse: Traditionally the tender fresh aerial parts are used topically, bruised as a simple poultice, or infused into oils and salves, as a cooling, soothing skin material. Because the value is in the fresh, succulent tissue, it is a small-batch, gather-when-you-need-it material rather than something to dry. Educational, traditional framing only, no efficacy or 'treats' claims.
safetyNote: Distinguish from toxic scarlet pimpernel (squarish stem, orange-red flat flowers, no single hair line) and from milky-sapped spurges before harvesting.

sources.title: UC IPM, Scarlet pimpernel (Lysimachia/Anagallis arvensis) weed profile
Illinois Wildflowers, Common Chickweed (Stellaria media)

summary: Witch hazel (Hamamelis virginiana) is a native understory shrub/small tree of eastern US woodlands, unusual for blooming in late fall. Its tannin-rich bark, twigs, and leaves are the source of the classic astringent distillate, and on this map it is a topical, external-use entry with an observe-only harvest ethic.
identification: A large multi-stemmed shrub or small tree with smooth gray bark and broad, wavy-toothed (scalloped) leaves that are asymmetric (oblique) at the base. Its most distinctive feature is spidery, ribbon-like yellow flowers that open in fall and early winter (September-December), often while the leaves are still dropping, hence the common name 'winter bloom.' The woody seed capsules explosively eject their seeds. The leaves and hazelnut-like fruit superficially resemble true hazel (Corylus), but the fall bloom time and strappy yellow petals are diagnostic; there are no dangerous toxic lookalikes for the flowering shrub.
habitat: Native across the eastern contiguous US in moist woodlands, forest understories, and stream banks. Bark, twigs, and leaves are the harvested parts, traditionally taken in the fall/dormant season (aligned with the September-December bloom window on this map).
craftUse: The classic preparation is the astringent witch hazel distillate, bark and twigs steamed/distilled, or a leaf-and-bark water infusion or tincture, used externally as a skin toner and wash. Note the honest limit built into this map: taking bark and twigs is destructive to a slow-growing shrub, so it is flagged observe-only unless you already have pruned material with permission. Commercial distillate exists precisely because home woody-part harvest is hard on the plant. External use only; educational and historical framing, no efficacy or 'treats' claims.
safetyNote: External use only. Harvesting bark and twigs damages the shrub, observe rather than cut unless pruning material is already available with permission.

sources.title: USDA PLANTS Database, Hamamelis virginiana (American witch hazel)
NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox, Hamamelis virginiana

summary: Elderberry (Sambucus nigra ssp. canadensis, American elder, still widely listed as S. canadensis) is a native shrub of moist ground whose cooked ripe berries and flowers have a long place in folk tradition. It is an 'immune' entry here and carries explicit toxic-parts and drug-interaction flags, this is one of the map's most safety-sensitive plants.
identification: A large woody shrub 6-20 ft tall with multiple stems, corky gray-brown bark, and opposite pinnately compound leaves of 5-11 toothed leaflets. Flowers form broad, FLAT-topped white clusters (cymes) in early summer; berries hang in those same flat, umbrella-like clusters, ripening dark purple-black (roughly June-September). Three dangerous confusions: (1) water hemlock (Cicuta), one of North America's most lethal plants, a non-woody herb with smooth purple-streaked stems and tripinnate, alternately-arranged leaves; elder is a woody shrub with opposite once-compound leaves. (2) pokeweed (Phytolacca americana), a toxic herbaceous perennial with a fleshy (not woody) stem that dies back each year and berries in drooping elongated RACEMES, not flat cymes. (3) red elderberry (Sambucus racemosa), a related shrub whose RED berries sit in a rounded/dome- or pyramid-shaped cluster (not flat) and whose raw fruit and seeds are toxic, do not use its red fruit. If the plant is not woody, or the berries droop in a long chain, or the ripe berries are red in a mounded cluster, it is not edible black elderberry.
habitat: Native and widespread across the eastern and central contiguous US in moist, sunny places: stream banks, ditches, wet field edges, and roadside thickets. Flowers are gathered in early summer; berries when fully ripe (dark purple-black) in late summer.
craftUse: Only two parts are used in folk practice, and only prepared: the fresh flowers (for infusions/cordials) and the FULLY RIPE dark berries, always COOKED, made into syrups and preserves. Raw berries, unripe fruit, and all leaves, stems, bark, and roots contain cyanogenic glycosides and are unsafe; cooking fully ripe berries reduces these. Framed here as historical/traditional use only, with no efficacy, immune-boosting, or dosing claims.
safetyNote: Leaves, stems, bark, roots, and raw/unripe berries are toxic (cyanogenic); use only cooked, fully ripe dark berries or the flowers. Raw berries can cause nausea/vomiting even when ripe. May interact with medications. Rule out lethal water hemlock, toxic pokeweed, and toxic red elderberry (red fruit).

sources.title: Oregon State University Extension, Play it safe when preserving elderberries (EM 9446)
USDA PLANTS Database, Sambucus canadensis (American black elderberry)

summary: Echinacea (purple coneflower, Echinacea purpurea) is a native prairie perennial widely grown in gardens; its roots, leaves, and flowering tops have a well-known place in North American folk tradition. It is an 'immune' entry here, flagged for drug interactions and autoimmune caution, with root harvest discouraged in favor of cultivated stock.
identification: An upright perennial 2-4 ft tall with coarse, rough-hairy lance-shaped leaves and large daisy-like flower heads: drooping pinkish-purple ray petals around a raised, spiny, coppery-orange central cone (blooming June-September). The raised bristly cone is the giveaway. It has no dangerous toxic lookalikes; be aware that several related native Echinacea species (some rare or state-protected) look similar, which is one reason wild digging is discouraged. Confirm the raised bristly cone and coneflower form.
habitat: Native to prairies, open woods, and glades of the central and southeastern contiguous US, but by far most people will encounter it as a cultivated garden and landscape plant. Aerial parts are gathered in the summer bloom; roots (if used at all) in the dormant season.
craftUse: In folk tradition the roots, leaves, and flowering tops are tinctured or infused as an 'immune' material. This map deliberately steers you to cultivated or planted stock and away from wild roots: root harvest kills the plant and wild populations of some coneflower species are declining, so growing your own is the honest, low-impact source. Educational, historical framing only, no efficacy, prevention, or dosing claims.
safetyNote: May interact with medications; traditionally avoided by people with autoimmune conditions (its reputed immune-stimulating action), by immunocompromised people, and in pregnancy, folk caution, not medical advice. Prefer cultivated plants; avoid digging roots from wild or uncertain-access populations.

sources.title: USDA PLANTS Database, Echinacea purpurea (eastern purple coneflower)
NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox, Echinacea purpurea

summary: Mullein (Verbascum thapsus) is a tall, wooly Eurasian biennial of disturbed ground, naturalized across the contiguous US, whose soft leaves and yellow flowers have a long history of topical and respiratory folk use. It is an 'immune' entry here, flagged external-use.
identification: In its first year a large basal rosette of thick, soft, wooly gray-green leaves; in its second year a tall unbranched flower stalk 3-6+ ft topped with a dense spike of five-petaled yellow flowers (blooming summer). The felty, densely hairy leaves are its signature. The confusion worth flagging is with the toxic first-year rosette of foxglove (Digitalis): foxglove leaves are toothed, darker, and only softly downy (not densely wooly-felted like mullein), and foxglove contains dangerous cardiac glycosides, if the rosette is toothed and merely fuzzy rather than thick-felted, do not treat it as mullein. A practical handling note: the tiny leaf hairs are irritating, so material is usually strained well before use.
habitat: Naturalized across the contiguous US in dry, sunny, disturbed ground: roadsides, old fields, gravel, pastures, and waste places. Leaves and flowers are gathered through the summer (roughly May-September on this map).
craftUse: Traditionally the leaves and the yellow flowers are used: leaves in poultices and steam, and flowers infused into oil as a classic external ear/skin preparation of folk practice. Because the fine leaf hairs can irritate skin and throat, preparations are strained through cloth, a real handling limit. Kept in external framing here. Educational, historical use only; no efficacy or dosing claims.
safetyNote: Confirm the thick, wooly-felted, untoothed leaves to separate first-year mullein from the toxic first-year rosette of foxglove. The fine leaf hairs are irritating, strain infusions well. External framing on this map.

sources.title: USDA PLANTS Database, Verbascum thapsus (common mullein)
USDA Forest Service, Verbascum thapsus, Fire Effects Information System

summary: Wild bergamot / bee balm (Monarda fistulosa) is a native aromatic mint-family perennial of prairies and open woods, important to pollinators and long used in Indigenous and folk North American tradition. It is an 'immune' entry here, flagged for pregnancy and drug-interaction caution.
identification: An herbaceous perennial 2-4 ft tall with the mint-family signatures: square stems and opposite leaves, plus a strongly aromatic (oregano/thyme-like) scent when crushed. Flowers are shaggy, lavender-to-pink tubular clusters in a single head atop each stem (blooming June-September). The square stem, opposite leaves, and strong aromatic scent make it easy to confirm and separate from unrelated plants; note that scarlet bee balm (Monarda didyma) is a red-flowered relative used similarly. Its aroma comes largely from thymol and carvacrol.
habitat: Native and widespread across much of the contiguous US in prairies, meadows, open woods, and roadsides in sun to part shade. Leaves and flowering tops are gathered through the summer bloom (June-September).
craftUse: In tradition the aromatic leaves and flowering tops are dried for teas and steams as an aromatic material; the thymol-rich oil is why it smells strongly of thyme/oregano. It dries and stores well. An honest ethic note built into this map: it is a key pollinator plant, so leave ample flowers for bees and seed set. Educational, historical framing only, no efficacy, respiratory, or dosing claims.
safetyNote: Traditionally avoided in pregnancy and may interact with medications; the concentrated thymol-rich oil can irritate skin and mucous membranes. Leave flowers for pollinators.

sources.title: USDA PLANTS Database, Monarda fistulosa (wild bergamot)
NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox, Monarda fistulosa

summary: Goldenrod (Solidago) is a genus of native late-summer composites with plume-like golden flowers, long used in folk tradition and unfairly blamed for hay fever. It is an 'immune' entry here with important cautions about ragweed confusion, toxic ragwort lookalikes, rare Solidago species, and drug interactions.
identification: Erect perennials, often 2-5 ft, with a main leafy stem, alternate lance-shaped leaves, and dense sprays of tiny bright-yellow flower heads in late summer/fall (August-October). Two things to reconcile: (1) goldenrod is routinely blamed for allergies but is INSECT-pollinated with heavy, sticky pollen that does not blow into your nose, the real culprit, ragweed (Ambrosia), blooms at the same time with inconspicuous greenish flowers and airborne pollen; know ragweed so you neither blame nor gather the wrong plant. (2) Toxic yellow-flowered composites, especially ragworts/groundsels (Senecio/Packera), can loosely resemble goldenrod; these contain hepatotoxic pyrrolizidine alkaloids that can cause serious liver damage. Ragworts tend to have larger, more daisy-like flower heads, fewer heads, and often bloom earlier. Also avoid rare, rayless, or hard-to-ID Solidago species. Confirm the dense plume of tiny yellow heads on a single leafy stem.
habitat: Native across the contiguous US in fields, meadows, roadsides, and open disturbed ground, flowering abundantly in late summer and fall (roughly August-October). Flowering tops and leaves are gathered at bloom.
craftUse: In folk tradition the flowering tops and leaves are dried for teas or tinctured as a material of the herbal repertoire. It is abundant at bloom, so selective picking has little impact, but skip uncertain, rare, or rayless Solidago and any daisy-headed yellow composite you cannot confirm is goldenrod. Educational, historical framing only, no efficacy, urinary, or dosing claims.
safetyNote: Don't confuse with airborne-pollen ragweed or with toxic ragwort/groundsel (Senecio/Packera; pyrrolizidine-alkaloid liver toxins); avoid rare or rayless Solidago. People with aster-family (Asteraceae) pollen allergies should be cautious. May interact with medications.

sources.title: Clemson Cooperative Extension HGIC, How to tell the difference between goldenrod and ragweed
USDA PLANTS Database, Solidago (goldenrod)

summary: Dandelion (Taraxacum officinale) is a ubiquitous Eurasian rosette weed, naturalized everywhere in the contiguous US, whose leaves, flowers, and roots have long folk use as a bitter digestive material. It is a 'digestive' entry here, flagged for drug interactions with root harvest discouraged.
identification: A basal rosette of deeply toothed leaves (the toothed edge gives the name, 'dent de lion') and a single bright-yellow flower head on a smooth, HOLLOW, leafless stalk that exudes milky sap when broken, one head per stalk. Leaves and stems arise directly from the root, with no leafy branching stem. Several yellow composites (cat's ear, hawkbit, sow thistle) resemble it; those are not dangerously toxic, but the reliable dandelion tells are the single flower on a hollow, unbranched, milky stalk and the ground-level rosette. No lethal lookalikes are known, but confirm the hollow single-flower stalk before use.
habitat: Naturalized in essentially all of the contiguous US in lawns, fields, meadows, and disturbed ground. Leaves, flowers, and roots are available across a long season (roughly March-November); leaves are least bitter in early spring.
craftUse: Traditionally the whole plant is used as a bitter material: young leaves and flowers infused, and roots (roasted or decocted) as the classic bitter tonic. This map discourages root digging where you can use the abundant leaves and flowers instead. Because it thrives on lawns and verges, sourcing from unsprayed, uncompacted ground matters. Educational, historical framing only, no efficacy, digestive-cure, or dosing claims.
safetyNote: May interact with medications (including diuretics and, via bitter action, others); traditional caution for those with bile-duct or gallbladder obstruction. People with Asteraceae allergies may react. Avoid treated lawns and roadsides. Prefer leaves/flowers over digging roots.

sources.title: USDA PLANTS Database, Taraxacum officinale (common dandelion)
University of Maryland Extension, Dandelion (Taraxacum officinale)

summary: Garlic mustard (Alliaria petiolata) is an aggressive Eurasian invasive biennial of shaded woodlands across much of the contiguous US, edible and traditionally used as a pungent digestive green. It is a 'digestive' entry here, flagged to confirm ID, to cook (cyanide/oxalates), and to bag pulled plants because it is invasive.
identification: A biennial: first-year plants are ground-level rosettes of kidney-shaped, scalloped-edged leaves; second-year plants send up a 1-4 ft stalk with triangular, coarsely toothed leaves and clusters of small four-petaled white flowers (spring). The single most useful ID test is smell, crushed leaves smell distinctly of GARLIC. First-year rosettes are easily confused with native violets, with young lesser celandine (Ficaria verna, toxic), and with creeping charlie / ground ivy (Glechoma hederacea), but none of those has the garlic odor (and ground ivy has square mint-family stems, lesser celandine has glossy leaves and yellow buttercup flowers). If a crushed leaf does not smell clearly garlicky, do not treat it as garlic mustard.
habitat: A widespread woodland invasive across the eastern, midwestern, and parts of the western contiguous US, thriving in shaded forest floors, trail edges, and disturbed woods. Leaves, flowers, and roots are gathered in spring (roughly March-June); pulling it also serves ecological restoration.
craftUse: In folk use the pungent leaves, flowers, and roots are eaten as a bitter, garlicky green (small amounts of young raw leaves, or cooked). Because the leaves release hydrogen cyanide when cut or chewed and also contain oxalates, cooking or blanching is recommended and it should not be eaten in large raw quantities; cyanide levels are highest in first-year plants. Harvesting it doubles as invasive removal, but bag and dispose of pulled plants so seeds don't spread. Educational, historical framing only.
safetyNote: Confirm the garlic smell to separate from lookalikes (including toxic lesser celandine); cook or blanch (leaves release cyanide when cut and contain oxalates); do not eat large raw amounts, especially of first-year plants. Invasive, bag pulled plants where local rules require.

sources.title: University of Minnesota Extension, Garlic mustard
Rutgers NJAES FS1212, Identification, Control, and Impact of Garlic Mustard (Alliaria petiolata)

summary: Common blue violet (Viola sororia) is a native low-growing perennial of lawns and moist woods across the eastern contiguous US, whose leaves and flowers have gentle folk use as a soothing 'lymphatic' material. It is a lymphatic entry here with a lookalike caution.
identification: A stemless rosette of long-stalked, heart-shaped, scalloped leaves with five-petaled blue-to-purple (sometimes white) flowers, each on its own leafless stalk, the lowest petal often bearded/streaked (blooming March-June). The important caution: BEFORE bloom, the heart-shaped leaves resemble toxic lesser celandine (Ficaria verna) and can also be confused with first-year garlic mustard, but lesser celandine has glossy leaves and bright yellow buttercup flowers, and garlic mustard smells of garlic. Note that a few native YELLOW-flowered violets exist and are called out on this map as uncertain, wait for the classic blue/purple violet flower, which makes ID nearly foolproof. (Foliage of some ornamental/other rosette plants can also be confused before flowering.)
habitat: Native across the eastern and central contiguous US in moist woods, shaded lawns, and yard edges. Leaves and flowers are gathered in the spring flowering season (roughly March-June).
craftUse: In folk tradition the mucilaginous leaves and flowers are infused as teas, syrups, or cooling washes and used as a mild, soothing material. It is a gentle, beginner-friendly plant, best gathered lightly from clean, abundant patches. Note the map's caution to avoid unverified yellow violets and any uncertain rosette. Educational, historical framing only, no efficacy, 'lymphatic-cure,' or dosing claims. (Roots and seeds of violets can be emetic/purgative in quantity, use leaves and flowers only.)
safetyNote: Wait for the distinctive blue/purple violet flower to rule out toxic lesser celandine and other heart-leaved lookalikes; avoid yellow violets and uncertain IDs. Use leaves and flowers only, violet roots and seeds can cause vomiting in quantity.

sources.title: USDA PLANTS Database, Viola sororia (common blue violet)
University of Minnesota Extension, Wild violet

summary: Cleavers (Galium aparine) is a sprawling, clinging spring annual widespread across the contiguous US, long used fresh as a cooling folk 'lymphatic' material. It is a lymphatic entry here, flagged for drug interactions, and best gathered before it turns tough and seedy.
identification: A weak, straggling annual with square stems and narrow leaves borne in whorls of 6-8 at each node; the whole plant is covered in tiny hooked hairs that make it cling to clothing and skin ('sticky willy,' 'velcro plant'). Tiny white four-petaled flowers appear in spring, followed by round bur-like seeds that also stick. The clinging texture plus whorled leaves is diagnostic. Other bedstraws (Galium) are similar and generally harmless (one, Galium odoratum / sweet woodruff, is used only in small amounts as it can be harmful in large doses); there are no dangerous toxic lookalikes, but confirm the clinging hooked hairs and whorled leaves.
habitat: Widespread across the contiguous US in moist, shaded, disturbed ground: hedgerows, garden edges, stream banks, and woodland margins. It is a spring plant, gathered while still tender (roughly March-June) before it becomes tough and sets its sticky seeds.
craftUse: Traditionally the fresh, tender aerial parts (before seed) are used as a cooling material, juiced fresh, infused as a cold-water infusion, or used in washes. The value is in the fresh spring growth, so it is a small-batch, gather-in-season material rather than something to dry. Educational, historical framing only, no efficacy, 'lymphatic-cure,' or dosing claims.
safetyNote: May interact with medications, including diuretics. Harvest the tender spring growth before it becomes tough and seedy. The hooked hairs can mildly irritate sensitive skin.

sources.title: NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox, Galium aparine (catchweed bedstraw / cleavers)
University of Wisconsin-Madison Horticulture, Catchweed bedstraw (Galium aparine)

summary: Quartz is crystalline silica (SiO2), one of the most abundant minerals in the crust and a Mohs-7 workhorse of lapidary. On this map it stands for clear rock crystal and its colored varieties (amethyst, smoky, citrine) worked into cabochons, faceted stones, beads, and wire-wrapped points.
identification: Look for six-sided (hexagonal) prisms with a glassy luster, often ending in a pointed pyramid. Key field tests: quartz is hard (Mohs 7), scratches glass and a steel knife blade, and has no cleavage, breaking instead with curved, shell-like conchoidal fractures. Its most common lookalike is calcite, which is much softer (Mohs 3), breaks along flat cleavage planes into rhombs, and fizzes in dilute acid, quartz does not fizz. Clear quartz is also confused with man-made glass, but glass is softer (~5.5), so quartz scratches glass, not the reverse. No toxic lookalikes.
