The real hazard is not visual: dry cutting, grinding, or sanding any quartz (or any silica rock) produces respirable crystalline silica, a serious and cumulative lung hazard.
habitat: Quartz occurs nationwide in veins, pegmatites, and pockets. The most famous US crystal district is the Ouachita Mountains around Mount Ida and Montgomery County, Arkansas, where clear crystal clusters are dug. It is available year-round.
craftUse: Rock crystal is faceted, cut into cabochons, carved, and drilled for beads; single points are a beginner favorite for wire-wrapped pendants (see the wire-wrap recipe). Its hardness means it holds a durable polish but also demands diamond or silicon-carbide abrasives and patience, it is slow to work by hand. Note that around Mount Ida most named 'mines' are commercial pay-to-dig or claimed ground, not open free collecting (one exception is the free Crystal Vista site in the Ouachita National Forest); occurrence on the map is not permission.
safetyNote: Cut, grind, and sand wet, dry work on quartz releases respirable crystalline silica dust, which causes silicosis with repeated exposure; a nuisance dust mask is not enough for sustained dry work.

sources.title: How to Identify Quartz Crystals, Rock & Gem Magazine
Calcite vs. Quartz: How To Tell Them Apart, Rock Chasing
Rockhounding Arkansas: Quartz as gems, Arkansas Geological Survey

summary: Novaculite is a dense, microcrystalline quartz rock, the stone behind the classic 'Arkansas' whetstone. On this map it represents natural sharpening stones (and, historically, knapping toolstone), graded from coarse to ultra-fine by density.
identification: A fine-grained, very hard (Mohs ~7) siliceous rock, typically white, cream, gray, or black, with a smooth, almost porcelain-like fracture. It looks like chert or flint and is chemically the same (silica); the distinction is textural, novaculite is the exceptionally even, fine-grained variety prized for whetstones. There is no toxic lookalike, but do not confuse graded commercial stones with raw outcrop novaculite: the Ouachita/Novaculite Uplift outcrops include ancient quarry sites protected as archaeological resources on public land. Dry lapping or truing releases respirable crystalline silica.
habitat: The classic source is the Arkansas Novaculite formation of the Ouachita Mountains (Benton–Broken Bow Uplift) in western Arkansas and adjacent Oklahoma. Available year-round; buy graded stones rather than collecting.
craftUse: Graded by density: Washita (coarsest, roughly 400–600 grit) through Soft, Hard, and Black/Translucent Arkansas (ultra-fine finishing stones). Grit equivalents depend on the scale, the finest translucent stones rate around 1200+ on the US abrasives scale and are sometimes cited near 4000 on the Japanese waterstone scale. Novaculite refines an edge to a polish rather than removing much metal, so it follows a coarser stone in the progression (see the novaculite whetstone recipe). Run it wet with oil or water and re-true the face when it dishes; a single stone can last decades. Also lapped into fine instrument-finishing and pen-tip stones.
safetyNote: Never dry-hone, lap, or true, keep the stone flooded with oil or water; dry work on this silica rock raises respirable crystalline silica dust.

sources.title: Novaculite (Silica Stone), Arkansas Geological Survey
Arkansas Stone Grades 101, Dan's Whetstone
Novaculite, Wikipedia

summary: This entry is the general silica (SiO2) toolstone category, chert and flint, the knappable cousins of novaculite. It is the raw material of flaked stone tools and of flint-and-steel firemaking, and on this map it also holds unlabeled novaculite occurrences.
identification: Chert and flint are hard (Mohs ~7), dense, fine-grained silica rocks that break with sharp conchoidal fractures, the property that makes them knappable. Colors range from tan and gray to near-black (flint) and red (jasper). They scratch glass and a steel blade and do not fizz in acid, unlike the softer carbonate rocks (limestone, chalk) they sometimes weather out of. There is no toxic lookalike, but there is a real legal hazard: chert/flint outcrops and float are frequently archaeological toolstone sources, and on federal and tribal lands collecting artifacts (worked flakes, points) is prohibited under ARPA. On private land, state antiquities and trespass laws apply instead. This map shows raw geology only, not permission to collect artifacts anywhere.
habitat: Chert and flint occur across the US as nodules and beds in limestone and as stream gravels. Available year-round.
craftUse: Knappers percussion- and pressure-flake chert and flint into blades, scrapers, and points; the same hard, sharp edge makes it a good striker for flint-and-steel firemaking, the spark is actually a shaving of hot steel the stone shears off the striker, not the stone itself (see the flint-and-steel recipe). A good knapped edge is razor-sharp and brittle. Grinding or knapping produces silica dust and sharp shatter, eye protection and wet grinding are essential. Do not collect worked stone from archaeological sites.
safetyNote: Freshly flaked silica is extremely sharp and grinding it raises respirable crystalline silica dust; wear eye protection and cut/grind wet.

sources.title: Chalcedony, USGS Gemstones (Minerals Information)
Novaculite (Silica Stone), Arkansas Geological Survey
Archaeological Resources Protection Act, National Park Service

summary: Soapstone (steatite) is a soft, talc-rich metamorphic rock that carves with hand tools and shrugs off heat, historically made into bowls, pipes, molds, and cookware. On this map it is a carving and low-fire pottery material, with an important asbestos caveat.
identification: Recognize soapstone by its soapy, greasy feel, gray-green to bluish color, and extreme softness, pure grades can be scratched with a fingernail (talc is Mohs 1). Carving grades run high in talc (softest); harder steatite holds finer detail. The critical hazard is invisible: soapstone commonly contains amphibole minerals (tremolite, anthophyllite) and serpentine chrysotile that can occur as asbestos, by some estimates a large fraction of soapstone carries at least some asbestos, and asbestos fibers cannot be seen with the naked eye. Content varies sharply by source (some Vermont soapstone is typically asbestos-free; some other deposits are not), and stone sold as 'asbestos-free' has occasionally still tested positive. There is no reliable visual or field test; only lab analysis is definitive. Eastern steatite outcrops are also frequently prehistoric quarry sites protected on public land (ARPA).
habitat: Soapstone belts run through the Appalachians (Virginia, North Carolina, Georgia), New England, and the Pacific Northwest. Available year-round.
craftUse: Its softness makes it the friendliest carving stone for beginners, knives, rasps, and rifflers cut it easily, and it takes a soft sheen. Because it is heat-stable it was used for cookware, griddles, and pipe bowls; powdered as talc it is also a low-fire pottery body and a matte-glaze flux (see the talc-body and talc-matte-glaze recipes). The limit is durability: it is soft, scratches, and marks easily.
safetyNote: Treat unknown soapstone as if it contains asbestos: cut, grind, and sand wet with proper respiratory protection, or have the stone lab-tested first. Its dust carries both possible asbestos and silicate/talc lung hazards; there is no safe way to judge fiber content by eye.

sources.title: Soapstone, Wikipedia
Sculpture safety (soapstone/asbestos), Princeton EHS
Asbestos test: talcum, steatite, CRB GmbH

summary: Clay is the foundation of pottery, fine-grained sheet-silicate minerals (kaolinite group and others) that become plastic when wet, hold a shape, and harden permanently when fired. This map covers regional wild clays for hand-building, throwing, and pipe-making, plus their use as slips, glazes, and pigment.
identification: Wild clay is plastic when wet, forms a ribbon that bends without cracking when rolled, and feels smooth rather than gritty (sand and silt do not become plastic). Iron-bearing clays fire buff to red-brown; kaolins fire white. There are no toxic lookalikes for the raw material, but two cautions matter: never assume a clay is fire-safe or food-safe without testing (impurities, lime pops, glaze fit), and clay bodies contain free crystalline silica. A field test, wedge a small ball, dry it, and fire it, tells you whether a deposit is usable (see the wild-clay field-test recipe).
habitat: Clay deposits occur nationwide in river floodplains, road cuts, stream banks, and weathered bedrock. Regional types include plastic ball clay for throwing, white kaolin for porcelain, iron stoneware/earthenware, and high-alumina fire clay. Available year-round; easiest to dig when moist.
craftUse: Hand-built and wheel-thrown pottery, tile, tobacco-pipe clay, terra sigillata burnishing slips, raw slip decoration, and wood-ash and celadon glazes (see the many clay recipes). Iron-rich clay is also the source of the oldest human pigment, ochre (see the ochre recipe). Wild clay usually needs processing: slaking, sieving, and often tempering with grog or sand to survive firing.
safetyNote: Process and clean up clay wet, dry clay dust and glaze materials contain respirable crystalline silica, the classic potter's-lung hazard; sweep with water, not a broom, and test unknown wild clays before firing food surfaces.

sources.title: Silica (Crystalline) Hazards, CDC/NIOSH
Clays Statistics and Information, USGS Mineral Resources
Kaolin and ball clay, USGS Minerals Information

summary: Slate is a fine-grained metamorphic rock that splits cleanly into flat sheets along its foliation. That natural cleavage makes it the material for engraving, inlay, coasters, and flagstone craft on this map.
identification: Recognize slate by its dull to slightly satiny sheen, fine grain, and, the defining trait, 'slaty cleavage' that lets it split into thin, flat, sound plates. Colors run gray, black, green, purple, and red depending on the belt. It is harder and rings more than shale (its unmetamorphosed parent, which crumbles), and unlike schist it shows no mica flakes large enough to see and no wavy, coarse foliation. No toxic lookalikes; the main hazard is silica dust from dry cutting or engraving, since slate contains quartz and other silicates.
habitat: US slate belts include the Slate Valley (Vermont/New York), the Pennsylvania Slate Belt, Buckingham (Virginia), and Monson (Maine). These are mostly private quarry operations. Available year-round.
craftUse: Slate's flat sheets are engraved, sandblasted, or hand-incised for lettering and signage, cut into coasters and tiles, and used as inlay and flagstone (see the slate-engraving recipe). It is moderately soft and works with carbide tools, but it is brittle and prone to delaminating along its cleavage, so pieces can flake unexpectedly. Because deposits are private, prefer waste-pile slate obtained with the operator's permission.
safetyNote: Cut and engrave wet or with dust extraction and eye protection, slate dust contains respirable crystalline silica and the rock chips sharply.

sources.title: Slate, Wikipedia
Dimension Stone (incl. slate), USGS Minerals Information
Slate Valley Museum, Slate industry history

summary: This entry centers on the one US site where the public can legally dig and keep gem-quality diamonds: Crater of Diamonds State Park in Arkansas. It stands for faceting and setting-grade gem rough recovered by hand from an eroded volcanic pipe.
identification: Rough diamonds at the Crater are small, rounded, greasy or metallic-looking crystals in white, brown, and yellow, easy to overlook among ordinary gravel. Their oily surface sheds water and dust and stays relatively clean and shiny when wet, while common lookalikes (clear quartz, calcite, barite, jasper) are duller or opaque when wet. Do NOT rely on scratch or hardness tests to identify a suspected diamond: genuine diamonds are brittle and a careless scratch test can chip or shatter a real stone, and scratching softer minerals is ambiguous. Instead, use the wet/greasy-luster test and take every candidate to the park's Diamond Discovery Center, where staff identify and certify finds for free. No toxic hazards.
habitat: The Prairie Creek diatreme (an Early Cretaceous lamproite volcanic pipe) near Murfreesboro, Pike County, Arkansas, a 37-acre plowed search field. Diamonds surface continually as the ground erodes and is turned; open year-round, with more finds often reported after rain.
craftUse: For a per-day fee the park runs a 'finders keepers' policy, any diamond or other gem you unearth is yours to cut, set, and keep. Recovered rough is faceted or set as-found; smaller stones and the accompanying amethyst, jasper, and agate suit cabochon and bead work. This is the rare public gemstone site; nearly everywhere else, gem rough is on claimed or private ground and occurrence on the map is not collecting permission.
safetyNote: No chemical or dust hazard from the rough itself; the main caution is to have finds identified at the park's Diamond Discovery Center rather than attempting damaging scratch tests, which can chip a genuine (brittle) diamond.

sources.title: Crater of Diamonds State Park, Arkansas State Parks
Geology of Crater of Diamonds State Park, Arkansas Geological Survey
Crater of Diamonds State Park, Wikipedia

summary: Alabaster (in the US, almost always the gypsum variety, CaSO4·2H2O) is a soft, fine-grained, translucent stone that carves with hand tools and takes a soft glow. On this map it is a sculpture and inlay material, and its close mineral relative is the source of plaster.
identification: Massive gypsum alabaster is very soft (Mohs ~1.5–2), a fingernail scratches it, fine-grained, and translucent, in white, cream, honey, and pink. Its important lookalike is calcite (marble/onyx) alabaster: they look alike but calcite is harder (Mohs 3, fingernail won't scratch it) and effervesces in dilute hydrochloric acid, while gypsum does not fizz. This matters for carving because gypsum is softer and, critically, slightly water-soluble. Both types are slightly water-soluble and neither survives outdoors, gypsum least of all. No toxic lookalikes; the hazard is dust.
habitat: Massive gypsum occurs in the evaporite districts of Colorado, Oklahoma, New Mexico, Utah, Wyoming, and Texas. Collect loose float and quarry-waste blocks rather than fresh outcrop; bulk gypsum is a salable federal mineral, so keep it to casual specimen amounts. Available year-round.
craftUse: Alabaster's softness makes it a premier hand-carving stone for sculpture, lamps, boxes, and translucent inlay, it cuts with rasps and knives and polishes to a soft translucency (see the alabaster-carving recipe). Its limits are hardness and water: it dents, scratches, and dissolves slowly in water, so it is strictly indoor work and cannot be left wet. Fired and ground, gypsum becomes plaster of Paris for molds and casting bats (see the plaster-molds recipe).
safetyNote: Work wet or masked, fine gypsum dust irritates the eyes and airways (it is a nuisance/irritant dust, not crystalline silica); keep finished pieces indoors and dry, since alabaster is water-soluble.

sources.title: Alabaster, Wikipedia
Gypsum, Common Minerals, University of Minnesota
Gypsum Statistics and Information, USGS

summary: Pipestone (catlinite) is a soft red claystone that Plains nations have quarried for ceremonial pipes for as long as three millennia. It is on this map as an educational and culturally restricted entry, mapped so you know to protect it, not to take it.
identification: Catlinite is a deep brick-red to pale-orange, fine-grained, soft claystone (carvable with hand tools) that occurs as thin beds sandwiched within the very hard, roughly 1.7–1.8-billion-year-old Sioux Quartzite. It contains little to no quartz, which is why it carves so cleanly. It can be confused with other soft red argillites and 'pipestones' sold commercially; true Pipestone Monument catlinite comes only from the reserved Minnesota quarries. There is no toxic lookalike, but there is a cultural and legal boundary that overrides any craft interest.
habitat: The Upper Midwest, Minnesota (Pipestone National Monument), South Dakota, and Wisconsin, within the Sioux Quartzite formation. The monument quarries are worked with hand tools only.
craftUse: Do not carve it. At Pipestone National Monument, quarrying is reserved by federal law for enrolled members of federally recognized tribes holding a permit, using traditional hand tools only; the Park Service consults with roughly two dozen affiliated tribal nations. Traditionally it is carved into ceremonial pipe bowls (chanunpas), effigies, and beads, by Native carvers, within that tradition. To engage respectfully: visit the monument, learn from tribal-authored sources, and buy finished work directly from authorized Native carvers. Occurrence on the map is emphatically not permission.
safetyNote: Culturally restricted, pipestone is sacred and legally reserved for tribal quarrying; do not source, quarry, or carve it. (Its dust would carry the usual clay/silicate lung hazards, but the binding rule here is the cultural and legal restriction, not a dust caveat.)

sources.title: Pipestone National Monument, National Park Service
Geologic Formations, Pipestone National Monument (NPS)
Catlinite, Wikipedia

summary: Serpentine (serpentinite) is a green, waxy carving and lapidary stone, California's state rock. It works easily and polishes well, but it carries a genuine asbestos hazard that governs how it must be handled.
identification: Serpentinite is fine-grained and compact, green to blue-green or black, often mottled or veined, with a shiny, waxy luster and a slightly soapy feel; it is moderately soft (Mohs ~3–5). Its defining hazard is chrysotile, the fibrous, silky white or greenish veinlets threading many serpentine bodies are the most common form of asbestos. Not every serpentinite contains it, and you cannot reliably judge fiber content by eye. Serpentine is sometimes confused with jade or soapstone; because any of these may carry asbestos, conservative wet handling is the safe default regardless of which it is.
habitat: The California Coast Ranges, Klamath Mountains, and Sierra Nevada foothills, plus the Vermont/Appalachian ultramafic belt. Gather float and loose blocks; avoid disturbing serpentine barrens, which host rare endemic plants. Available year-round.
craftUse: Serpentine carves and cabs readily into figurines, bowls, beads, and cabochons and takes a good polish, making it a popular soft-stone carving material. The overriding limit is health: because it may contain naturally occurring asbestos, all cutting, grinding, and sanding must be done wet with HEPA/P100 respiratory protection, never dry, and never in a shared living space where fibers can settle.
safetyNote: May contain naturally occurring asbestos (chrysotile), always cut, grind, and sand wet with proper (HEPA/P100) respiratory protection, never dry and never indoors around others; asbestos fibers released by dry working cause lung disease and cannot be judged safe by appearance.

sources.title: Serpentinite: California's State Rock, California Geological Survey
Naturally Occurring Asbestos in California, US EPA Region 9
CGS Note 57, Serpentinite and Serpentine in California

summary: Carving-grade limestone is a soft, even-textured calcium-carbonate 'freestone' that cuts cleanly in any direction, the sculptor's and letter-cutter's stone. The classic is Salem (Indiana) limestone. It is also the raw material of the lime cycle and a glaze flux.
identification: Carving limestone is a homogeneous, fine- to medium-grained buff-to-gray stone, soft enough to scratch with a knife (calcite is Mohs 3) and, the diagnostic test, it fizzes in dilute hydrochloric acid. Salem limestone is oolitic, made of tiny rounded grains plus microscopic fossils, and is prized because it has no strong bedding direction ('freestone'). Distinguish carving freestone from ordinary aggregate/cement limestone, which is often shattered or impure; most limestone on geological databases is not carving grade. No toxic lookalikes. Note that some limestones contain chert/silica nodules, so dust from a given stone is not always purely carbonate.
habitat: The signature source is the Salem Limestone belt of south-central Indiana (Owen, Monroe, and Lawrence Counties, between Bloomington and Bedford); other freestones come from Texas, Kansas, and Minnesota. Available year-round; source mill off-cuts and reject blocks.
craftUse: Limestone is the standard stone for architectural sculpture, relief, and lettering, soft enough for hand chisels yet fine enough to hold crisp detail (Salem stone faces the Empire State Building and many capitols). Its limits: it is porous, weathers over decades, and dust must be controlled. Burned and slaked, the same stone becomes quicklime for lime plaster, limewash, and mortar (see the lime-cycle recipe); ground fine, it is a glaze flux/whiting (see the limestone-flux recipes). Note that quicklime (calcium oxide) is caustic and reacts violently with water, handle with skin and eye protection.
safetyNote: Wear a dust mask and eye protection, carving raises fine calcium-carbonate dust and flying chips (mostly a nuisance/irritant dust, though some limestones carry chert/silica, so treat visibly siliceous stone as a silica risk).

sources.title: Salem Limestone, Indiana Geological & Water Survey
Salem Limestone, Wikipedia
Dimension Stone Statistics and Information, USGS

summary: Marble is metamorphosed limestone, recrystallized calcite, the classic statuary stone. Finer and more uniform than limestone, it holds crisp detail and takes a high polish, and its offcuts and dust also serve gesso and plaster work.
identification: Marble is a crystalline calcite (or dolomite) rock with a sugary, sparkling grain, usually white but often veined or colored by impurities; it is soft (calcite Mohs 3, scratched by a knife) and fizzes in dilute hydrochloric acid, the test that separates true marble from harder, silica-based 'marbles' sold in the trade (quartzite, serpentine 'verde' marbles) that do not fizz and dull tools. Unlike limestone it is fully recrystallized, so it has no visible fossils and a more even, translucent surface. No toxic lookalikes, but note that some 'marbles' sold commercially are actually serpentine, which can carry asbestos, verify a green 'marble' before dry-working it.
habitat: US statuary and craft marble comes from Danby/Proctor (Vermont), Tate (Georgia), Yule (Colorado), the Tennessee marble district, and Sylacauga (Alabama). Statuary grades are near-exhausted or tightly held; source quarry scrap and reject blocks by purchase or permission. Available year-round.
craftUse: Marble is the premier stone for figurative sculpture, relief, and inlay, cut with chisels, rasps, and rifflers and finished to a polish. It is harder and less forgiving than limestone or alabaster, and calcite's cleavage means it can bruise or fracture along crystal boundaries under a heavy blow. True calcite marble carries no crystalline-silica hazard, but chips fly and fine dust is an irritant. Ground marble/chalk is also a gesso and clay-plaster filler (see the chalk-gesso recipe); marble also takes fine incised lettering (see the marble-lettering recipe).
safetyNote: Wear eye protection and a dust mask, carving true (calcite) marble throws sharp chips and raises fine calcium-carbonate dust (an irritant dust, not crystalline silica); if a green 'verde marble' is actually serpentine, treat it as a possible asbestos risk and work it wet.

sources.title: Marble, Wikipedia
Dimension Stone Statistics and Information, USGS
Colorado Yule Marble, Colorado Geological Survey

summary: Obsidian is natural volcanic glass, silica-rich lava cooled too fast to crystallize. Its glassy conchoidal fracture yields edges sharper than steel, making it the premier knapping toolstone of the American West, with some lapidary use.
identification: Obsidian is a homogeneous, glassy black (sometimes brown, mahogany, snowflake, or rainbow-sheen) volcanic glass with a bright vitreous luster and sharp, curved conchoidal fractures, no crystals, no grain. It scratches glass (Mohs ~5–6) and shows warm tones in thin edges held to light. Its lookalikes are man-made glass/slag and dark chert; obsidian's uniform glassiness and lack of trapped bubbles or banding distinguish it from slag, and its glassy fracture distinguishes it from grainy chert. No toxic lookalike, but freshly knapped edges are a genuine laceration hazard.
habitat: Western volcanic terrains, Oregon (Glass Buttes), California, Idaho, New Mexico, Nevada, Arizona, Utah, and Wyoming. Surface-collect loose float only at designated open areas (e.g., the BLM Glass Buttes free-use area), and confirm current local rules first. Available year-round.
craftUse: Obsidian is the knapper's favorite: it pressure-flakes into extraordinarily sharp points and blades and works faster than any stone toolstone. Some grades cab and polish for lapidary. Its edges can be sharper than a scalpel, respect them. Two hard limits: obsidian is heavily studied archaeological toolstone, so on federal and tribal lands worked pieces are ARPA-protected artifacts, not free finds (state law governs on private land); and collecting is prohibited in NPS units such as Yellowstone (Obsidian Cliff).
safetyNote: Freshly flaked obsidian is extremely sharp, treat every edge as a scalpel and expect lacerations; grinding it wet also controls the silica/glass dust, so cut and grind wet with eye protection.

sources.title: Obsidian: The MVP of Yellowstone's Stones, National Park Service
Obsidian, Volcano World, Oregon State University
Can I Keep This? (rock collecting rules), Bureau of Land Management

summary: Agate, banded chalcedony, a cryptocrystalline form of quartz, is the workhorse lapidary stone, together with its cousins chalcedony, carnelian, and jasper. It tumbles, cabs, and polishes beautifully, and is abundant enough for beginners.
identification: Agate is translucent chalcedony with distinct concentric or fortification banding and a waxy-to-glassy luster; it is hard (Mohs 7), scratches glass, and chips in conchoidal, half-moon divots. It glistens when wet, the classic field trick for spotting it in gravel. Distinguish it from jasper (opaque, iron-stained, unbanded) and from ordinary quartz pebbles (crystalline, not translucent-banded). There are no toxic lookalikes; the hazard is crystalline-silica dust when cutting.
habitat: Across the US: Lake Superior agate (Minnesota, Wisconsin, Michigan), Montana/moss agate, and jasper and agate throughout Oregon, Wyoming, Texas, and Idaho. Surface-collect float and gravel-bar material; take reasonable personal amounts, refill holes, and stay off active mining claims. Available year-round; best hunting after rain or in wave-washed gravels.
craftUse: Agate is the entry point to lapidary, durable enough to survive tumbling and cabbing, and forgiving enough for a first cabochon, plus beads, inlay, and knife scales (see the tumbling and cabochon recipes). Its hardness means it holds a mirror polish but requires diamond/silicon-carbide abrasives and patience. It is chemically the same silica as quartz and chert, so all wet-cutting dust rules apply.
safetyNote: Cut, grind, and sand wet, agate is crystalline silica, and dry lapidary work releases respirable silica dust that causes silicosis.

sources.title: Lake Superior Agate, Wikipedia
Chalcedony, USGS Gemstones (Minerals Information)
Agates, Banded and Beautiful, Michigan Rockhounds

summary: Petrified wood is fossil wood whose cell structure has been replaced by silica (chalcedony/agate), so it cuts and polishes like agate while preserving grain, rings, and bark. On this map it is a lapidary material with strict, location-specific collecting limits.
identification: Petrified wood looks like wood, visible grain, growth rings, sometimes bark and knots, but is stone-hard (Mohs ~7), heavy, and cold, and it takes a glassy polish; colors come from mineral impurities (red/yellow iron, black manganese, etc.). Distinguish it from ordinary banded agate by its woody structure, and from actual weathered wood by its weight and hardness (it scratches glass). No toxic lookalikes; the practical hazards are crystalline-silica dust and, above all, where you collect.
habitat: The Southwest and Interior West, Arizona, Oregon, Washington, Wyoming, Utah, Colorado, Texas, and Montana, in eroding sedimentary badlands and gravels. Available year-round.
craftUse: Because it is silicified, petrified wood cabs, slabs, and tumbles exactly like agate into cabochons, bookends, beads, and display slabs (see the petrified-wood lapidary recipe), with the bonus of a wood-grain pattern. The binding constraint is the law of the land it comes from: on most BLM land, free casual collection is limited to 25 pounds per day plus one piece, up to 250 pounds per year, for personal (non-commercial) use, with some areas more restrictive, so check the local office. It is prohibited in NPS units; removing petrified wood from Petrified Forest National Park is a federal offense with fines.
safetyNote: Cut, grind, and sand wet, petrified wood is crystalline silica and its dust causes silicosis; and never collect in a National Park (a federal offense) or exceed BLM personal-use limits.

sources.title: Laws & Policies, Petrified Forest National Park (NPS)
